topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Networking** is the specialized branch of data communication engineering that deals with the design, deployment, and management of communication networks used to interconnect sensors, controllers, actuators, drives, HMI panels, and supervisory computers within an industrial automation environment such as a manufacturing plant, process industry, power station, or discrete assembly line.

The formal KTU 2024 definition emphasizes that industrial networks are **not** simply "office LANs in a factory." They are engineered communication systems whose primary purpose is to exchange **time-critical, deterministic control and monitoring data** between field devices (Level 0/1) and control/SCADA systems (Level 2/3) of the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights — KTU PERUT741 Module 1**
> 1. Definition and scope of Industrial Networking
> 2. Office Network vs. Industrial Network — characteristic differences
> 3. Evolution of Industrial Communication Systems
> 4. Classification of Industrial Networks (Sensor, Fieldbus, Control, Enterprise)
> 5. Role of the OSI 7-Layer Model in Industrial Communication
> 6. Key requirements: Real-time, Determinism, Reliability, Safety, Security

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Plain English Intuition"

Imagine a **hospital's emergency ward**:
- The **patients** are the field devices (sensors, motors, valves).
- The **doctors and nurses** are the PLCs and controllers.
- The **intercom system** inside the ward is the **industrial network**.
- Every word spoken on the intercom must reach the right person **within a guaranteed time**, or the patient could be harmed. Casual chatting is forbidden; the message must be **short, exact, and on time**.

A factory floor behaves exactly the same way. An industrial network is essentially the **"intercom of a factory"** — every sensor reading and every actuator command must be delivered with **bounded delay, zero ambiguity, and extreme reliability**, often under electrical noise, vibration, temperature extremes, and electromagnetic interference.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Anchor:** In a bottling plant, the sensor detecting a missing cap must tell the PLC to reject the bottle **within 10–50 milliseconds**. If the network is non-deterministic, defective bottles will pass downstream — this is why **determinism** is the *single most important* property of an industrial network.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | Standard / Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum End-to-End Latency (Fieldbus) | **1 ms – 100 ms** | Time from sensor trigger to actuator action |
| MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | **> 100,000 hours** | Industrial switches must run for > 11 years continuously |
| Operating Temperature | **−40 °C to +75 °C** | Industrial-grade hardware tolerance |
| EMC Compliance | **IEC 61000-6-2 / IEC 61850-3** | Immunity to surge, ESD, RF interference |
| Determinism Guarantee | **Bounded jitter (worst-case delay known)** | Predictable response regardless of load |
| Data Rate (typical Fieldbus) | **31.25 kbps – 100 Mbps** | Depends on hierarchy level (sensor vs. plant) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Industrial Networking? — The Operational Rationale

A modern automation pyramid (Purdue Model) consists of physically distributed devices that **must** talk to each other. The motivations for industrial networking are:

1. **Distributed Control** — Replacing point-to-point wiring between sensors and a central controller with a **single shared communication cable**, drastically reducing copper cost and commissioning time.
2. **Centralized Visibility (SCADA / HMI)** — Supervisors must see real-time values of temperature, pressure, flow, and speed from across the plant.
3. **Diagnostics & Predictive Maintenance** — Smart sensors expose health information (drift, calibration date, error codes) over the network.
4. **Interoperability** — Devices from multiple vendors (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, ABB, Schneider) must communicate using open standards.
5. **Scalability** — Adding a new motor or valve should require only one cable drop, not a new analog wire back to the PLC.
6. **Safety Integration** — Functional Safety (SIL-rated) communication channels (e.g., PROFIsafe, CIP Safety) ride on the same network.

## 2.2 Office Network vs. Industrial Network — KTU Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question** topic. Memorize the contrasting axis.

| Parameter | Office / IT Network | Industrial Network |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Information transfer, web, email | **Real-time control & monitoring** |
| Latency Tolerance | **Seconds** (email) | **Milliseconds or microseconds** |
| Determinism | Best-effort (TCP/IP) | **Hard real-time, bounded jitter** |
| Environment | Clean, air-conditioned, controlled | **Harsh** — dust, moisture, EMI, vibration, temperature |
| Hardware | Commercial-grade | **Industrial-grade** (ruggedized, conformal coated) |
| Topology | Mostly Star, Ethernet switched | **Bus, Ring, Star, Line, Tree** |
| Cable | Cat-5/6 UTP, fibre | **Shielded twisted pair, fibre, PROFIBUS cable** |
| Protocols | TCP/IP, HTTP, SMTP | **Modbus, PROFIBUS, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, CAN, DeviceNet, Foundation Fieldbus** |
| Power | 230 V AC, stable | Often **24 V DC**, battery-backed, redundant |
| Uptime | 99.9% ("three nines") | **99.99% – 99.999%** ("four nines / five nines") |
| Data Priority | All packets equal | **Cyclic / process data > acyclic / diagnostic** |
| Typical Lifetime | 3–5 years refresh | **15–20 years** field installation |
| OSI Layers Used | All 7 layers | Often **layers 1, 2, 7** (Application-only) |

## 2.3 Evolution of Industrial Communication — Timeline of KTU Relevance

| Generation | Era | Dominant Technology | Communication Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1G — Pneumatic / Hydraulic** | Pre-1970 | Air pressure (3–15 psi) | One dedicated tube per signal |
| **2G — Analog 4–20 mA** | 1970–1985 | Current loop | One twisted pair per variable |
| **3G — Smart + HART** | 1985–1995 | 4–20 mA + digital overlay | **Hybrid analog + digital** |
| **4G — Fieldbus** | 1995–2010 | PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet | **Digital, multidrop, deterministic** |
| **5G — Industrial Ethernet** | 2010–today | PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, TSN | **Gigabit, real-time Ethernet** |
| **6G — TSN + IIoT** | 2020+ | Time-Sensitive Networking, OPC UA, 5G URLLC | **Cloud-integrated, wireless, time-critical** |

## 2.4 Classification of Industrial Networks

Industrial networks are classified on **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Hierarchy Level (ISA-95 / Purdue Model)
- **Level 0 — Sensor / Actuator Network** (IO-Link, AS-Interface)
- **Level 1 — Control / Fieldbus Network** (PROFIBUS, Modbus RTU, DeviceNet)
- **Level 2 — Control LAN** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP)
- **Level 3 — Plant / Operations LAN** (OPC UA, MQTT)
- **Level 4 — Enterprise Network** (Classical TCP/IP, ERP integration)

### B. By Topology
- **Bus** (PROFIBUS, CAN) — single trunk with stubs
- **Ring** (DLR — Device Level Ring) — redundant
- **Star** (Ethernet switched) — central switch
- **Line / Daisy-chain** (IO-Link, single-pair Ethernet)
- **Tree** (Foundation Fieldbus H1/H2)

### C. By Real-Time Class
- **Class 1 — Non real-time** (e.g., FTP, email on plant network)
- **Class 2 — Soft real-time** (e.g., HMI refresh, 100 ms class)
- **Class 3 — Hard real-time** (e.g., motion control, < 1 ms class)
- **Class 4 — Isochronous real-time** (e.g., synchronized multi-axis drives, < 1 µs synchronization)

## 2.5 The OSI 7-Layer Model in Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *Which OSI layers are implemented in a typical Fieldbus?* Answer: Layers **1 (Physical), 2 (Data Link), and 7 (Application)** — this is the famous **"3-layer profile"** of most fieldbuses.

| OSI Layer | Function | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 7 — Application | Service to user | PROFIBUS-DP V0/V1/V2 services |
| 6 — Presentation | Data formatting | OPC UA information modelling |
| 5 — Session | Dialog control | Often not used |
| 4 — Transport | End-to-end, TCP/UDP | Used in PROFINET (TCP for acyclic, UDP for cyclic) |
| 3 — Network | Routing | IP for plant-wide addressing |
| 2 — Data Link | Framing, MAC, error detection | CSMA/CD (CAN), Token Passing (PROFIBUS) |
| 1 — Physical | Voltages, connectors, bit timing | RS-485, 100BASE-TX, fibre |

The **simplified 3-layer model** of industrial networks (Layers 1, 2, 7) is one of the most important KTU board points because it explains *why fieldbuses are so fast* — they skip the overhead of the upper layers.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following engineering relationships are used in industrial network planning and are the basis of numerical KTU problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} = T_{propagation} + T_{transmission} + T_{queuing} + T_{processing}$ | End-to-end cycle time (total latency) | Hard real-time analysis |
| 2 | $T_{transmission} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_{baud}}$ | Time to push a frame of $N_{bits}$ at baud rate $R_{baud}$ | Frame timing analysis |
| 3 | $\eta_{utilization} = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | Bandwidth utilization efficiency | Bus loading analysis |
| 4 | $N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{deadline}}{T_{cycle}} \right\rfloor$ | Max number of devices sharing a time window | Scheduling dimensioning |
| 5 | $J_{jitter} = T_{max} - T_{min}$ | Worst-case variation in delivery time | Determinism verification |
| 6 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Plant uptime calculation | Reliability engineering |
| 7 | $R_{ring} = 1 - (1 - R_{node})^{N}$ (for series) | Reliability of N identical nodes in series | Redundancy design |
| 8 | $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_{1})(1 - R_{2})$ | Reliability of redundant ring/links | Media redundancy (MRP, DLR) |
| 9 | $N_{addressable} = 2^{n} - 1$ | Maximum nodes for *n*-bit addressing | Fieldbus node count |
| 10 | $T_{baud}^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{R_{baud}}$ | Bit duration | Bit-time calculations |

### Real-World Utility

- **Equations 1–5** are used by automation engineers to **prove determinism** before deploying a PROFINET IRT or EtherCAT network with hundreds of drives.
- **Equations 6–8** are used in **functional safety** and **SIL** calculations (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511).
- **Equations 9–10** are used in **Fieldbus commissioning** to size the segment and choose baud rates (e.g., why PROFIBUS-DP at 1.5 Mbps limits you to ~32 devices per segment).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency of a Deterministic Fieldbus Frame

**Problem Statement:** A PROFIBUS-DP segment runs at $R_{baud} = 1.5$ Mbps. A process data frame contains **N = 240 bits** (header + payload + FCS + delimiters). The cable length is **L = 1000 m**; propagation velocity on twisted pair is approximately $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Queuing delay at a repeater is $T_{queuing} = 5\,\mu s$, and the processing delay at the master is $T_{processing} = 10\,\mu s$. Compute the **total cycle time** $T_{cycle}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the components of $T_{cycle}$.**

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{propagation} + T_{transmission} + T_{queuing} + T_{processing}
$$

> **Logic:** Every communication cycle on a deterministic bus is the sum of *time spent travelling*, *time spent pushing bits onto the wire*, *time spent waiting at intermediate nodes*, and *time spent decoding*.

**Step 2 — Compute the transmission time $T_{transmission}$.**

$$
T_{transmission} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{baud}} = \frac{240}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{transmission} = 160\ \mu s
$$

> **Logic:** We are sending 240 bits one after another at a clock rate of 1.5 million bits per second.

**Step 3 — Compute the propagation time $T_{propagation}$.**

$$
T_{propagation} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{propagation} = 5\ \mu s
$$

> **Logic:** An electrical signal needs ~5 nanoseconds per metre on twisted pair; over 1 km that is 5 microseconds.

**Step 4 — Substitute all known values into the master formula.**

$$
T_{cycle} = 5\ \mu s + 160\ \mu s + 5\ \mu s + 10\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 180\ \mu s = 0.18\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify against the PROFIBUS-DP hard real-time class.**

$$
T_{cycle} = 0.18\ \text{ms} \ll 1\ \text{ms}
$$

> **Logic:** A cycle time of 0.18 ms is well within the hard real-time envelope (sub-millisecond), confirming the segment is suitable for **fast I/O and motion control** applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Number of Field Devices per Segment

**Problem Statement:** A PLC must scan **N_devices** within a deterministic time window $T_{window} = 5$ ms using the cycle time from Derivation 1. Each device contributes one frame. How many devices can be addressed?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the scheduling constraint.**

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{window}}{T_{cycle}} \right\rfloor
$$

> **Logic:** The PLC has 5 ms to complete one full scan; in each cycle 0.18 ms is consumed, so the number of full cycles that fit is the integer floor of the ratio.

**Step 2 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{180 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor 27.77 \right\rfloor = 27
$$

**Step 3 — Apply protocol-specific derating.**

PROFIBUS-DP at 1.5 Mbps with default telegram overhead and one repeater typically supports **up to 32 stations per segment**; therefore:

$$
N_{max}^{practical} = \min(N_{max},\ 32) = \min(27,\ 32) = 27
$$

> **Logic:** The smaller of the two limits wins — we can only address 27 devices *within the deadline* even though the *electrical* limit is 32. This is a classic KTU "design-vs-protocol" trade-off.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bus Utilization Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** For the same PROFIBUS segment, the PLC also carries acyclic diagnostic traffic that consumes 20% of bandwidth. What is the **net utilization efficiency** for cyclic process data?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{utilization} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute useful-time fraction.**

$$
T_{useful} = T_{cycle} \times (1 - 0.20) = 180\ \mu s \times 0.80 = 144\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{utilization} = \frac{144\ \mu s}{180\ \mu s} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

> **Logic:** Out of the 180 microsecond cycle, only 144 µs carries real process data — 36 µs is consumed by protocol overhead and diagnostic services. KTU tip: cite this as the *reason why fieldbuses are preferred over Ethernet for control loops* — Ethernet has typically < 60% efficiency at high load.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Decision Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **direct KTU exam-prep tool** for "compare-and-justify" questions. Learn it by heart.

| Network | Real-Time Class | Topology | Max Nodes | Typical Use | Industrial Sector |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU** | Soft | Bus (RS-485) | **247** | SCADA polling | Water, energy |
| **PROFIBUS-DP** | Hard | Bus / Star | **126** (32/seg) | Discrete I/O, drives | Automotive, FMCG |
| **PROFINET** | Hard (RT/IRT) | Star | **Unlimited (managed)** | Plant-wide control | Process & discrete |
| **EtherCAT** | Isochronous | Line / Ring | **65,535** | High-speed motion | Robotics, machine tools |
| **DeviceNet** | Soft | Bus | **64** | Sensor / actuator | Material handling |
| **CAN / CANopen** | Hard | Bus | **2032** logical / 8 phys | Vehicle, embedded | Automotive, lifts |
| **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | Hard | Bus / Tree | **240 / segment** | Process automation | Oil & gas, chemicals |
| **AS-Interface (ASi)** | Soft | Line / Tree | **62** | Binary sensor level | Conveyor, packaging |
| **IO-Link** | Soft | Point-to-point | **1 master → N devices** | Smart sensor | Level 0 retrofit |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Soft–Hard | Star | Unlimited | Motion + IT convergence | Packaging, OEM |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Availability of a Redundant Ring

**Problem Statement:** A copper ring connecting 6 industrial switches has individual link reliability $R_{link} = 0.99$. Compute **(a)** the reliability of the ring if it depends on a single path in series, and **(b)** the reliability if both directions are active (parallel redundancy).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Series reliability.**

$$
R_{ring}^{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{6} R_{i} = (0.99)^{6}
$$

$$
R_{ring}^{series} = 0.9415 \quad \text{or}\quad 94.15\%
$$

> **Logic:** With one cable cut anywhere in the ring, the *only* path is broken — every link is in series, so reliability multiplies downward with each link.

**Part (b) — Parallel redundancy (both directions active).**

First compute per-direction reliability of one path with 6 links:

$$
R_{path} = 0.99^{6} = 0.9415
$$

Now combine the **two** counter-rotating paths in parallel:

$$
R_{ring}^{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_{path})^{2} = 1 - (1 - 0.9415)^{2}
$$

$$
R_{ring}^{parallel} = 1 - (0.0585)^{2} = 1 - 0.003422 = 0.996578
$$

$$
R_{ring}^{parallel} \approx 99.66\%
$$

> **Logic:** With **two** independent paths, the ring fails only if *both* paths are simultaneously broken. This is precisely why protocols like **DLR (Device Level Ring)** and **MRP (Media Redundancy Protocol)** exist — they convert 94% availability into 99.66% availability, which is the difference between 8 hours of plant downtime per month and 1 hour.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model — Industrial Network Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL4["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE NETWORK ERP and Corporate IT"]
    nodeL3["LEVEL 3 OPERATIONS NETWORK MES SCADA Historian Plant Reporting"]
    nodeL2["LEVEL 2 CONTROL AND SUPERVISORY LAN PROFINET EtherNet IP"]
    nodeL1["LEVEL 1 CONTROL AND FIELD BUS PROFIBUS CAN Modbus"]
    nodeL0["LEVEL 0 FIELD IO Sensors Actuators Drives IO Link"]

    nodeL4 --> nodeL3
    nodeL3 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL1
    nodeL1 --> nodeL0
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the canonical "automation pyramid" that defines *where* every industrial network lives. KTU exam tip: any answer that talks about "where PROFIBUS sits" or "where OPC UA sits" should explicitly name the level number.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Industrial Network Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeApp["Application Layer PROFIsafe CIP Safety Modbus Service"]
    nodeTr["Transport Layer TCP UDP RT RT over UDP"]
    nodeNet["Network Layer IP Subnets Routers VLANs"]
    nodeMac["Data Link Layer CSMA CD Token Passing Hub Switching"]
    nodePhy["Physical Layer RS 485 100BASE-TX Fibre Wireless IEC 61158"]

    nodeApp --> nodeTr
    nodeTr --> nodeNet
    nodeNet --> nodeMac
    nodeMac --> nodePhy
```

> **Reading Guide:** This shows the OSI 3-layer / 5-layer simplification actually deployed in industrial networks. The arrows follow the *data path* (top-down encapsulation, bottom-up decapsulation).

---

## 4.3 Deterministic Cycle Timeline — PROFINET IRT Class 3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["T0 Master Polls Slave 1"]
    nodeT1["T1 Slave 1 Replies with Process Data"]
    nodeT2["T2 Master Polls Slave 2"]
    nodeT3["T3 Slave 2 Replies with Process Data"]
    nodeT4["T4 Reserved Bandwidth for Acyclic and Diagnostics"]

    nodeT0 --> nodeT1 --> nodeT2 --> nodeT3 --> nodeT4 --> nodeT0
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **time-slice scheduling** used by hard real-time industrial Ethernet. Notice the *reserved* last slot for non-critical traffic — this is the architectural trick that gives PROFINET IRT its deterministic behaviour. Acyclic traffic cannot delay cyclic process data because it has no slot.

---

## 4.4 Fieldbus Topology Comparison — Bus, Ring, Star, Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeBus["BUS TOPOLOGY PROFIBUS CAN Modbus RTU"]
        nodeB1["PLC Master"]
        nodeB2["Slave 1"]
        nodeB3["Slave 2"]
        nodeB4["Slave N"]
        nodeB1 --- nodeB2
        nodeB2 --- nodeB3
        nodeB3 --- nodeB4
    end

    subgraph nodeRing["RING TOPOLOGY DLR MRP EtherCAT"]
        nodeR1["Switch 1"]
        nodeR2["Switch 2"]
        nodeR3["Switch 3"]
        nodeR4["Switch 4"]
        nodeR1 --- nodeR2
        nodeR2 --- nodeR3
        nodeR3 --- nodeR4
        nodeR4 --- nodeR1
    end

    subgraph nodeStar["STAR TOPOLOGY Industrial Ethernet"]
        nodeSCore["Managed Ethernet Switch"]
        nodeSD1["Device 1"]
        nodeSD2["Device 2"]
        nodeSD3["Device 3"]
        nodeSD4["Device 4"]
        nodeSCore --- nodeSD1
        nodeSCore --- nodeSD2
        nodeSCore --- nodeSD3
        nodeSCore --- nodeSD4
    end
```

> **Reading Guide:** Bus = cheap & long but single point of failure. Ring = same cable cost as bus but redundant. Star = highest availability, highest cable cost. KTU often asks: *which topology is used by PROFIBUS-DP?* — Answer: **Bus**, with optional repeaters to form trees.

---

## 4.5 Industrial Network Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["What is the dominant requirement"]
    nodeQ1["Hard real time sub millisecond"]
    nodeQ2["Deterministic cycle 1 to 10 ms"]
    nodeQ3["Best effort SCADA only"]
    nodeQ4["Bit level sensor actuator"]
    nodeAns1["EtherCAT or PROFINET IRT"]
    nodeAns2["PROFIBUS DP or PROFINET RT"]
    nodeAns3["Modbus TCP or EtherNet IP"]
    nodeAns4["IO Link or AS Interface"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeStart --> nodeQ2
    nodeStart --> nodeQ3
    nodeStart --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ1 --> nodeAns1
    nodeQ2 --> nodeAns2
    nodeQ3 --> nodeAns3
    nodeQ4 --> nodeAns4
```

> **Reading Guide:** This decision tree is the **closest thing to a KTU viva question** in the entire introduction module. A student who can recite this tree correctly answers almost any "which protocol would you choose and why" question in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*Define Industrial Networking. List any four key characteristics that distinguish an industrial network from a conventional office LAN.*

**Model Answer (12–15 lines expected):**

> **Definition:** Industrial Networking is the branch of communication engineering dealing with the design and operation of digital networks that interconnect sensors, controllers, actuators, and supervisory computers within a manufacturing or process plant to exchange real-time control and monitoring data.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Determinism:** The maximum time for any message to be delivered is *bounded and known in advance*. An office LAN offers "best effort" — a packet may take milliseconds or seconds; an industrial network guarantees delivery within a hard deadline (e.g., 1 ms).

2. **Real-Time Behaviour:** Industrial networks operate under **hard real-time** constraints (safety-critical) or **soft real-time** constraints (HMI refresh). Office networks have **no real-time requirement**.

3. **Harsh Environmental Tolerance:** Industrial hardware operates from **−40 °C to +75 °C**, with **IEC 61000-6-2** EMC compliance, shock, vibration, and humidity resistance. Office hardware is rated 0–40 °C and assumes clean air.

4. **Long Field Lifetime (15–20 years) and High Availability (99.99%):** Industrial networks are designed for *decades* of unattended operation and minimal maintenance. Office networks are typically refreshed every 3–5 years.

> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **D-R-E-L** — **D**eterminism, **R**eal-time, **E**nvironment, **L**ongevity.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for characteristics with brief justification.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the role of the OSI 7-layer model in industrial communication. Which layers are typically implemented in a classical Fieldbus and why?*

**Model Answer (≈15 lines + diagram):**

The **OSI Reference Model** decomposes communication into seven layers, each performing a well-defined service for the layer above and requesting services from the layer below. In industrial communication, the model is used as a *design reference* to ensure interoperability between devices from different vendors.

**Typical Fieldbus Layer Profile (the famous 3-layer model):**

- **Layer 1 — Physical:** Defines voltages, connectors, bit timing (e.g., RS-485 for PROFIBUS, 31.25 kbps current mode for Foundation Fieldbus H1).
- **Layer 2 — Data Link:** Implements media access (e.g., PROFIBUS token passing, CAN CSMA/CA+AMP), framing, and error detection.
- **Layer 7 — Application:** Provides user services (read/write process variables, diagnostics, parameterization).

**Why only these three?** Classical fieldbuses skip the upper layers (3–6) because they introduce unacceptable latency and overhead. The application layer directly invokes data-link services, achieving sub-millisecond cycle times.

**Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP)** additionally uses **Layer 3 (IP)** and **Layer 4 (TCP/UDP)** for acyclic, diagnostic, and parameter data, while keeping cyclic process data on Layer 2 cut-through forwarding for hard real-time performance.

*(3 marks: 1 for diagram, 1 for layer identification, 1 for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### OPTION A — Full 14-Mark Question

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast **Office Networks and Industrial Networks** across any **seven technical parameters**, with a short justification for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95) diagram**, describe the **five hierarchy levels of an industrial plant**. For each level, name the **type of network** typically deployed and the **class of data** carried. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Office vs. Industrial Network Comparison

| # | Parameter | Office Network | Industrial Network | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Purpose** | Information exchange, web, email | **Real-time control & monitoring** | Industry needs deterministic commands, not document transfer |
| 2 | **Latency** | Best-effort, seconds | **Hard real-time, sub-ms to ms** | A 100 ms delay can ruin a 1,000 bottles/min line |
| 3 | **Determinism** | Not guaranteed | **Bounded and verifiable** | Required for motion control and interlocks |
| 4 | **Environment** | Air-conditioned, 0–40 °C | **−40 to +75 °C, dust, EMI, vibration** | Field cabinets in steel plants, refineries |
| 5 | **Hardware Reliability** | Commercial grade | **Industrial grade, MTBF > 100,000 h** | No scheduled downtime in a continuous plant |
| 6 | **Topology** | Star, switched | **Bus, ring, line, star, tree** | Bus minimises cable cost in the field |
| 7 | **Protocol** | TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS | **PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Modbus, EtherCAT, CAN, Foundation Fieldbus** | Open fieldbus standards enable multi-vendor integration |
| 8 | **Cable** | Cat-5/6 UTP, fibre | **Shielded twisted pair, Profibus cable, fibre, M12 connectors** | Shielding rejects common-mode noise from VFDs |
| 9 | **Cybersecurity** | High (firewalls, IDS) | **Zone & conduit (IEC 62443)**, isolation from IT | Bridging IT and OT is now mandated |
| 10 | **Lifetime** | 3–5 years | **15–20 years** | Retrofit cost is prohibitive in plants |

*(7 marks awarded as: 1 mark for table correctness per parameter, 1 mark for justification quality, 1 mark for the concluding engineering insight.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Purdue Model / ISA-95 Five Levels

```
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
LEVEL 4 │ Enterprise Zone  —  ERP, Corporate IT     │  ← Classical IT
        ├───────────────────────────────────────────┤
LEVEL 3 │ Operations Zone — MES, SCADA, Historian  │  ← Plant operations
        ├───────────────────────────────────────────┤
LEVEL 2 │ Supervisory LAN — PROFINET, EtherNet/IP   │  ← Control network
        ├───────────────────────────────────────────┤
LEVEL 1 │ Control / Fieldbus — PROFIBUS, Modbus     │  ← Real-time I/O
        ├───────────────────────────────────────────┤
LEVEL 0 │ Field I/O — Sensors, Actuators, IO-Link   │  ← Physical process
        └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

| Level | Function | Typical Network | Data Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Physical process: temperature, pressure, flow, level, speed | **IO-Link, AS-Interface, point-to-point 4–20 mA** | Raw sensor / actuator signals |
| **Level 1** | Discrete and continuous control, interlocking, I/O scan | **PROFIBUS-DP, Modbus RTU, DeviceNet, CANopen, Foundation Fieldbus H1** | Cyclic process variables (real-time) |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory control, HMI, recipe management | **PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, OPC UA** | Cyclic process image + acyclic diagnostics |
| **Level 3** | Plant-wide operations, production reporting, batch management | **Industrial Ethernet, OPC UA, MQTT, plant historian network** | Acyclic historical and statistical data |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise resource planning, corporate functions | **Classical IT network, TCP/IP, ERP interfaces (SAP, Oracle)** | Business and administrative data |

> **Engineering Justification (1 mark):** Each lower level operates with a *stricter* real-time class than the level above it. This is why a single homogeneous TCP/IP network cannot serve all five levels — the determinism and bandwidth trade-offs differ at every tier.

*(7 marks awarded as: 1 mark for diagram, 4 marks for correctly identifying networks/data at each of the 5 levels, 1 mark for engineering justification, 1 mark for presentation.)*

---

### OPTION B — Alternative 14-Mark Question

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Retest Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the **evolution of industrial communication systems** from analog 4–20 mA loops to modern Industrial Ethernet and TSN. List **three technical limitations** of the analog era that digital fieldbuses solved. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PROFIBUS-DP segment must scan **30 field devices** with each device producing a **128-bit** process data frame. The baud rate is **500 kbps**, propagation velocity is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, total cable length is **500 m**, and the bus master requires **one polling token** cycle per device. Compute: 
(i) the transmission time per frame,
(ii) the total cycle time including propagation and a fixed **20 µs** of processing overhead per device, and
(iii) the bus utilization efficiency if 15% of the cycle is consumed by acyclic diagnostics. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Numerical

**Given:**
$N = 30$ devices, $L = 128$ bits, $R_{baud} = 500$ kbps, $D = 500$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $T_{proc} = 20\ \mu s$ per device.

**(i) Transmission time per frame:**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{L_{bits}}{R_{baud}} = \frac{128}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 2.56 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{tx} = 256\ \mu s
$$

> **Logic:** A 128-bit frame at half-megabaud occupies 256 microseconds on the wire.

**(ii) Total cycle time:**

Propagation (one direction):

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5\ \mu s
$$

Per-device cycle contribution:

$$
T_{device} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} = 256 + 2.5 + 20 = 278.5\ \mu s
$$

Total for 30 devices:

$$
T_{cycle}^{total} = 30 \times 278.5\ \mu s = 8355\ \mu s = 8.355\ \text{ms}
$$

> **Logic:** Each device occupies ~278 µs of the bus; 30 devices serialize into ~8.36 ms. This is **acceptable for soft real-time process control** (10–100 ms class) but **not for hard motion control** (1 ms class).

**(iii) Bus utilization efficiency:**

Acyclic diagnostics consume 15% of the cycle, leaving 85% useful:

$$
\eta_{util} = (1 - 0.15) \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 3 for (ii) including per-device breakdown, 1 for (iii), 1 for the engineering conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Writing "Industrial Network = LAN" in factory** — Examiner deducts for failing to mention **determinism** and **real-time**; these two words alone are worth 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Fieldbus with Industrial Ethernet** — Fieldbus = typically layers 1, 2, 7; Industrial Ethernet = uses layers 1–4 and 7. Mixing them up = −1 to −2 marks.
> 3. **Numerical mistakes in $T_{cycle}$ problems** — Forgetting to add $T_{prop}$ or $T_{proc}$ is the #1 error. Always list all four components *before* plugging numbers.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the final answer ($T_{cycle}$ in ms, not in bits). Examiner commonly deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 5. **Purdue Model labelling errors** — Level 0 is *process*, not "input". Level 4 is *enterprise*, not "internet". Misnaming costs at least 1 mark.
> 6. **No diagram in Q3-type answers** — KTU mandates a diagram for "explain with neat block diagram" questions; absence = automatic −1 mark.
> 7. **Writing TCP/IP as a "real-time protocol"** — TCP is **not** real-time. Use TCP/UDP only for acyclic; cyclic real-time needs hardware cut-through (e.g., EtherCAT) or scheduled time slots (e.g., PROFINET IRT).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list 3 times the night before the exam — these are the highest-yield KTU Module 1 facts.**

- ✅ Industrial Networking = **time-deterministic, real-time communication** in plants.
- ✅ The **Purdue Model** has **5 levels** (0 to 4); remember the network at each level.
- ✅ **Determinism** is the single most important property — bounded worst-case latency.
- ✅ **Fieldbus uses a 3-layer OSI profile** (Layers 1, 2, 7); **Industrial Ethernet** uses all relevant layers.
- ✅ **Hard real-time** = sub-millisecond, motion / safety; **Soft real-time** = 10–100 ms, HMI / SCADA.
- ✅ **Office Network** = best-effort, commercial, 3–5 yr life; **Industrial Network** = deterministic, ruggedized, 15–20 yr life.
- ✅ Four key protocol families: **Modbus, PROFIBUS/PROFINET, CAN/DeviceNet, Foundation Fieldbus**, plus Industrial Ethernet (EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT).
- ✅ MTBF for industrial hardware is **> 100,000 hours** (> 11 years continuous).
- ✅ Industrial temperature range: **−40 °C to +75 °C**; EMC: **IEC 61000-6-2**.
- ✅ Cycle time formula: $T_{cycle} = T_{prop} + T_{tx} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ — **always list all four**.
- ✅ Transmission time formula: $T_{tx} = N_{bits} / R_{baud}$ — keep **units consistent** (bits / bps = seconds).
- ✅ Propagation time formula: $T_{prop} = L / v$, with $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s on twisted pair.
- ✅ Redundant ring reliability: $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_{path})^{2}$ — this is why **DLR/MRP** are deployed.
- ✅ Availability target: **99.99%** ("four nines") is the industrial minimum; process plants aim for **99.999%**.
- ✅ **5G of industrial communication** = Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT).
- ✅ **6G of industrial communication** = TSN + IIoT + 5G URLLC + OPC UA over MQTT.
- ✅ **Modbus RTU** is still the most-deployed fieldbus worldwide; **PROFINET** leads Industrial Ethernet in Europe; **EtherNet/IP** leads in North America.
- ✅ **AS-Interface** is the simplest binary-sensor network (Level 0); **IO-Link** is the modern replacement.
- ✅ **Safety** protocols (PROFIsafe, CIP Safety, FSoE) ride *on top of* the same physical layer — they do not require a separate cable.
- ✅ For a KTU 14-mark answer, **always include a labelled diagram** (block diagram / pyramid / topology). It is the cheapest mark on the paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibre Optic Cable Components and Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fibre Optic Cable Components and Parameters # 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Fibre Optic Cable? A **Fibre Optic Cable** is a guided-wave, dielectric transmission medium that propagates information-carrying light pulses through a hair-thin strand of ultra-pure glass (silica, $\text{SiO}_2$) or plastic polymer. It operates on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core–cladding interface, enabling high-bandwidth, low-attenuation, and EMI-immune communication across industrial networks, telecom backbones, and process-control fieldbuses. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"A fibre optic cable is a network cable that contains strands of glass fibres inside an insulated casing. They are designed for long-distance, high-performance data networking and telecommunications. Compared to wired cables, fibre optic cables provide higher bandwidth and transmit data over longer distances."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Pipe in a Water Fountain" Imagine a long, transparent glass tube. You shine a flashlight into one end at a slight angle. If the tube is curved gently, the light still emerges at the other end — **bouncing off the inner walls instead of escaping**. | Real-World Object | Optical Fibre Equivalent | |---|---| | The glass tube itself | **Core** (carries the light) | | The thin mirror coating on the outer wall | **Cladding** (reflects light back into core) | | The black rubber outer sleeve | **Jacket / Buffer** (mechanical & environmental protection) | | The flashlight beam | **Modulated light signal** (carries data) | The fibre is essentially a **"light pipe"** — it traps photons the way a copper wire traps electrons, but with vastly superior speed, distance, and noise immunity. ## 1.3 Why Fibre Optics Matter in Industrial Networking In factories with heavy motors, VFDs, and welding equipment, **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** destroys copper-based signals. Fibre is **immune to EMI**, intrinsically safe in explosive environments (no spark risk), and supports the high data rates demanded by **EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, and Modbus TCP** at the field level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:** > - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ > - Refractive index of vacuum: $n_0 = 1.000$ > - Refractive index of typical silica core: $n_1 \approx 1.48$ > - Refractive index of typical silica cladding: $n_2 \approx 1.46$ > - Operating wavelengths: **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** ## 1.4 Classification Overview $$ \text{Fibre Optic Cable} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Single-Mode Fibre (SMF)} \\ \text{Multi-Mode Fibre (MMF)} \begin{cases} \text{Step-Index (SI)} \\ \text{Graded-Index (GI)} \end{cases} \end{cases} $$ # 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Components of a Fibre Optic Cable (Layer by Layer) A fibre optic cable is a **layered cylindrical structure**. From inside out: 1. **Core ($n_1$)** — The central glass region (diameter 8–10 µm for SMF, 50/62.5 µm for MMF) where light actually propagates. 2. **Cladding ($n_2 < n_1$)** — Surrounds the core. The refractive index difference forces TIR. Typical diameter: **125 µm** (standard across all silica fibres). 3. **Primary Coating / Buffer** — A soft acrylate layer (250 µm) that absorbs micro-bending losses and mechanical shocks. 4. **Secondary Coating / Buffer** — A tougher polymer jacket protecting against abrasion. 5. **Strength Members** — **Kevlar (Aramid yarn)** strands to bear tensile loads during installation. 6. **Outer Jacket** — PVC, LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen), or PE sheath, color-coded (yellow = SMF, orange = 62.5 µm MMF, aqua = 50 µm MMF OM3/OM4). > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Networking Relevance:** For plant-floor deployments, look for **armoured fibre cables with steel tape** (to resist crushing in cable trays) and **LSZH jackets** (mandatory in tunnels, ships, and chemical plants). ## 2.2 The Three Critical Optical Parameters ### Parameter 1: Numerical Aperture (NA) The **Numerical Aperture** defines the light-gathering ability of the fibre. It is a dimensionless quantity that characterises the cone of light accepted by the core. $$ \text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ - Higher NA → larger acceptance cone → easier coupling from LED sources. - Lower NA → tighter beam → better for long-distance laser-based SMF links. ### Parameter 2: Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$) The **maximum angle** (measured from the fibre axis) at which an incoming ray will be guided through the core via TIR. $$ \sin \theta_a = \text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ ### Parameter 3: Critical Angle ($\theta_c$) The angle of incidence (inside the core, at the core-cladding interface) below which the ray **escapes** instead of being totally internally reflected. $$ \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ ## 2.3 Transmission Loss Parameters ### Attenuation Coefficient ($\alpha$) Measured in **dB/km**, it quantifies signal loss per unit length. $$ \alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB/km]} $$ Typical values: - **850 nm window:** $\alpha \approx 2\text{–}3$ dB/km (MMF, LED sources) - **1300 nm window:** $\alpha \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5$ dB/km (SMF/MMF) - **1550 nm window:** $\alpha \approx 0.2$ dB/km (SMF, telecom-grade) ### Dispersion & Bandwidth **Dispersion** is the spreading of light pulses, limiting bandwidth-distance product. $$ \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Distance} = B \cdot L \quad \text{[MHz·km]} $$ - MMF OM1: 200 MHz·km @ 850 nm - MMF OM3: 2000 MHz·km @ 850 nm (laser-optimised) - SMF: virtually unlimited (limited by chromatic dispersion compensation) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Range | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless (0.1–0.5) | Light collection efficiency | | 2 | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ | degrees / radians | Coupling lens design | | 3 | Critical Angle | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 \mid n_1)$ | degrees / radians | TIR boundary check | | 4 | Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | dimensionless | Ray tracing at interface | | 5 | Velocity in medium | $v = c \mid n$ | m/s | Propagation delay calc. | | 6 | Attenuation Coeff. | $\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}} \mid P_{\text{in}})$ | dB/km | Link loss budget | | 7 | Total Link Loss | $L_{\text{total}} = \alpha L + L_{\text{splices}} + L_{\text{connectors}}$ | dB | Power budget margin | | 8 | Refractive Index Profile (GRIN) | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r \mid a)^\gamma}$ | dimensionless | MMF-GI design | | 9 | Relative Index Diff. | $\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | dimensionless (0.002–0.02) | NA derivation | | 10 | Number of Modes | $M \approx \frac{V^2}{2}$ | count | Multi-mode capacity | | 11 | V-Number (Normalised Frequency) | $V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot \text{NA}$ | dimensionless | Single vs multi-mode cutoff | | 12 | Propagation Delay | $t_d = \frac{L \cdot n_1}{c}$ | seconds | Latency in industrial ring | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Quick Tip:** Always write the **unit** alongside the formula. A correct formula without unit = **−1 mark deduction** in valuation. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Factory Floor Backbones:** Single-mode fibre links SCADA / DCS controllers across plant distances up to 40 km without repeaters. - **Substation Communications:** IEC 61850-based protection signalling uses fibre to isolate ground loops in high-voltage switchyards. - **Marine & Offshore:** LSZH-jacketed armoured fibre survives hydrocarbon exposure and saltwater corrosion. - **Data Centres:** OM4 MMF handles 100 GbE at 150 m; SMF handles 100 GbE at 40 km. # 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Numerical Aperture (NA) **Given:** A step-index fibre with core refractive index $n_1$, cladding refractive index $n_2$ ($n_1 > n_2$). A light ray enters the fibre from air ($n_0 = 1$) at an external angle $\theta_0$ to the fibre axis. **Goal:** Express the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ in terms of $n_1$ and $n_2$. ### Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the air–core entrance face The ray strikes the flat entrance face at angle $\theta_0$ (external). Inside the core, it refracts to angle $\theta_1$ (with respect to the axis). $$ n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1 $$ Since $n_0 = 1$ (air), this reduces to: $$ \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1 $$ ### Step 2 — Relate internal refraction angle to incidence at the core–cladding interface By geometry of the right triangle formed by the ray inside the core: $$ \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90^\circ \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 90^\circ - \theta_1 $$ where $\theta_2$ is the angle of incidence at the core–cladding interface (measured from the normal). ### Step 3 — Apply the Total Internal Reflection (TIR) condition at the core–cladding interface For TIR to occur, the angle of incidence must equal or exceed the critical angle $\theta_c$: $$ \theta_2 \geq \theta_c \quad \text{where} \quad \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ For the **limiting (worst-case) ray** that just undergoes TIR, set $\theta_2 = \theta_c$: $$ \sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ $$ \cos \theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ ### Step 4 — Use trigonometric identity to eliminate $\theta_1$ Recall: $\sin^2 \theta_1 + \cos^2 \theta_1 = 1$. $$ \sin^2 \theta_1 = 1 - \cos^2 \theta_1 = 1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2 $$ $$ \sin \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back into Snell's Law from Step 1 $$ \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \cdot \sin \theta_1 = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} $$ $$ \sin \theta_0 = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ ### Step 6 — Define Numerical Aperture The maximum external acceptance angle is denoted $\theta_a$, and its sine is defined as the **Numerical Aperture**: $$ \boxed{\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Significance:** This formula tells us that a **larger index difference** between core and cladding yields a **higher NA**, meaning the fibre can collect light from a wider cone — crucial for cheap LED sources in industrial MMF links. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Attenuation Coefficient from Power Measurements **Given:** Input optical power $P_{\text{in}}$ at fibre start; output power $P_{\text{out}}$ after length $L$ km. Assume only intrinsic fibre loss (no splices/connectors). **Step 1:** Define attenuation in decibels (dB): $$ A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) $$ *Explanation:* A ratio of 10:1 in power = 10 dB loss; 100:1 = 20 dB loss. **Step 2:** Define the **attenuation per unit length** (coefficient $\alpha$): $$ \alpha = \frac{A_{\text{dB}}}{L} $$ where $L$ is in km and $\alpha$ is in dB/km. **Step 3:** Combine the two: $$ \alpha = \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) $$ Rewriting with a negative sign for a lossy medium (since $P_{\text{out}} < P_{\text{in}}$): $$ \boxed{\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB/km]} } $$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** An optical fibre of length 12 km transmits a signal. Input power = 1 mW, output power = 0.4 mW. Calculate the attenuation coefficient in dB/km. **Solution:** $$ \alpha = -\frac{10}{12} \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.4 \text{ mW}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) $$ $$ \alpha = -\frac{10}{12} \log_{10}(0.4) $$ $$ \alpha = -\frac{10}{12} \times (-0.3979) $$ $$ \alpha = \frac{10 \times 0.3979}{12} = \frac{3.979}{12} $$ $$ \alpha \approx 0.332 \text{ dB/km} $$ This corresponds to the **1300 nm transmission window** — consistent with low-loss silica fibre. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fibre Types | Parameter | SMF (Step Index) | MMF (Step Index) | MMF (Graded Index) | |---|---|---|---| | Core diameter | 8–10 µm | 50 / 62.5 / 100 µm | 50 / 62.5 µm | | Cladding diameter | 125 µm | 125 µm | 125 µm | | NA | 0.10 – 0.15 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.20 – 0.30 | | Bandwidth·Distance | > 100 GHz·km | 10 – 200 MHz·km | 200 – 2000 MHz·km | | Source type | Laser (LD) | LED | LED / VCSEL | | Wavelength | 1310 / 1550 nm | 850 / 1300 nm | 850 / 1300 nm | | Cost | High | Low | Moderate | | Industrial use | Backbone, long-haul | Short links, PLC comms | LAN, data centres | | Modal dispersion | None (single mode) | High | Low (parabolic profile) | # 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fibre Optic Cable Cross-Section ```mermaid graph TD subgraph coreLayer["Inner Light-Guidance Region"] nodeCore["CORE
Silica Glass n1 approx 1.48
Diameter 8 to 62.5 um"] nodeClad["CLADDING
Silica Glass n2 approx 1.46
Diameter 125 um"] end subgraph coatLayer["Protective Coatings"] nodePrim["PRIMARY BUFFER
Soft Acrylate 250 um"] nodeSec["SECONDARY BUFFER
Hard Polymer 900 um"] end subgraph strengthLayer["Strength and Jacket"] nodeKev["STRENGTH MEMBERS
Kevlar Aramid Yarn"] nodeJack["OUTER JACKET
PVC or LSZH
Colour Yellow Orange Aqua"] nodeArm["ARMOUR Optional
Steel Tape for Industrial"] end nodeCore --> nodeClad nodeClad --> nodePrim nodePrim --> nodeSec nodeSec --> nodeKev nodeKev --> nodeJack nodeJack --> nodeArm ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Light Propagation by Total Internal Reflection ```mermaid graph LR subgraph inputA["External Medium Air"] nodeIn["Incident Ray
Angle theta0"] end subgraph coreB["Optical Fibre Core n1"] nodeRef["Refracted Ray
Angle theta1"] nodeBounce1["TIR Bounce 1
at core cladding"] nodeBounce2["TIR Bounce 2"] nodeBounce3["TIR Bounce 3"] end subgraph outputC["Far End"] nodeOut["Emergent Ray
Modulated Signal"] end nodeIn --> nodeRef nodeRef --> nodeBounce1 nodeBounce1 --> nodeBounce2 nodeBounce2 --> nodeBounce3 nodeBounce3 --> nodeOut ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Industrial Fibre Link ```mermaid graph TD subgraph txEnd["Transmitter End"] nodePLC1["PLC or SCADA Node A"] nodeELEC["E O Converter
Driver Circuit"] nodeLEDorLD["Light Source
LED 850nm or Laser 1310nm"] end subgraph linkMid["Fibre Cable Plant"] nodeConnector1["ST or SC Connector"] nodeFibre["Optical Fibre Core Cladding"] nodeSplice["Fusion Splice or Mechanical Splice"] nodeConnector2["Connector Far End"] end subgraph rxEnd["Receiver End"] nodePD["Photodetector
PIN or APD"] nodeOEconv["O E Converter
Amplifier"] nodePLC2["PLC or SCADA Node B"] end nodePLC1 --> nodeELEC nodeELEC --> nodeLEDorLD nodeLEDorLD --> nodeConnector1 nodeConnector1 --> nodeFibre nodeFibre --> nodeSplice nodeSplice --> nodeConnector2 nodeConnector2 --> nodePD nodePD --> nodeOEconv nodeOEconv --> nodePLC2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** This functional block diagram maps the complete signal chain — from the industrial controller's electrical signal through E/O conversion, light propagation in the fibre (with splice and connector junctions that contribute insertion loss), back to O/E conversion at the receiver. # 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fibre. Mention its significance in fibre optic communication.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — to-the-point KTU format):** The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fibre is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the light-gathering ability of the fibre core. It is mathematically defined as: $$ \text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ where $\theta_a$ is the maximum acceptance angle, $n_1$ is the refractive index of the core, and $n_2$ is the refractive index of the cladding. **Significance:** A higher NA allows the fibre to accept light from a wider cone, enabling easier coupling from inexpensive LED sources — critical for industrial multi-mode links. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for significance)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** **List any three major components of a fibre optic cable and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — bulleted KTU format):** 1. **Core** — The central glass region ($n_1 \approx 1.48$) through which the optical signal propagates by total internal reflection. *(1 mark)* 2. **Cladding** — A surrounding glass layer ($n_2 \approx 1.46$, $n_2 < n_1$) that provides the refractive index step required for TIR and confines light within the core. *(1 mark)* 3. **Kevlar (Aramid) Strength Members** — Bear tensile loads during installation, preventing fibre stretch and breakage. *(1 mark)* *(Acceptable alternatives: Primary/Secondary Buffer, Outer Jacket — each with 1 mark if explained correctly.)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation]** --- ### ✅ **QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a fibre optic cable and discuss the function of each layer. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A fibre optic cable is a multi-layered cylindrical structure designed to protect the delicate glass fibre while enabling efficient light transmission. The major components (from inner to outer) are: **(i) Core (1 mark):** The innermost region made of high-purity silica glass ($n_1 \approx 1.48$). Diameter: **8–10 µm for SMF**, **50/62.5 µm for MMF**. Light propagates through this region. **(ii) Cladding (1 mark):** Surrounds the core with slightly lower refractive index ($n_2 \approx 1.46$). The core-cladding interface provides the TIR boundary. Standard outer diameter: **125 µm**. **(iii) Primary Coating / Buffer (1 mark):** A soft acrylate layer (~250 µm diameter) that absorbs microbending losses and cushions the glass against mechanical shocks. **(iv) Secondary Coating (1 mark):** A harder polymer layer (~900 µm diameter) providing abrasion resistance and ease of handling. **(v) Strength Members (1 mark):** **Kevlar (Aramid yarn)** strands wound around the buffered fibre. They bear tensile load during installation. **(vi) Outer Jacket (1 mark):** PVC, PE, or **LSZH** polymer sheath. Colour-coded as per TIA standards (yellow = SMF, orange = OM1/OM2 MMF, aqua = OM3/OM4 MMF). Protects against moisture, chemicals, and crushing. **(vii) Armour (1 mark, optional but industrial-grade):** Steel tape armour for outdoor/industrial cables to resist rodent bites and crushing in cable trays. A neat block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above) earns full credit. --- **(b) Define acceptance angle and numerical aperture. Derive the expression for NA in terms of core and cladding refractive indices. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The **acceptance angle** $\theta_a$ is the maximum angle (measured from the fibre axis) at which an incoming light ray will be coupled into the core and propagate by total internal reflection. The **numerical aperture** is defined as $\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a$ and represents the light-gathering capacity of the fibre. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let an external ray strike the fibre entrance face at angle $\theta_0$ from the axis. Inside the core, it refracts to angle $\theta_1$. **Step 1 — Snell's Law at entrance:** $$ n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1 $$ Since $n_0 = 1$ (air): $$ \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1 $$ **Step 2 — Geometry of internal ray:** $$ \theta_2 = 90^\circ - \theta_1 $$ **Step 3 — TIR condition at core-cladding boundary:** $$ \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ For the worst-case (limiting) ray: $\theta_2 = \theta_c$. **Step 4 — Substituting and solving:** $$ \sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ $$ \sin \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} $$ **Step 5 — Final expression:** $$ \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} $$ --- ### ✅ **QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative)** **(a) Explain the different types of optical fibres based on the refractive index profile and mode of propagation. Compare their characteristics. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(1) Based on Refractive Index Profile (2 marks):** - **Step-Index Fibre:** The refractive index changes abruptly from $n_1$ (core) to $n_2$ (cladding) at the interface. Constant $n_1$ throughout the core. - **Graded-Index Fibre:** The refractive index $n(r)$ varies parabolically with radius $r$, being maximum at the centre and decreasing toward the cladding. This causes continuous refraction, bending rays smoothly back toward the axis. **(2) Based on Mode of Propagation (2 marks):** - **Single-Mode Fibre (SMF):** Core diameter ~8–10 µm; only one propagation mode (fundamental). Requires laser source. - **Multi-Mode Fibre (MMF):** Core diameter 50/62.5/100 µm; supports hundreds of modes. Uses LED or VCSEL. **(3) Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Property | SMF (Step Index) | MMF (Step Index) | MMF (Graded Index) | |---|---|---|---| | Core diameter | 8–10 µm | 50–100 µm | 50–62.5 µm | | NA | 0.10–0.15 | 0.20–0.30 | 0.20–0.30 | | Bandwidth | Very high | Low | High | | Attenuation | 0.2 dB/km @ 1550 nm | 3 dB/km @ 850 nm | 0.5 dB/km @ 1300 nm | | Source | Laser (LD) | LED | LED / VCSEL | | Cost | High | Low | Moderate | | Application | Telecom, long-haul | Short links | LAN, data centres | --- **(b) An optical fibre has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance Angle, and (iii) Critical Angle. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$ **(i) Numerical Aperture (2 marks):** $$ \text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} $$ $$ \text{NA} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{NA} \approx 0.384} $$ **(ii) Acceptance Angle (2 marks):** $$ \theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}) = \sin^{-1}(0.384) $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_a \approx 22.6^\circ} $$ **(iii) Critical Angle (2 marks):** $$ \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} = 0.9667 $$ $$ \theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9667) $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.2^\circ} $$ **Verification of TIR (1 mark):** Since $\theta_c = 75.2^\circ$, any internal ray striking the core-cladding interface at an angle greater than $75.2^\circ$ (from normal) will undergo total internal reflection — confirming proper light guiding. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the medium in Snell's Law.** When a ray enters from air, students write $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ directly at the entrance — **WRONG**. Always write $n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$ and substitute $n_0 = 1$ separately. > 2. **Confusing acceptance angle with critical angle.** $\theta_a$ is measured from the fibre **axis** (external); $\theta_c$ is measured from the **normal** at the interface (internal). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks if dB/km or µm units are missing. > 4. **Forgetting $\log_{10}$ base in attenuation formula.** Writing $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ gives wrong numerical answer — easy 1-mark trap. > 5. **Not stating TIR condition explicitly.** Always write "for total internal reflection, $\theta_{\text{incidence}} > \theta_c$" before solving. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Fibre Optic Cable** = light-guiding medium using **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. - ✅ **Core** ($n_1$) carries light; **Cladding** ($n_2 < n_1$) confines it; **Jacket** + **Kevlar** protect it. - ✅ **Numerical Aperture:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ → dimensionless. - ✅ **Acceptance Angle:** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ → measured from axis, external to fibre. - ✅ **Critical Angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 \mid n_1)$ → measured from normal, internal to core. - ✅ **TIR Condition:** $\theta_{\text{incidence at interface}} \geq \theta_c$ AND $n_1 > n_2$. - ✅ **Attenuation:** $\alpha = -\frac{10}{L}\log_{10}(P_{\text{out}} \mid P_{\text{in}})$ in **dB/km**. - ✅ **Wavelengths of Operation:** 850 nm (short), 1300 nm (medium), 1550 nm (long-haul). - ✅ **SMF** = single mode, 8–10 µm core, laser source, long distance, high bandwidth. - ✅ **MMF** = multi-mode, 50/62.5 µm core, LED source, short distance, lower bandwidth. - ✅ **Step Index** = abrupt $n$ change; **Graded Index** = parabolic $n(r)$, reduces modal dispersion. - ✅ **Industrial Applications:** EMI-immune PLC/SCADA backbones, substation IEC 61850 links, hazardous-area intrinsically safe networks. - ✅ **Link Loss Budget** = $\alpha L + \text{Splice Loss} + \text{Connector Loss}$ (all in dB). - ✅ **Standard Cladding Diameter:** Always **125 µm** (regardless of SMF/MMF). - ✅ **Standard Buffer Diameter:** **250 µm** (coated) and **900 µm** (tight buffered). - ✅ **Colour Code:** Yellow (SMF), Orange (OM1/OM2), Aqua (OM3/OM4), Erika Violet (OM5).

topics.title: Basic Cable Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Cable Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Industrial Networking**, *Basic Cable Types* refer to the standardized physical transmission media used to carry data signals, control commands, and power between field devices, controllers, sensors, actuators, and supervisory systems in an industrial automation environment. These cables form the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI reference model and are governed by standards issued by **IEEE (802.3)**, **TIA/EIA (568 series)**, **IEC (61156, 61754)**, and **ISO/IEC 11801**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under PERUT741, *Basic Cable Types* covers the classification, construction, electrical characteristics, and application suitability of copper-based and fiber-based media used in factory-floor, process-plant, and control-room networks.

The three principal families recognized by KTU and international standards bodies are:

1. **Coaxial Cables** — Concentric conductor design with controlled characteristic impedance.
2. **Twisted-Pair Cables** — Balanced-pair copper media, subdivided into **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)**, **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)**, **FTP (Foiled Twisted Pair)**, and **S/FTP (Screened Foiled Twisted Pair)**.
3. **Fibre-Optic Cables** — Glass or plastic waveguide media, classified as **SMF (Single-Mode Fibre)** and **MMF (Multi-Mode Fibre)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Industrial "Nervous System"

Imagine a large manufacturing plant as a living organism. The Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) are the **brain**, the sensors are the **senses (eyes, ears, skin)**, and the actuators are the **muscles**. The cables connecting them are literally the **nervous system** of the plant. Just as the human nervous system uses different nerve types (motor neurons for fast movement, sensory neurons for fine touch, spinal cords for long-distance signalling), an industrial network uses **different cable types** matched to the *speed*, *distance*, and *electrical-noise environment* of the connection.

- A **coaxial cable** is like a **protected motor-neuron bundle** — robust, shielded, and used for legacy high-frequency runs.
- A **twisted-pair cable** is like a **spinal nerve** — versatile, balanced, and the universal medium for Ethernet and fieldbus.
- A **fibre-optic cable** is like the **optic nerve** — immune to electrical noise and capable of carrying signals at the speed of light over kilometres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - Velocity of propagation in fibre (typical): $\approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (**≈ 67% of $c$**)
> - Velocity of propagation in Cat-6 UTP: $\approx 1.97 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (**≈ 66% of $c$**)
> - Nominal characteristic impedance of Ethernet twisted pair: **$Z_0 = 100 \, \Omega$** (legacy token ring used $150 \, \Omega$; coaxial Ethernet uses $50 \, \Omega$).

### 1.3 Classification Overview

| Media Class | Typical Bandwidth | Max Segment Length | EMI Immunity | Industrial Use |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Coaxial (RG-58, RG-6) | up to 1 GHz | 185 m (10BASE-2) | High (moderate) | Legacy fieldbus, video |
| UTP Cat-5e | 100 MHz | 100 m | Low–Moderate | Office Ethernet, light-industrial |
| STP/FTP Cat-6a | 500 MHz | 100 m | High | Factory-floor Ethernet |
| S/FTP Cat-7 | 600 MHz | 100 m | Very High | Heavy-industrial, motor drives |
| MMF (OM3) | 2000 MHz·km | 300 m (10G) | Total (immune) | Plant backbone, noisy zones |
| SMF (OS2) | Very high | 40 km+ | Total (immune) | Inter-plant, hazardous areas |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coaxial Cable — Construction and Theory

A **coaxial cable** consists of four concentric elements:

1. **Inner conductor** (solid or stranded copper).
2. **Dielectric insulator** (foamed polyethylene, PE, or PTFE for high-temperature industrial use).
3. **Outer conductor / shield** (braided copper, aluminium foil, or both).
4. **Outer jacket** (PVC, LSZH, or PUR for chemical/abrasion resistance).

The electromagnetic field is confined between the inner and outer conductor — this is the **"coaxial"** principle. Because the field does not radiate and is not affected by external fields, coaxial cable enjoys excellent **EMI rejection** for its era.

**Characteristic Impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) \;\; \Omega$$

where $D$ = inner diameter of the outer conductor, $d$ = diameter of the inner conductor, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the dielectric.

**Propagation Velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Attenuation (per unit length):**

$$\alpha_{\text{dB/km}} = 4.343 \, \left( \frac{R_s}{2 Z_0} \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r}{\mu_r}} + \frac{G \cdot Z_0}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\mu_r}{\varepsilon_r}} \right)$$

where $R_s$ is the surface-resistance of the conductors and $G$ is the dielectric loss conductance.

### 2.2 Twisted-Pair Cable — Balanced Transmission

A **twisted pair** is two insulated copper conductors twisted together at a defined **lay length** (number of twists per metre). The twist is the genius: it ensures that any external EMI induces *equal and opposite* voltages on both wires, which **cancel at the differential receiver**.

**Categories** (per TIA-568 / IEC 61156):

- **Cat-3** → 16 MHz, 10BASE-T, voice.
- **Cat-5e** → 100 MHz, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T (limited).
- **Cat-6** → 250 MHz, 1000BASE-T, 10GBASE-T (up to 55 m).
- **Cat-6A** → 500 MHz, 10GBASE-T to 100 m.
- **Cat-7 / Class F** → 600 MHz, 10GBASE-T.
- **Cat-8 / Class II** → 2000 MHz, 25GBASE-T / 40GBASE-T (data-centre only, 30 m).

**Shielding Variants** (IEC naming):

- **U/UTP** — Unshielded / Unshielded Twisted Pair (basic Cat-5e).
- **F/UTP** — Overall foil shield (FTP) — common industrial.
- **S/UTP** — Overall braid shield.
- **S/FTP** — Overall braid + each pair foil-wrapped — highest noise rejection (Cat-7/8).

**Why the Twist Works:**

For a uniform external field, the induced voltage in one half-twist is **opposite** to the next half-twist. After $N$ full twists, the net induced differential voltage is ideally zero.

$$V_{\text{diff,induced}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cdot v_k \approx 0$$

### 2.3 Fibre-Optic Cable — Optical Waveguide

A **fibre-optic strand** has three parts:

- **Core** (8–10 µm for SMF, 50 or 62.5 µm for MMF).
- **Cladding** (125 µm outer diameter always).
- **Buffer / jacket** (acrylic, Kevlar strength members, outer PE/PUR sheath).

Light is guided by **total internal reflection (TIR)**. The condition for TIR is:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_{\text{clad}}}{n_{\text{core}}}$$

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** defines the cone of acceptance:

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_{\text{core}}^2 - n_{\text{clad}}^2} = n_0 \sin \theta_a$$

where $\theta_a$ is the acceptance half-angle. For typical MMF: $n_{\text{core}} = 1.48$, $n_{\text{clad}} = 1.46$, so $\text{NA} \approx 0.24$, $\theta_a \approx 13.9^\circ$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\alpha_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$ | Power loss | dB |
| 2 | $\alpha_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{in}} / V_{\text{out}})$ | Voltage loss | dB |
| 3 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L \mid C}$ | Characteristic impedance (TP) | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | $v_p = 1 \mid \sqrt{LC}$ | Propagation velocity (TP) | m/s |
| 5 | $t_{\text{prop}} = L \mid v_p$ | One-way propagation delay | s |
| 6 | $\text{NVP} = v_p \mid c$ | Nominal Velocity of Propagation | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{BER} = P(\text{bit error})$ | Bit-Error Rate | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s \mid P_n)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $L_{\max} = \text{Loss Budget} \mid \alpha_{\text{dB/km}}$ | Max optical length | km |
| 10 | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Fibre numerical aperture | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Delay Skew} = \lvert t_{\text{fast}} - t_{\text{slow}} \rvert$ | Intra-pair delay | ns |
| 12 | $\text{NEXT}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{test}} \mid V_{\text{crosstalk}})$ | Near-End Crosstalk | dB |
| 13 | $\text{Attenuation-to-Crosstalk Ratio} = \text{NEXT}_{\text{dB}} - \alpha_{\text{dB}}$ | ACR (headroom) | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Industrial Ethernet protocols (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT) demand **Cat-5e or higher** with **≤ 100 m segments**. **PROFINET** specifically defines three conductor types: *Type A* (standard), *Type B* (flexible), *Type C* (torsion-resistant for robot tracks), each with tested **≥ 1 million bending cycles**. Choosing the wrong cable is the #1 cause of intermittent fieldbus failure in factory audits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Permissible Cable Length from Power-Budget Equation

In any optical or copper link, the **power budget** sets the upper limit on segment length.

**Given (typical 100BASE-FX MMF link):**

- Transmitter launch power: $P_T = -19 \text{ dBm}$
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_R = -31 \text{ dBm}$
- Connector loss: $L_C = 1.0 \text{ dB}$ (×2 connectors)
- Splice/margin allowance: $L_M = 1.5 \text{ dB}$
- Fibre attenuation: $\alpha = 1.0 \text{ dB/km}$ @ 1300 nm
- LED bandwidth–distance penalty for OM1: $P_{BD} = 0 \text{ dB}$ (at 100 Mb/s)

**Step 1 — Total Loss Budget:**

$$\text{Loss Budget} = P_T - P_R = (-19) - (-31) = 12 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract fixed losses:**

$$\text{Budget for fibre} = 12 - (2 \times 1.0) - 1.5 = 12 - 2.0 - 1.5 = 8.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for maximum length:**

$$L_{\max} = \frac{\text{Budget for fibre}}{\alpha} = \frac{8.5 \text{ dB}}{1.0 \text{ dB/km}} = 8.5 \text{ km}$$

> ⚠️ However, the **bandwidth–distance product** of OM1 MMF at 1300 nm is **500 MHz·km**. For 100 Mb/s NRZ encoding, the required bandwidth is **≈ 100 MHz**. Therefore:

$$L_{\text{disp-limited}} = \frac{500 \text{ MHz·km}}{100 \text{ MHz}} = 5.0 \text{ km}$$

The **effective** maximum length is the **smaller** of the two:

$$L_{\text{eff}} = \min(L_{\text{attenuation}}, L_{\text{dispersion}}) = \min(8.5,\ 5.0) = 5.0 \text{ km}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Attenuation to Crosstalk Ratio (ACR) of Cat-6 Channel

**ACR** is the engineering *headroom* between the wanted signal (after attenuation) and the unwanted crosstalk noise.

**Given (Cat-6, 100 m, at 100 MHz):**

- Insertion loss: $\alpha = 19.8 \text{ dB}$
- NEXT (worst pair): $\text{NEXT} = 44.3 \text{ dB}$

**Step 1 — Attenuation in dB (recall: $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage):**

$$\alpha_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{out}}}\right) = 19.8 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ACR:**

$$\text{ACR} = \text{NEXT}_{\text{dB}} - \alpha_{\text{dB}} = 44.3 - 19.8 = 24.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Margin check against 10GBASE-T requirement:**

For 10GBASE-T over Cat-6 (55 m limit), the standard mandates **ACR ≥ 4 dB** with **SNR margin ≥ 2 dB** after equalization. We have **24.5 dB** raw, so even after 12 dB of equalization loss there is comfortable headroom at 55 m.

> **Interpretation:** A larger positive ACR means the received signal sits well *above* the noise floor — the link is robust. A negative or near-zero ACR (typical at the high-frequency edge of a cable category) means equalization may fail and the link drops packets.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Propagation Delay in a Twisted-Pair Cable

**Given (Cat-5e, 100 m, typical):**

- NVP (Nominal Velocity of Propagation) = 0.66
- $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Compute physical propagation velocity:**

$$v_p = \text{NVP} \times c = 0.66 \times 3 \times 10^8 = 1.98 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute one-way delay:**

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{L}{v_p} = \frac{100}{1.98 \times 10^8} = 5.05 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 505 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Round-trip / collision-window delay (CSMA/CD, legacy 100BASE-TX, 5-bit collision window):**

$$t_{\text{RT}} = 2 \times 505 \text{ ns} = 1010 \text{ ns} = 1.01 \;\mu\text{s}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cable Selection by Industrial Scenario

| Scenario | Required Property | Recommended Cable | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|--------|
| PLC ↔ Drive, 20 m, motor VFD noise | High EMI immunity, flexible | **S/FTP Cat-6A PUR jacket** | Common-mode noise rejection |
| Field junction box to cabinet, 80 m | Standard Ethernet | **F/UTP Cat-5e LSZH** | Cost-effective, fire-safe |
| Inter-building, 500 m, lightning zone | Galvanic isolation, long reach | **SMF OS2 single-mode** | No ground loops, immune to surges |
| Robot track, 5 m, continuous flex | Torsion endurance, oil resistance | **PROFINET Type C Cat-5e** | Tested 1M bend cycles |
| Hazardous area (Ex zone 1) | No spark risk | **Fibre-optic MMF OM1** | Non-conductive |
| Legacy 4–20 mA + HART, 200 m | Analogue integrity, shielded | **2-conductor twisted shielded (Beldon 8760)** | Low noise, HART frequency pass |
| Backbone between control rooms, 1.5 km | High bandwidth, EMI free | **MMF OM3 10GBASE-SR** | 10 Gbps, 300 m; OM3 supports 1.5 km at 1 Gbps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb used by KTU examiners:** The 100-metre UTP limit comes from IEEE 802.3 timing constraints — it is **not** an electrical or attenuation limit. Copper can carry 1 Gbps further, but the **round-trip delay** of CSMA/CD (and now frame-burst timing in full-duplex) would violate the standard. Going beyond 100 m requires a switch, repeater, or fibre.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cable Classification Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL MEDIA L1] --> B[COPPER MEDIA]
    A --> C[OPTICAL MEDIA]
    A --> D[SPECIALISED INDUSTRIAL]
    B --> B1[COAXIAL]
    B --> B2[TWISTED PAIR]
    C --> C1[MULTI MODE FIBRE]
    C --> C2[SINGLE MODE FIBRE]
    D --> D1[PROFIBUS DP CABLE]
    D --> D2[FOUNDATION FIELDBUS]
    D --> D3[HYBRID POWER DATA]
    B1 --> B1A[RG 58 THINNET 50 OHM]
    B1 --> B1B[RG 6 THICKNET 75 OHM]
    B2 --> B2A[UTP CAT 5E]
    B2 --> B2B[FTP CAT 6]
    B2 --> B2C[SFTP CAT 7]
    B2 --> B2D[UFTP CAT 8]
    C1 --> C1A[OM1 62.5 UM]
    C1 --> C1B[OM2 50 UM]
    C1 --> C1C[OM3 50 UM LASER OPT]
    C1 --> C1D[OM4 50 UM]
    C2 --> C2A[OS1 BENDING INSENSITIVE]
    C2 --> C2B[OS2 LONG HAUL]
    D1 --> D1A[PROFIBUS PUR 2 WIRE]
    D2 --> D2A[IEC 61158 2 WIRE SHIELDED]
    D3 --> D3A[COMPOSITE POWER AND ETHERNET]
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Functional Architecture — Copper vs Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[TX DEVICE] -->|ELECTRICAL| X1[DIFFERENTIAL DRIVER]
    X1 -->|BALANCED PAIR| X2[TWISTED PAIR CHANNEL 100 M]
    X2 --> X3[DIFFERENTIAL RECEIVER]
    X3 --> RX[RX DEVICE]
    X2 -.EMI COUPLING.-> X4[NOISE]
    X3 -.CMRR REJECTS.-> X4
    TX2[TX OPTICAL] --> X5[LASER OR LED SOURCE]
    X5 -->|1300 OR 1550 NM| X6[FIBRE CORE 9 OR 50 UM]
    X6 -->|TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION| X7[PHOTODETECTOR]
    X7 --> RX2[RX OPTICAL]
    X6 -.IMMUNE.-> X8[EMI AND GROUND LOOPS]
```

### 4.3 Cable-Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START] --> Q1{DISTANCE LESS THAN 100 M}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{HIGH EMI NEAR VFD OR WELDING}
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3[USE FIBRE OPTIC]
    Q2 -->|YES| R1[SFTP CAT 6A WITH PUR JACKET]
    Q2 -->|NO| R2[UTP CAT 5E OR CAT 6 LSZH]
    Q3 --> Q4{BANDWIDTH NEEDED}
    Q4 -->|1 GBPS| R3[MMF OM2 OR OM3]
    Q4 -->|10 GBPS OR DISTANCE 300 M| R4[MMF OM3 OR OM4]
    Q4 -->|10 GBPS OVER 500 M| R5[SMF OS2]
    R1 --> END[SELECTED CABLE]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
    R5 --> END
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Twisted-Pair S/FTP Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[OUTER JACKET PUR OR LSZH] --> P1[OVERALL BRAID SHIELD]
    P1 --> P2[OVERALL FOIL SHIELD]
    P2 --> P3[FOIL WRAP PAIR 1]
    P2 --> P4[FOIL WRAP PAIR 2]
    P2 --> P5[FOIL WRAP PAIR 3]
    P2 --> P6[FOIL WRAP PAIR 4]
    P3 --> P31[CONDUCTOR A SOLID 24 AWG]
    P3 --> P32[CONDUCTOR B STRANDED 24 AWG]
    P4 --> P41[CONDUCTOR A 24 AWG]
    P4 --> P42[CONDUCTOR B 24 AWG]
    P5 --> P51[CONDUCTOR A 24 AWG]
    P5 --> P52[CONDUCTOR B 24 AWG]
    P6 --> P61[CONDUCTOR A 24 AWG]
    P6 --> P62[CONDUCTOR B 24 AWG]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Characteristic Impedance" of a transmission line. State its value for the Ethernet twisted pair and for the 10BASE-2 coaxial cable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is the ratio of voltage to current for a wave propagating along an infinitely long, lossless transmission line. It is determined solely by the geometry and dielectric of the cable and is independent of length. For **Ethernet twisted pair (100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T)**, $Z_0 = 100 \; \Omega$. For **10BASE-2 thin coaxial cable**, $Z_0 = 50 \; \Omega$. Matching the source and load impedances to $Z_0$ prevents reflections and standing waves.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between UTP, FTP, STP and S/FTP cables. In which industrial scenario would you prefer S/FTP over plain UTP?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Construction | Industrial Suitability |
|------|--------------|------------------------|
| **UTP** | Unshielded pairs | Office, light-industrial |
| **FTP (F/UTP)** | Overall foil shield | Moderate-noise zones |
| **STP (S/UTP)** | Overall braid shield | Higher mechanical robustness |
| **S/FTP** | Overall braid + each pair foil | Severe EMI, near VFD/welding |
