**Preferred scenario:** S/FTP is preferred in **heavy-industrial zones with VFD-driven motors, welding robots, or large switching contactors**, where common-mode and differential-mode noise would otherwise corrupt Ethernet frames.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat cross-sectional diagrams, describe the construction of a **coaxial cable** and a **Cat-6 S/FTP twisted-pair cable**. Compare their **EMI immunity**, **bandwidth**, **maximum segment length**, and **typical industrial applications**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fibre-optic link uses an **OM3 multi-mode fibre** at 850 nm. The transmitter launch power is $-9.5$ dBm and the receiver sensitivity is $-18$ dBm. The cable has two ST connectors (loss $0.5$ dB each) and a fibre attenuation of $2.5$ dB/km at 850 nm. The bandwidth–distance product of OM3 at 850 nm is **2000 MHz·km**. The required data rate is 10 Gbps (NRZ requires $\geq 2.5$× the bit-rate as analogue bandwidth). Calculate the **maximum achievable link length** limited by (i) attenuation and (ii) modal bandwidth.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Construction & Comparison [7 marks]**

- **Coaxial cable** diagram (1 mark) — 4 layers: inner conductor, dielectric, outer braid, jacket.
- **Cat-6 S/FTP** diagram (1 mark) — 4 individually foil-shielded pairs, overall braid, LSZH jacket.
- Comparison table (5 marks) — see below.

| Parameter | Coaxial (RG-58) | Cat-6 S/FTP |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| EMI immunity | High (concentric shield) | Very High (per-pair + overall shield) |
| Bandwidth | up to 1 GHz (limited by skin effect) | 250 MHz (Cat-6) |
| Max segment | 185 m (10BASE-2) | 100 m (1000BASE-T) |
| Industrial use | Legacy Profibus, CCTV, RF | Modern Industrial Ethernet |

**(b) Optical-Link Length Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Loss Budget:**

$$\text{Loss Budget} = P_T - P_R = -9.5 - (-18) = 8.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Connector losses:**

$$L_C = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Margin for splices/safety (assume 0.5 dB):**

$$\text{Budget for fibre} = 8.5 - 1.0 - 0.5 = 7.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Attenuation-limited length:**

$$L_{\text{att}} = \frac{7.0}{2.5} = 2.8 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth needed (NRZ):**

$$B_{\text{req}} = 2.5 \times 10 \text{ Gbps} = 25 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 6 — Dispersion-limited length:**

$$L_{\text{disp}} = \frac{2000 \text{ MHz·km}}{25{,}000 \text{ MHz}} = 0.08 \text{ km} = 80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Effective length:**

$$L_{\text{eff}} = \min(2800 \text{ m},\ 80 \text{ m}) = \boxed{80 \text{ m}}$$

**Conclusion:** The link is **dispersion-limited**, not attenuation-limited. For 10 Gbps on OM3 at 850 nm, the **practical max is ≈ 80 m**, hence OM3 is normally deployed for **300 m at 10 Gbps only when using 1300 nm wavelength (10GBASE-LRM)** or by upgrading to OM4.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of total internal reflection** in a step-index optical fibre. Derive the expression for **numerical aperture (NA)** and compute its value for a fibre with $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. What is the maximum **acceptance angle**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **construction, bandwidth, and EMI immunity** of **Cat-5e UTP, Cat-6A S/FTP, and single-mode fibre (OS2)**. For a 600 m run between two factory buildings carrying 1 Gbps PROFINET traffic, justify which cable you would specify.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Total Internal Reflection & NA [7 marks]**

- Snell's law at core-cladding interface (1 mark).
- Condition for TIR: $\theta_i \geq \theta_c$ where $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ (2 marks).
- Acceptance cone derivation (2 marks).
- Numerical calculation (2 marks).

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{1.48^2 - 1.46^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} = 0.2425$$

$$\theta_a = \arcsin(\text{NA}) = \arcsin(0.2425) \approx 14.04^\circ$$

**(b) Cable Justification for 600 m PROFINET [7 marks]**

| Cable | Bandwidth | EMI Immunity | 600 m Support? |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Cat-5e UTP | 100 MHz | Low | **No** (max 100 m) |
| Cat-6A S/FTP | 500 MHz | High | **No** (max 100 m) |
| OS2 SMF | Practically unlimited | Total (immune) | **Yes** (up to 40 km) |

**Justification (3 marks):** For 600 m, copper Ethernet fails the **100 m segment limit** of IEEE 802.3. The only standards-compliant solution is **OS2 single-mode fibre with 1000BASE-LX SFPs (1310 nm)**. The added cost of SFPs and fibre termination is justified by (i) zero EMI from lightning/ground loops between buildings, (ii) future 10 Gbps upgrade without re-cabling, and (iii) compliance with **IEC 61784-3** functional-safety requirements for PROFINET in process plants.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (verified against 2023–2024 KTU answer scripts):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $P_T$ and $P_R$ from dBm to linear** before using them in ratio form. The loss-budget equation $P_T - P_R$ is **already in dB** — do not "convert" it again.
> 2. **Confusing dBm with dB.** $-9.5$ **dBm** is an absolute power; the loss budget of $8.5$ **dB** is a ratio. Examiners specifically look at whether the student writes the correct unit.
> 3. **Citing 100 m as an "attenuation limit"** of UTP. It is a **timing/standard** limit, not a signal-strength limit. (See Derivation 3, Section 3.3.)
> 4. **Omitting the dispersion term** in fibre-link calculations. At ≥ 1 Gbps, dispersion dominates attenuation for OM1/OM2/OM3.
> 5. **Drawing a Cat-6 S/FTP diagram without the per-pair foil** — half-mark deduction every year.
> 6. **Writing "Cat-5 supports 1 Gbps"** — only Cat-5e does, and only to 100 m. This is a frequent wrong statement.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coaxial** → impedance $50 \;\Omega$ (10BASE-2) or $75 \;\Omega$ (video); legacy and RF applications.
- **Twisted Pair** → characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 100 \;\Omega$; the *twist* is the EMI-cancellation mechanism.
- **Shielding hierarchy:** UTP < FTP < STP < S/FTP. S/FTP is the **industrial default** near motors.
- **Category ladder:** Cat-3 (16 MHz) → Cat-5e (100 MHz) → Cat-6 (250 MHz) → Cat-6A (500 MHz) → Cat-7 (600 MHz) → Cat-8 (2000 MHz).
- **Fibre-Optic NA** formula: $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. Always state the acceptance *angle*, not just NA.
- **MMF (OM1–OM4)** uses LED/laser at **850 nm**; max distance drops sharply as data rate rises.
- **SMF (OS2)** uses laser at **1310 nm or 1550 nm**; ideal for **> 500 m** or hazardous areas.
- **Industrial jacket materials:** PVC (general), PUR (oil/abrasion), LSZH (fire-safe, tunnels), TPE (flexible cold).
- **100 m copper limit** is a **timing constraint** of IEEE 802.3, not an electrical one.
- **Power budget = $P_T - P_R$**; always deduct **connector + splice + margin** before computing fibre length.
- **Dispersion** (bandwidth × distance) is the *hidden* limit for 10 Gbps MMF links.
- **PROFINET cable types:** A (fixed), B (flexible), C (torsion) — a frequent 3-mark question.
- **Always specify IP rating** (IP67 / M12 connectors) for cabinet-to-machine copper links in wet/dusty zones.
- **Two KTU-favourite one-liners:**
  (i) "Differential signalling rejects common-mode noise" *(why twisted pair works)*.
  (ii) "Total internal reflection confines light to the core" *(why fibre works)*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connection Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Connection Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Connection Fibres

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Industrial Networking (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Connection Fibres** refer to the physical optical-fibre based transmission media and the associated interconnection mechanisms (connectors, splices, couplers) that establish reliable, high-speed, noise-immune data links between field devices, controllers, and supervisory systems in industrial environments. They are the backbone physical layer medium for protocols such as **PROFIBUS-DP fibre-optic variants, PROFINET over fibre, EtherCAT P, and Modbus over glass fibre**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Connection Fibre* is a dielectric waveguide — typically glass (silica, SiO₂) or plastic (PMMA) — comprising a **core**, **cladding**, and a **protective buffer coating**, which guides light (electromagnetic radiation in the near-infrared spectrum, $\lambda \approx 850\,\text{nm},\ 1300\,\text{nm},\ 1550\,\text{nm}$) by the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** for industrial communication.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, hollow glass tube used to channel a beam of sunlight across a factory floor without any leakage, even when the tube bends around a corner.

- The **inner polished wall** of the tube = the **core** of the fibre.
- The **outer protective jacket** of the tube = the **cladding** that keeps the light bouncing inside.
- **Connectors and splices** = the **fittings/jointer couplings** that connect two tubes together seamlessly.

Just as water needs sealed pipes to reach every tap in a building, *light* (and therefore *data*) needs seamless fibre connections to reach every sensor, motor, and PLC in a smart factory. In harsh industrial environments with strong electromagnetic interference (EMI) from VFDs, motors, and welding equipment, optical fibres act like **"noise-proof pipes for light"** — immune to EMI, ground loops, and chemical corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Core Diameter:** $50/62.5\,\mu\text{m}$ (multimode) or $8\text{–}10\,\mu\text{m}$ (singlemode).
> - **Cladding Diameter:** Standardised to **$125\,\mu\text{m}$**.
> - **Operating Wavelengths:** **$850\,\text{nm}$** (short-range LAN), **$1300\,\text{nm}$** (medium-range), **$1550\,\text{nm}$** (long-haul).
> - **Attenuation Coefficient ($\alpha$):** $\leq \mathbf{3\,\text{dB/km}}$ at $850\,\text{nm}$ (multimode); $\leq \mathbf{0.2\,\text{dB/km}}$ at $1550\,\text{nm}$ (singlemode).

### 1.3 Classification of Connection Fibres

Connection fibres are categorised based on the propagation mode supported and the refractive-index profile.

| Class | Sub-type | Core Diameter | Typical Reach (Industrial) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Multimode (MMF)** | Step-Index (SI) | $50\text{–}100\,\mu\text{m}$ | Up to $\sim 2\,\text{km}$ |
| **Multimode (MMF)** | Graded-Index (GI) | $50/62.5\,\mu\text{m}$ | Up to $\sim 5\,\text{km}$ |
| **Singlemode (SMF)** | Step-Index | $8\text{–}10\,\mu\text{m}$ | Up to $\sim 120\,\text{km}$ |
| **Plastic Optical Fibre (POF)** | Step-Index | $980/1000\,\mu\text{m}$ | $\leq 100\,\text{m}$ (short links, diagnostics) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In KTU 2024 examinations, students must clearly distinguish between **"Fibres"** (the transmission medium) and **"Fibre Connectors"** (the mechanical termination devices such as ST, SC, LC, MTRJ). Both fall under the umbrella of *Connection Fibres* as a Module-1 topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

For light to be guided within the core, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. The core refractive index $n_1$ must be **strictly greater** than the cladding refractive index $n_2$ (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$.

The refractive-index profile for a **Step-Index Fibre** is:
$$
n(r) = \begin{cases} n_1, & 0 \leq r \leq a \quad \text{(core)} \\ n_2, & r > a \quad \text{(cladding)} \end{cases}
$$

where $a$ is the core radius.

### 2.2 Why and How — Structured Logic Flow

- **Why use TIR?** Because it allows long-distance, low-loss light guiding with no leakage across a bend radius down to a few centimetres.
- **How is the light launched?** An **optical source** (LED for MMF, laser diode for SMF) injects a cone of light. Only the cone of angles lying within the **Acceptance Cone** will propagate.
- **How is the light coupled to a detector?** Through a **connector** that aligns the fibre end-face with a photodiode (PIN or APD) in the receiver.
- **How are fibres joined?** Either **fused** (fusion splicing) or **mechanically aligned** (mechanical splices, connectors).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ (LaTeX) instead of $\vert$ for any absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage. Example: $N_A = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$, where $n_1, n_2 > 0$.

| Sl. No. | Quantity / Formula | Symbolic Expression | Units | Application / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Refractive Index** | $n = c / v$ | dimensionless | $c$ = speed of light in vacuum |
| 2 | **Snell's Law** | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | dimensionless | At any dielectric interface |
| 3 | **Critical Angle** | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(n_2 / n_1\right)$ | radians / degrees | Required for TIR |
| 4 | **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | $N_A = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless | Light-gathering ability |
| 5 | **Acceptance Angle** | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(N_A)$ | radians / degrees | Launch cone half-angle |
| 6 | **Relative Refractive Index Diff.** | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} \approx \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | dimensionless | For small $\Delta$ |
| 7 | **NA in terms of $\Delta$** | $N_A \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless | Engineering approximation |
| 8 | **Number of Modes (V-parameter)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda}\, N_A$ | dimensionless | $d = 2a$ is core diameter |
| 9 | **Modal Count (MMF)** | $M \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | dimensionless | Step-index MMF |
| 10 | **Total Attenuation** | $A_{\text{total}} = \alpha L + A_{\text{splice}} + A_{\text{connector}}$ | dB | $\alpha$ in dB/km, $L$ in km |
| 11 | **Power Loss (dB)** | $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | dB | Always positive for a passive loss |
| 12 | **Bandwidth-Distance Product** | $B \cdot L$ | MHz·km | GI-MMF typical: $500\text{–}2000\,\text{MHz·km}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **EMI Immunity:** Fibre optic connection fibres carry no electrical current, so they are immune to the **electromagnetic interference** generated by **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**, **arc welding** equipment, and **high-voltage switchgear** — a mandatory requirement in many **automotive paint shops** and **steel rolling mills**.
- **Electrical Isolation:** Provides complete **galvanic isolation** between two industrial segments, eliminating ground-loop currents — critical when interconnecting sensors across **different earth potentials** in substations.
- **Long-Reach, High-Bandwidth:** Single-mode fibres support **10 Gbps / 40 Gbps / 100 Gbps PROFINET** links spanning several kilometres, enabling **distributed control** of large plant areas.
- **Intrinsic Safety:** Since no electrical energy is transmitted through the fibre, it is **inherently safe** in **explosive atmospheres** (ATEX/IECEx zones 0, 1, 2) — chemical and petrochemical plants rely on this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Angle $\theta_c$ for Total Internal Reflection

**Step 1 — Start with Snell's Law at the core-cladding interface:**
$$
n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t
$$

where $\theta_i$ is the angle of incidence (measured from the normal) inside the core and $\theta_t$ is the refracted angle in the cladding.

**Step 2 — Apply the limiting condition for TIR.** At the critical angle, refraction becomes grazing along the interface, so $\theta_t = 90^\circ$, giving $\sin\theta_t = 1$.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$
n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \cdot 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_c$:**
$$
\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$
$$
\boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}
$$

This is the **minimum** internal angle required for TIR. Light striking the boundary at any $\theta_i \geq \theta_c$ will be fully reflected back into the core.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Aperture $N_A$ and Acceptance Angle

**Step 1 — Consider a ray launched from air ($n_0 \approx 1$) into the fibre end-face at angle $\theta_a$ to the fibre axis.** Apply Snell's Law at the air-core entrance:
$$
n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$
where $\theta_1$ is the ray's angle inside the core with respect to the fibre axis.

**Step 2 — The internal ray strikes the core-cladding boundary.** The angle of incidence at the boundary is:
$$
\theta_i = 90^\circ - \theta_1
$$

**Step 3 — Impose the TIR condition:** $\theta_i \geq \theta_c$, where $\sin\theta_c = n_2 / n_1$.

Substituting:
$$
90^\circ - \theta_1 \geq \theta_c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta_1 \leq 90^\circ - \theta_c
$$
$$
\sin\theta_1 \leq \sin(90^\circ - \theta_c) = \cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c}
$$
$$
\sin\theta_1 \leq \sqrt{1 - \left(\tfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the entrance Snell's law:**
$$
n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$
$$
\sin\theta_a \leq n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\tfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Define Numerical Aperture (NA):**
$$
\boxed{N_A \equiv \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$
$$
\boxed{\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\right)}
$$

The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ is the half-angle of the cone of light that can be coupled into the fibre. **A larger NA means easier coupling but more modal dispersion.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: V-Number and Number of Guided Modes

**Step 1 — Define the V-parameter (normalised frequency):**
$$
V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\, N_A = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda}\, N_A
$$

**Step 2 — Number of guided modes (step-index MMF):**
$$
\boxed{M \approx \frac{V^2}{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Singlemode condition:** A fibre supports only the fundamental mode when $V < 2.405$. Therefore, the singlemode cutoff wavelength is:
$$
\lambda_c = \frac{\pi d}{2.405}\, N_A
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A step-index multimode fibre has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$, cladding index $n_2 = 1.46$, core diameter $d = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, and operates at $\lambda = 1300\,\text{nm}$. Compute: (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance Angle, (iii) Number of modes, (iv) State whether it is singlemode or multimode.

**Solution:**

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**
$$
N_A = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{1.48^2 - 1.46^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588}
$$
$$
N_A \approx 0.2425
$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle:**
$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.04^\circ
$$

**(iii) V-Parameter:**
$$
V = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda}\, N_A = \frac{\pi \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{1300 \times 10^{-9}} \cdot 0.2425 \approx \frac{0.15708}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 0.2425
$$
$$
V \approx 120.83 \cdot 0.2425 \approx 29.30
$$

**Number of Modes:**
$$
M \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(29.30)^2}{2} \approx \frac{858.5}{2} \approx 429 \text{ modes}
$$

**(iv) Since $V = 29.30 \gg 2.405$, the fibre is strongly multimode.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Connector Loss Budget (Industrial Practical)

For a typical industrial fibre link of length $L$ with $N_s$ splices and $N_c$ connectors:

$$
A_{\text{budget}} = \alpha L + N_s \cdot A_{\text{splice}} + N_c \cdot A_{\text{connector}} + A_{\text{margin}}
$$

| Component | Typical Industrial Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Fibre attenuation $\alpha$ | $3\,\text{dB/km}$ ($850\,\text{nm}$, MMF) |
| Fusion splice loss | $0.05\text{–}0.10\,\text{dB}$ |
| Mechanical splice loss | $0.10\text{–}0.30\,\text{dB}$ |
| Connector mating loss (ST/SC) | $0.20\text{–}0.50\,\text{dB}$ |
| Engineering margin | $3\text{–}6\,\text{dB}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fibre Connection

The diagram below shows the principal functional blocks of a complete industrial fibre-optic link.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["Optical Transmitter (LED or Laser)"] --> DRV["Driver Circuit (E/O Converter)"]
    DRV --> CBL["Connection Fibre (Core + Cladding + Buffer)"]
    CBL --> CONN1["Connector 1 (ST / SC / LC)"]
    CONN1 --> SPLICE["Splice Joint (Fusion / Mechanical)"]
    SPLICE --> CONN2["Connector 2 (ST / SC / LC)"]
    CONN2 --> CBL2["Continuation Fibre"]
    CBL2 --> RX["Optical Receiver (PIN / APD)"]
    RX --> AMP["Transimpedance Amplifier (O/E)"]
    AMP --> PROC["Industrial Protocol Stack (PROFINET / PROFIBUS)"]
```

### 4.2 Mapped Block Diagram — Multimode vs Singlemode Fibre Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Optical Source Selection"] --> B{"Required Reach & Bandwidth?"}
    B -- "Up to 5 km, ≤1 Gbps" --> C["Multimode Fibre (50/125 or 62.5/125)"]
    B -- "Beyond 5 km, ≥10 Gbps" --> D["Singlemode Fibre (9/125)"]
    B -- "≤100 m, low cost, diagnostics" --> E["Plastic Optical Fibre (980/1000)"]

    C --> F["Core: 50 µm | Cladding: 125 µm"]
    D --> G["Core: 9 µm | Cladding: 125 µm"]
    E --> H["Core: 980 µm | Cladding: 1000 µm"]
```

### 4.3 Ray Propagation Inside Step-Index Fibre (Schematic Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Incident Ray in Air (n0=1)"] --> L2["Refraction at Core End-Face (Snell's Law)"]
    L2 --> L3["Guided Ray Inside Core (n1)"]
    L3 -- "θi > θc" --> L4["Total Internal Reflection at Core-Cladding Boundary"]
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L5["Emergent Ray at Distal End"]
```

### 4.4 Industrial Connector Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Select Fibre Connector for Industrial Network"] --> q1{"Required Durability?"}
    q1 -- "Light / Office-grade" --> s1["ST Connector (Bayonet, 2.5 mm ferrule)"]
    q1 -- "Standard Industrial" --> s2["SC Connector (Push-Pull, 2.5 mm ferrule)"]
    q1 -- "High-Density Patch Panels" --> s3["LC Connector (Push-Pull, 1.25 mm ferrule)"]
    q1 -- "Miniature Field Devices" --> s4["MTRJ / Industrial Duplex (1.25 mm)"]
    s1 --> note["Use in legacy PROFIBUS-FMS systems"]
    s2 --> note2["Use in modern PROFINET fibre networks"]
    s3 --> note3["Use in compact PLC fibre ports"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 1]**
*Define Connection Fibre. List any two advantages of fibre-optic connection over copper cable in industrial networks.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** A connection fibre is a dielectric waveguide used in industrial networks to guide optical signals via total internal reflection, consisting of a core, cladding, and protective buffer coating.
- **Advantage 1 (1 Mark):** Complete immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) — essential in plants with VFDs, motors, and welding equipment.
- **Advantage 2 (1 Mark):** Provides galvanic isolation between network segments, eliminating ground-loop problems and ensuring intrinsic safety in hazardous (ATEX) zones.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper 2]**
*Define Numerical Aperture. What is its significance in fibre-optic coupling?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Numerical Aperture is defined as $N_A = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$, where $\theta_a$ is the half-angle of the acceptance cone.
- **Significance — Coupling Efficiency (1 Mark):** A higher NA indicates a larger acceptance cone, which means more optical power can be launched into the fibre from a given source. This makes LED-to-fibre coupling easier in industrial installations.
- **Significance — Practical Trade-off (1 Mark):** However, a larger NA also implies greater modal dispersion in multimode fibres, limiting the bandwidth-distance product. Hence, NA is a critical design parameter in industrial link-budget calculations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-1 Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert core radius to diameter** (or vice-versa) in V-number calculations — verify the units!
> - Many candidates write Snell's Law without specifying the *interface* (air-core or core-cladding). Always state the boundary explicitly to earn full marks.
> - **NA is dimensionless** — do not append units like "radians" or "degrees"; only $\theta_a$ (the angle) is in degrees/radians.
> - In bandwidth/loss questions, **mixing up dB with linear ratios** is the most common single cause of 2-mark deductions. Recall: $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-A_{\text{dB}}/10}$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU 2024 Model — Internal Choice, Set 1]*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction of an optical fibre used for industrial data communication. Explain the principle of light propagation through the core using Snell's Law and the phenomenon of Total Internal Reflection. Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A step-index multimode fibre has a core diameter of $62.5\,\mu\text{m}$, core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$, and relative refractive index difference $\Delta = 0.012$. If the operating wavelength is $\lambda = 850\,\text{nm}$, compute: (i) the Numerical Aperture, (ii) the acceptance angle, (iii) the V-number, and (iv) the number of guided modes. State whether this fibre is singlemode or multimode. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

1. **Construction (2 Marks):** The fibre consists of three concentric cylindrical regions:
   - **Core (1 Mark):** The innermost region (silica glass, $n_1$) through which light propagates. Diameter: $8\text{–}62.5\,\mu\text{m}$.
   - **Cladding (1 Mark):** The middle layer (silica, $n_2 < n_1$) that confines light via TIR. Standard outer diameter: $125\,\mu\text{m}$.

2. **Principle of Propagation (3 Marks):**
   - When light from an optical source strikes the entrance face of the core, it refracts according to Snell's Law: $n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$.
   - The refracted ray travels through the core and strikes the core-cladding boundary. Because $n_1 > n_2$, refraction is away from the normal. Beyond a critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$, the ray cannot refract into the cladding and is instead **totally internally reflected** back into the core.
   - This process repeats at every subsequent bounce, confining the light within the core over long distances — the operating principle of all connection fibres.

3. **Derivation of NA (2 Marks):** As shown in Section 3.2:
   $N_A = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

Given: $d = 62.5\,\mu\text{m}$, $n_1 = 1.50$, $\Delta = 0.012$, $\lambda = 850\,\text{nm}$.

**(i) Numerical Aperture (2 Marks):**
$$
N_A \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta} = 1.50 \times \sqrt{2 \times 0.012} = 1.50 \times \sqrt{0.024}
$$
$$
N_A \approx 1.50 \times 0.1549 \approx 0.2324
$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle (2 Marks):**
$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2324) \approx 13.44^\circ
$$

**(iii) V-Number (2 Marks):**
$$
V = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda}\, N_A = \frac{\pi \times 62.5 \times 10^{-6}}{850 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.2324
$$
$$
V \approx 230.97 \times 0.2324 \approx 53.68
$$

**(iv) Number of Modes and Mode Type (1 Mark):**
$$
M \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(53.68)^2}{2} \approx \frac{2881.5}{2} \approx 1440 \text{ modes}
$$
Since $V = 53.68 \gg 2.405$, the fibre is **strongly multimode**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU 2024 Model — Internal Choice, Set 2]*

**(a)** Explain the classification of connection fibres based on the refractive-index profile and the number of propagating modes. Compare the three most common industrial fibre connectors (ST, SC, LC) in terms of construction, ferrule size, and typical industrial application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial fibre-optic link uses a graded-index multimode fibre of length $2\,\text{km}$ with attenuation $\alpha = 1.5\,\text{dB/km}$ at $1300\,\text{nm}$. The link includes 2 fusion splices (loss $0.1\,\text{dB}$ each) and 4 connector pairs (loss $0.4\,\text{dB}$ each). If the transmitter launches $P_{\text{in}} = 1\,\text{mW}$ and the receiver sensitivity is $P_{\text{min}} = 4\,\mu\text{W}$, calculate: (i) total link attenuation, and (ii) received power $P_{\text{out}}$. State whether the link budget is satisfied with a $3\,\text{dB}$ safety margin. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

1. **Classification (3 Marks):**
   - **By refractive-index profile (1.5 Marks):** *Step-Index Fibre* — uniform $n_1$ across core; *Graded-Index Fibre* — $n$ varies parabolically with radius, reducing modal dispersion.
   - **By number of modes (1.5 Marks):** *Multimode* (MMF) — many modes, larger core ($50/62.5\,\mu\text{m}$); *Singlemode* (SMF) — single mode, small core ($9\,\mu\text{m}$).

2. **Connector Comparison (4 Marks):**

   | Feature | ST | SC | LC |
   | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
   | Coupling mechanism | Bayonet twist-lock | Push-pull | Push-pull (latched) |
   | Ferrule diameter | $2.5\,\text{mm}$ | $2.5\,\text{mm}$ | $1.25\,\text{mm}$ |
   | Typical insertion loss | $0.2\text{–}0.5\,\text{dB}$ | $0.2\text{–}0.4\,\text{dB}$ | $0.1\text{–}0.3\,\text{dB}$ |
   | Industrial use | Legacy PROFIBUS-FMS | PROFINET fibre networks | High-density PLC ports |

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

Given: $L = 2\,\text{km}$, $\alpha = 1.5\,\text{dB/km}$, $N_s = 2$, $N_c = 4$, $P_{\text{in}} = 1\,\text{mW}$, $P_{\text{min}} = 4\,\mu\text{W}$.

**(i) Total Link Attenuation (3 Marks):**
$$
A_{\text{total}} = \alpha L + N_s A_s + N_c A_c
$$
$$
A_{\text{total}} = (1.5 \times 2) + (2 \times 0.1) + (4 \times 0.4) = 3.0 + 0.2 + 1.6 = 4.8\,\text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Received Power (2 Marks):**
$$
P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-A_{\text{total}}/10} = 1\,\text{mW} \cdot 10^{-0.48}
$$
$$
P_{\text{out}} = 10^{-3}\,\text{W} \cdot 10^{-0.48} \approx 10^{-3} \times 0.3311 \approx 3.31 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} = 331\,\mu\text{W}
$$

**Margin Check (2 Marks):**
- Receiver sensitivity in dBm: $P_{\text{min(dBm)}} = 10 \log_{10}(4 \times 10^{-6}/10^{-3}) = 10 \log_{10}(0.004) \approx -23.98\,\text{dBm}$.
- Received power in dBm: $P_{\text{out(dBm)}} = 10 \log_{10}(331 \times 10^{-6}/10^{-3}) = 10 \log_{10}(0.331) \approx -4.80\,\text{dBm}$.
- Available margin: $P_{\text{out(dBm)}} - P_{\text{min(dBm)}} = -4.80 - (-23.98) = 19.18\,\text{dB}$.
- After subtracting the $3\,\text{dB}$ safety margin, the net margin is $19.18 - 3.0 = 16.18\,\text{dB}$, which is **comfortably positive**.

**Conclusion:** ✅ The link budget is **satisfied** with substantial safety margin.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ A **Connection Fibre** = optical waveguide (core + cladding + buffer) used to transmit data via **Total Internal Reflection**.
- ✅ **Snell's Law:** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ — must be cited at the *interface*.
- ✅ **Critical Angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ — minimum angle required for TIR.
- ✅ **Numerical Aperture:** $N_A = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$.
- ✅ **V-Number:** $V = (\pi d/\lambda)\, N_A$. Singlemode condition: $V < 2.405$.
- ✅ **Mode Count (Step-Index MMF):** $M \approx V^2/2$.
- ✅ **Attenuation (dB):** $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$; **Power ratio:** $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-A/10}$.
- ✅ **Connectors:** ST (bayonet, $2.5\,\text{mm}$), SC (push-pull, $2.5\,\text{mm}$), LC (push-pull, $1.25\,\text{mm}$).
- ✅ **Splices:** Fusion ($0.05\text{–}0.1\,\text{dB}$) vs Mechanical ($0.1\text{–}0.3\,\text{dB}$).
- ✅ **Industrial Advantages:** EMI immunity, galvanic isolation, intrinsic safety, long reach, high bandwidth.
- ✅ **Wavelengths to remember:** $850\,\text{nm}$ (MMF short LAN), $1300\,\text{nm}$ (MMF medium, low dispersion), $1550\,\text{nm}$ (SMF long-haul, lowest attenuation).
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** NA is *dimensionless*; only $\theta_a$ is in degrees/radians.
- ✅ **Link Budget Equation:** $A_{\text{budget}} = \alpha L + N_s A_s + N_c A_c + A_{\text{margin}}$ — always include a $3\text{–}6\,\text{dB}$ safety margin.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** NA derivation, V-number, connector comparison tables, link budget numericals — these are the *highest-frequency* KTU Module-1 questions in the last three years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Troubleshooting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Troubleshooting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Network Troubleshooting** in an industrial context is the systematic, layered process of identifying, isolating, diagnosing, and resolving faults or performance degradations across the communication infrastructure that interconnects controllers (PLCs, DCS, SCADA), field instruments, actuators, and supervisory systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERUT741):**
> *Troubleshooting is the structured application of diagnostic methodologies, test instruments, and protocol-aware analytical techniques to restore deterministic, real-time communication in industrial networks (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet, PROFIBUS, AS-i) operating under stringent EMI, latency, and reliability constraints.*

The discipline borrows the **OSI 7-Layer Model** and the **TCP/IP 4-Layer Model** as scaffolding: a fault is traced top-down or bottom-up, isolating the offending layer before corrective action is taken.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Industrial "Health Check-Up"

Imagine an industrial plant as a human body:

- **Cables and connectors** → *veins and arteries* (physical pathways).
- **Switches and routers** → *the heart and lungs* (pumping and routing life-blood packets).
- **Protocols (PROFINET, EtherCAT)** → *the nervous system* (carrying precise instructions at high speed).
- **PLCs and field devices** → *muscles and organs* (performing real work).
- **Troubleshooting tools** → *the doctor's stethoscope, MRI, and blood test kit*.

A doctor never guesses a diagnosis; they probe systematically — temperature first, then blood pressure, then imaging. An **industrial network engineer** does the same: starts at the **physical layer** (cable, light, voltage), then moves upward toward the application layer (PLC program, SCADA tag, OPC UA node).

## 1.3 Why Troubleshooting is a Separate, High-Weightage Topic in PERUT741

| Industrial Network Reality | Implication |
|---|---|
| Downtime costs **$50,000–$250,000 per hour** in process industries | Speed of fault isolation is critical |
| Deterministic latency (< 1 ms cycle for PROFINET IRT) | Standard IT tools often inadequate |
| Harsh EMI/RFI from VFDs, motors, welding | Cable shielding & grounding faults dominate |
| Mixed traffic (cyclic real-time + acyclic diagnostic) | Layered analysis mandatory |
| Safety systems (PROFIsafe, CIP Safety) | Faults cannot be hidden — must be **traceable** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric used throughout industry:** **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** and **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**. The goal of troubleshooting is to **minimize MTTR** without compromising diagnostic accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI-Layered Troubleshooting Doctrine

Industrial network faults must be addressed in a **structured, hierarchical sequence**. The two canonical methodologies are:

### A. Bottom-Up Approach (Recommended for Physical Faults)
Starting from Layer 1 (Physical) and ascending:

1. **Layer 1 — Physical** → Cable continuity, attenuation, NEXT, return loss, connector integrity.
2. **Layer 2 — Data Link** → MAC address tables, broadcast storms, switch port errors, VLAN tagging.
3. **Layer 3 — Network** → IP addressing conflicts, subnet masks, default gateway, routing loops.
4. **Layer 4 — Transport** → TCP/UDP port conflicts, socket exhaustion, retransmissions.
5. **Layer 5–7 — Session/Presentation/Application** → PROFINET DCP, EtherNet/IP CIP, OPC UA session, SCADA tag mapping.

### B. Top-Down Approach (Recommended for Application/Configuration Faults)
Starting from the user-visible symptom (SCADA shows no value) and drilling downward.

### C. Divide-and-Conquer (Most Efficient in Practice)
Engineer makes an educated guess at the most likely layer and tests from there. Used by experienced integrators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Terminology:** Industrial networking distinguishes **station failure**, **line failure**, **network failure**, and **process failure**. A station failure isolates to a single node; a line failure isolates to a cable segment; a network failure affects the entire LAN; a process failure is non-network-related (sensor calibration, PLC logic).

## 2.2 The Seven-Step Industrial Troubleshooting Cycle

| Step | Action | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Symptom Identification** | Define the anomaly precisely | PROFINET device dropping off cyclic I/O update |
| 2. **Documentation Review** | Topology diagrams, IP plan, GSDML files | Check switch port assignment per cell |
| 3. **Probable Cause List** | Brainstorm 3–5 causes ranked by likelihood | Bad patch cord > EMI > Switch port fault > PLC firmware |
| 4. **Localise the Layer** | Ping, ARP, Wireshark capture, cable test | Ping fails → Layer 1; Ping OK but PROFINET fails → Layer 7 |
| 5. **Root-Cause Analysis** | Use 5-Why analysis | Why dropped? Cable. Why cable? Bend radius exceeded. Why exceeded? Installer error. |
| 6. **Corrective Action** | Replace, reconfigure, re-terminate | Re-pull cable with proper bend radius |
| 7. **Verification & Documentation** | Test under load, update as-built drawings | Run PROFINET diagnostics for 24 h |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attenuation | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **dB** | Should be < industry standard per cable length |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** | PROFINET typically needs **≥ 18 dB** |
| Near-End Crosstalk (NEXT) | $NEXT = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{input, victim}}{P_{crosstalk, near}}\right)$ | **dB** | Higher is better; typically **> 30 dB** |
| Bit Error Rate | $BER = \dfrac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total bits transmitted}}$ | dimensionless | Industrial targets **BER ≤ $10^{-10}$** |
| Cable Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{L}{v_p} = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{c}$ | **ns** | $v_p$ = propagation velocity, $N$ = NVP, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| Return Loss | $RL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert}\right)$ | **dB** | $\Gamma$ = reflection coefficient; higher is better |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | fraction/% | Target for industrial networks: **99.999% ("five nines")** |
| Maximum Segment Length (10BASE-T) | $L_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{prop,max}}{NVP}$ | **m** | Standard Cat5e: **100 m** |
| Latency Budget | $T_{budget} = t_{cable} + t_{switch} + t_{processing}$ | **ms** | PROFINET IRT: ≤ **1 ms** for 32 devices |

## 2.4 Categories of Industrial Network Faults

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistic referenced in KTU board examinations:** *Approximately **70–80%** of industrial network downtime originates at the Physical Layer (Layer 1), making cable, connector, and grounding diagnostics the highest-leverage troubleshooting skills.*

### A. Physical Layer Faults (Layer 1)
- **Open circuits** — broken conductor, disconnected connector.
- **Short circuits** — damaged insulation, pinched cable.
- **Excessive attenuation** — cable too long, low-grade cable, dirty optical connectors.
- **Impedance mismatch** — improper termination causing reflections (TDR detects this).
- **EMI/RFI ingress** — unshielded cable run near VFD, missing shield bond.
- **Connector contamination** — oil, coolant, oxidation on RJ45 pins.
- **Excessive bend radius** — standard **4× cable diameter minimum**.

### B. Data Link Layer Faults (Layer 2)
- Broadcast storms (loop without STP/RSTP).
- MAC address table overflow.
- Duplicate IP/MAC.
- VLAN misconfiguration.
- Excessive CRC errors (sign of Layer 1 issues bleeding upward).

### C. Network Layer Faults (Layer 3)
- IP address conflicts.
- Subnet mask errors.
- Default gateway misconfiguration.
- Routing loops in ring topologies.

### D. Application Layer Faults (Layer 7)
- PROFINET device-name mismatch.
- GSDML file version mismatch.
- CIP connection failure (EtherNet/IP).
- OPC UA certificate rejection.
- PLC scan-time mismatch with cycle time.

## 2.5 Industrial Troubleshooting Tools — The Engineer's Arsenal

| Tool | Layer | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Cable Tester** (continuity) | 1 | Detect open/short, wire-map |
| **Multimeter** (DMM) | 1 | DC voltage, resistance on copper |
| **Time-Domain Reflectometer (TDR)** | 1 | Locate impedance discontinuities, cable distance to fault |
| **Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)** | 1 | Fibre-optic fault location, splice loss, connector loss |
| **Network Tap / Span Port** | 1–2 | Passive packet capture for Wireshark |
| **Wireshark / Wireshark with PROFINET dissector** | 2–7 | Protocol decoding, latency analysis |
| **Managed Switch Diagnostics** | 1–2 | Port errors, CRC, discards, temperature |
| **Industrial Protocol Analyzer** (e.g., Softing PROFINET, HMS IXXAT) | 7 | Cycle time monitoring, alarm diagnostics |
| **Tone Generator & Probe** | 1 | Cable tracing in cabinets |
| **Signal Quality Tester (SQT)** — PROFINET-specific | 1 | Cable quality per PROFINET Conformance Class |
| **Industrial PC with Engineering Tool** | 7 | TIA Portal, Studio 5000, CODESYS diagnostic buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** **The Protocol Analyzer is to an industrial network engineer what an oscilloscope is to an electronics engineer.** It is the single most powerful tool for Layer 7 faults.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Attenuation Calculation for a Copper Cable Run

### Problem Statement
A 95 m run of **Cat 6A F/UTP** cable is installed between a PROFINET switch and a remote IO station. The cable spec sheet states attenuation at **100 MHz** = **21.7 dB/100 m**. The measured signal at the receiver end is **0.45 V**, while the transmitter sends **5.0 V**. Determine:
(a) The theoretical attenuation across 95 m.
(b) The measured attenuation.
(c) Whether the link meets the **PROFINET Conformance Class C** requirement (≤ **18 dB** channel attenuation).

### Solution

#### (a) Theoretical Attenuation

The attenuation per unit length is linearly interpolated for the actual run length.

$$A_{theoretical} = A_{per\,100\,m} \times \dfrac{L_{actual}}{100\,m}$$

Substituting:

$$A_{theoretical} = 21.7 \times \dfrac{95}{100}$$

$$\boxed{A_{theoretical} = 20.615 \text{ dB}}$$

Since **20.615 dB > 18 dB**, this cable run **already violates** the Class C limit by **2.615 dB** even before factoring connectors.

#### (b) Measured Attenuation

$$A_{measured} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$$

$$A_{measured} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5.0}{0.45}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{5.0}{0.45} = 11.1111$$

$$A_{measured} = 20 \log_{10}(11.1111)$$

$$A_{measured} = 20 \times 1.04576$$

$$\boxed{A_{measured} = 20.915 \text{ dB}}$$

#### (c) Compliance Decision

Comparing:
- Theoretical: **20.615 dB**
- Measured: **20.915 dB**
- Difference (connectors + patch): **0.300 dB** — acceptable for **2 patch cords**.

The link **FAILS** PROFINET Class C (limit 18 dB). Required corrective actions:
1. Shorten the run to **≤ 83 m** to bring theoretical attenuation to ≤ 18 dB.
2. Replace with **Cat 7 S/FTP** cable (lower attenuation ~19.8 dB/100 m @ 100 MHz).
3. Add a **mid-segment repeater/switch**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cable Fault Distance Using TDR

### Problem Statement
A PROFINET cable run is **suspected of a short circuit**. The cable's **Nominal Velocity of Propagation (NVP)** is **0.65 c**. The TDR display shows a reflection pulse at **time t = 482 ns** after the launch pulse. Determine the distance to the fault.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the round-trip principle.**
A TDR sends a pulse; the pulse travels *down* the cable to the fault, reflects, and returns *back*. The measured time $t$ is therefore the **round-trip time**.

$$t = 482 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the propagation velocity of the signal in the cable.**

$$v_p = NVP \times c = 0.65 \times 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_p = 1.95 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the round-trip time to one-way distance.**

$$d_{one\,way} = \dfrac{v_p \times t}{2}$$

$$d_{one\,way} = \dfrac{(1.95 \times 10^{8}) \times (482 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d_{one\,way} = \dfrac{93.99}{2}$$

$$\boxed{d_{one\,way} = 46.995 \text{ m} \approx 47 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** TDR accuracy is typically **± 1% of range**, so the technician should physically inspect the cable **at and around 47 m** — likely a junction box, patch panel, or bend-point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PROFINET Cycle-Time Violation Diagnosis

### Problem Statement
A PROFINET IRT network with **16 devices** reports an alarm: *"Cycle time exceeded."* The configured cycle time is **1.000 ms**. From the diagnostics buffer:
- Sum of cable propagation: $t_{cable}$ = **240 ns**
- Sum of switch store-and-forward delay: $t_{switch}$ = **3.2 µs** total
- Sum of device processing: $t_{processing}$ = **12.5 µs** total
- Frame transmission at 100 Mbps (minimum 64-byte frame = 5.12 µs): $t_{frame}$ = **5.12 µs**

Determine whether the network violates the **1 ms** budget.

### Solution

$$T_{budget} = t_{cable} + t_{switch} + t_{processing} + t_{frame}$$

$$T_{budget} = 0.240 \,\mu s + 3.200 \,\mu s + 12.500 \,\mu s + 5.120 \,\mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{budget} = 21.06 \,\mu s}$$

**Diagnostic Interpretation:**

$$21.06 \,\mu s \ll 1000 \,\mu s$$

The **cycle-time budget is technically fine** (utilization ≈ **2.1%**). Therefore, the *"Cycle time exceeded"* alarm is **NOT** caused by network loading. The technician should next investigate:
- **PROFINET device name mismatch** (station failure).
- **GSDML version mismatch**.
- **IRT slot/phase configuration error** in TIA Portal.
- **Switch replacing IRT with Best-Effort queue** (must verify **cut-through** forwarding enabled).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Layer-by-Layer Triage

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix as a rapid triage tool in KTU board answers as well as on the plant floor.**

| Observed Symptom | First Test | If Test Passes → | If Test Fails → |
|---|---|---|---|
| No link LED on port | Cable continuity test | Check port config (admin down?) | Replace cable / port |
| Link LED ON but no ping | Ping the device | IP conflict; check ARP | L3 issue — check gateway |
| Ping OK, PLC not reachable | Wireshark on tap | CIP/PROFINET frame error | PLC firmware / GSDML issue |
| Intermittent drops | Check switch error counters | Look for broadcast storm | Enable STP/RSTP |
| High latency | Measure with ping -t / PTP | Queueing in switch | Enable QoS/DSCP |
| Random CRC errors | TDR / cable certifier | Bad connector or EMI source | Re-terminate, re-route |
| All devices slow | Switch CPU/memory | Look for loop | Disable loop port |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Availability Calculation

A PROFINET network in a paper mill has:
- **MTBF** = 60,000 hours
- **MTTR** = 4 hours

Determine the system availability.

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

$$A = \dfrac{60000}{60000 + 4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{60000}{60004}$$

$$A = 0.9999333...$$

Converting to percentage:

$$\boxed{A = 99.99333\,\%}$$

This is **4.33 nines**, falling short of the **5-nines (99.999%)** target common in pharma/semiconductor. To improve, the engineer must reduce MTTR — which is the **direct outcome of better troubleshooting discipline**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The OSI-Layered Troubleshooting Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart([Reported Fault: Communication Loss]) --> nodeL1{Q1: Link LEDs active on switch port?}
    nodeL1 -- No --> nodeL1a[Layer 1 Fault: Test cable, connectors, power]
    nodeL1 -- Yes --> nodeL2{Q2: Ping successful to device IP?}
    nodeL2 -- No --> nodeL2a[Layer 2/3 Fault: Check ARP, VLAN, IP config]
    nodeL2 -- Yes --> nodeL3{Q3: PROFINET/CIP discovery sees device?}
    nodeL3 -- No --> nodeL3a[Layer 7 Fault: Device name, GSDML, firmware]
    nodeL3 -- Yes --> nodeL4{Q4: Cyclic I/O data updating in PLC?}
    nodeL4 -- No --> nodeL4a[Layer 7 Fault: Slot config, watch-dog, slot fault]
    nodeL4 -- Yes --> nodeL5{Q5: Latency within cycle budget?}
    nodeL5 -- No --> nodeL5a[Performance: QoS, IRT phase, switch priority]
    nodeL5 -- Yes --> nodeOK([No Fault: Verify application logic])
    nodeL1a --> nodeFix([Apply Fix and Verify])
    nodeL2a --> nodeFix
    nodeL3a --> nodeFix
    nodeL4a --> nodeFix
    nodeL5a --> nodeFix
    nodeOK --> nodeFix
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Industrial Cable Fault Diagnostic Flow (TDR/OTDR)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA([Suspect Cable Fault]) --> nodeB{TDR available?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Connect TDR to cable end]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Reflection detected?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Measure round-trip time]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute distance: d = v_p * t / 2]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Inspect at calculated distance]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeH[Check far end with terminator]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Open end reflection?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Cable open at full length]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK[Cable intact; look beyond cable]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeL[Use cable tester for continuity]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Check pin-out, shorts, opens]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Replace suspect segment]
    nodeG --> nodeEnd([Verify with re-test])
    nodeJ --> nodeEnd
    nodeK --> nodeEnd
    nodeN --> nodeEnd
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Troubleshooting Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BL7["Application Layer"]
        nL7A[SCADA / HMI]
        nL7B[PLC Program]
        nL7C[OPC UA Server]
    end
    subgraph BL6["Layer 3-4 Network and Transport"]
        nL4A[IP Addressing]
        nL4B[Subnet / VLAN]

    end

        nL1B[Fibre Optic]
        nL1C[Connectors and Patch Panels]
    end
    subgraph BL1["Diagnostic and Tool Layer"]
        nDA[Wireshark Protocol Analyzer]
        nDB[TDR OTDR Cable Tester]
        nDC[Managed Switch Diagnostics]
        nDD[Industrial Protocol Probe]
    end
    nL7A --- nL4A
    nL7B --- nL4B
    nL7C --- nL4C
    nL4A --- nL2A
    nL4B --- nL2B

```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Fault Distribution Pareto (Conceptual)

```mermaid

    nodeP([Industrial Network Fault Distribution]) --> nF1[Layer 1 Physical: 70 to 80 percent]
    nodeP --> nF2[Layer 2 Data Link: 10 to 15 percent]
```
