# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define network troubleshooting. List any four common physical-layer faults in industrial networks.**

**Model Answer:**

*Network troubleshooting* is the systematic process of identifying, isolating, diagnosing, and resolving faults across an industrial communication network, following the OSI layered model from the physical layer up to the application layer.

Four common physical-layer faults:

1. **Open circuit** — broken conductor or disconnected RJ45/M12 connector.
2. **Short circuit** — insulation failure between conductors.
3. **Excessive attenuation** — degraded signal due to long cable or substandard grade.
4. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** — induced noise from VFDs, motors, or welding equipment.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award full 3 marks only if the *definition* plus *exactly four distinct faults* are clearly enumerated. Avoid overlapping entries (e.g., "cable damage" and "broken cable" are the same fault — penalised).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of a Time-Domain Reflectometer (TDR) in troubleshooting industrial copper cabling.**

**Model Answer:**

A **TDR** (Time-Domain Reflectometer) is an electronic instrument used to characterise and locate faults in metallic transmission lines.

**Working Principle:**
The TDR transmits a fast-rise-time pulse down the cable. Any impedance discontinuity (open, short, kink, bad connector, water ingress) reflects a portion of the pulse back. By measuring the **round-trip time** and knowing the cable's **Nominal Velocity of Propagation (NVP)**, the distance to the fault is computed as:

$$d = \dfrac{v_p \times t_{roundtrip}}{2}$$

**Industrial Use Cases:**
- Locating **exact metres** of cable breaks inside conduit.
- Detecting **water ingress** in underground cables (changes dielectric constant → velocity shift).
- Verifying **impedance continuity** (100 Ω ± 15 Ω for PROFINET).

A TDR detects faults that simple continuity testers miss, such as **intermittent high-resistance joints** and **partial shorts**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Part B of every Module permits **internal choice**. Therefore, every question below is offered as **Question A** OR **Question B**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **OSI 7-layer model** and describe a **bottom-up troubleshooting methodology** applied to a PROFINET network. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 90 m length of **Cat 6 UTP cable** is being used to connect an EtherNet/IP device at 100 MHz. The cable datasheet specifies attenuation of **19.8 dB/100 m** and NVP of **0.66**. Compute the theoretical attenuation and the one-way propagation delay. If the PROFINET Class C limit is **18 dB**, is this cable compliant? Suggest two remedies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**OSI 7-Layer Model (Block Description):**

| Layer # | Name | Function | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | User-facing services | PROFINET IO, EtherNet/IP CIP, OPC UA |
| 6 | Presentation | Data formatting, encryption | TLS for OPC UA |
| 5 | Session | Dialog control | RPC sessions |
| 4 | Transport | End-to-end reliability | TCP / UDP |
| 3 | Network | Routing, addressing | IP, ICMP |
| 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC | Ethernet, PROFINET RT |
| 1 | Physical | Bits on the wire | Cat 6 cable, M12 connector, fibre |

**Bottom-Up Methodology applied to PROFINET:**

1. **Step 1 — Physical Layer:** Verify link LEDs, run cable tester / TDR, measure attenuation, check M12 connector torque.
2. **Step 2 — Data Link Layer:** Verify MAC address learning on switch, check for broadcast storms, count CRC errors via switch diagnostics.
3. **Step 3 — Network Layer:** Use `ping` and `arp -a` to verify IP reachability and ARP resolution.
4. **Step 4 — Transport Layer:** Use `telnet` or `nc` to test TCP port 34962 (PROFINET RPC) and UDP 34963.
5. **Step 5–7 — Application Layers:** Use Wireshark with PROFINET dissector; check device name in TIA Portal; verify GSDML version; read PLC diagnostic buffer.

This method ensures **lower layers are validated first** before assuming higher-layer complexity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Theoretical Attenuation:**

$$A = A_{per\,100\,m} \times \dfrac{L}{100}$$

$$A = 19.8 \times \dfrac{90}{100}$$

$$\boxed{A = 17.82 \text{ dB}}$$

**One-way Propagation Delay:**

$$v_p = NVP \times c = 0.66 \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.98 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

$$t_p = \dfrac{L}{v_p} = \dfrac{90}{1.98 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\boxed{t_p = 4.545 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 454.5 \text{ ns}}$$

**Compliance Decision:**

$$A_{computed} = 17.82 \text{ dB} < 18 \text{ dB (limit)}$$

The cable **passes the attenuation check** with **0.18 dB margin** — very tight. Including 2 patch cords (≈ 0.3 dB each), the total becomes **18.42 dB**, which **FAILS**.

**Two Remedies:**

1. **Reduce cable length** to ≤ 82 m (free of patch cords) so that theoretical attenuation ≤ 16.24 dB.
2. **Upgrade cable** to **Cat 6A S/FTP** (typical 100 MHz attenuation = 18.4 dB/100 m; with shielding margin, Class C is comfortably met).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** List and explain **any six industrial-grade cable-testing parameters** that must be verified before commissioning a PROFINET copper network. For each parameter, state the typical acceptance limit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A TDR test on a Cat 6 cable run shows a reflection pulse **682 ns** after launch. The cable NVP is **0.70**. The installed cable spec is **Cat 6, attenuation 21.3 dB/100 m**, and the run is **suspected of an open at the calculated distance**. Compute the fault distance. The PROFINET IRT cycle time is **1 ms**; the cable's measured delay equals 1% of cycle time. Determine the **maximum tolerable run length** such that cable delay does not exceed 1%. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Six Cable-Testing Parameters:**

| # | Parameter | Description | Acceptance Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Attenuation (Insertion Loss)** | Signal strength lost per unit length | ≤ 18 dB for PROFINET Class C |
| 2 | **Near-End Crosstalk (NEXT)** | Coupling between pairs at the near end | ≥ 30 dB (higher better) |
| 3 | **Return Loss** | Reflection due to impedance mismatch | ≥ 10 dB |
| 4 | **Propagation Delay** | Time for signal to traverse the cable | ≤ 5.7 ns/m @ 100 MHz |
| 5 | **Delay Skew** | Difference in delay between pairs | ≤ 50 ns per 100 m |
| 6 | **DC Resistance / Loop Resistance** | Conductor resistance | ≤ 9.38 Ω per 100 m per pair |

These parameters are measured by a **Class D/E cable certifier** (e.g., Fluke DTX, Softing WireXpert).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Fault Distance from TDR:**

$$v_p = 0.70 \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 2.10 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

$$d = \dfrac{v_p \times t}{2} = \dfrac{2.10 \times 10^{8} \times 682 \times 10^{-9}}{2}$$

$$d = \dfrac{143.22}{2}$$

$$\boxed{d = 71.61 \text{ m}}$$

**Maximum Tolerable Run Length:**

Cycle time = 1 ms = **1,000,000 ns**. Cable delay = 1% = **10,000 ns**.

For Cat 6, typical propagation delay ≈ **5.7 ns/m**.

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{10000}{5.7} = 1754.39 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_{max} \approx 1754 \text{ m}}$$

**Industrial Note:** While the calculation permits ~1.7 km, **PROFINET mandates a maximum segment of 100 m** (copper) due to attenuation and CSMA/CD timing constraints (round-trip propagation ≤ 51.2 µs at 100 Mbps). Therefore, the **practical limit is 100 m**, governed by the **Ethernet standard**, not by cycle-time budget.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in Troubleshooting questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing attenuation units** — students often mix `dB` and `dBm`. KTU always expects **dB** for attenuation. Writing *21.7 dBm* instead of *21.7 dB* will lose the answer mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in TDR** — students compute one-way distance using $t$ directly. The measured pulse is **round-trip**; dividing by 2 is mandatory.
> 3. **Using speed of light in vacuum instead of $v_p = NVP \times c$** — gives an answer ~50% too large.
> 4. **Skipping the layer identification step** — in any *symptom-based* question, the answer must first **classify the layer of the fault** before listing diagnostics.
> 5. **Conflating availability and reliability** — availability includes MTTR; reliability is MTBF alone.
> 6. **Omitting units** — every numerical answer in KTU valuation **must carry SI units** (dB, ns, m, %, ms).
> 7. **Listing the OSI layers without explaining troubleshooting logic** — for 7-mark questions, merely naming layers fetches at most 3 marks; the *application to troubleshooting* is what scores the rest.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must read before entering the exam hall:**

- **Definition:** Troubleshooting = systematic fault isolation + diagnosis + correction in industrial networks, layer by layer.
- **70–80% of industrial faults originate at Layer 1** (Physical). Always check cable first.
- **Three methodologies:** Bottom-Up, Top-Down, Divide-and-Conquer. Divide-and-Conquer is the most efficient.
- **Seven-step cycle:** Symptom → Documentation → Probable Cause → Localise → Root-Cause → Correct → Verify.
- **Attenuation formula:** $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$ for power, $20 \log_{10}(V_{in}/V_{out})$ for voltage.
- **SNR for PROFINET:** ≥ 18 dB.
- **TDR fault distance:** $d = v_p \cdot t / 2$ — **always divide by 2** (round-trip).
- **Propagation velocity:** $v_p = NVP \times c$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Maximum copper segment:** 100 m (Ethernet standard, not PROFINET-specific).
- **PROFINET IRT cycle:** ≤ 1 ms for high-performance applications.
- **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. Goal: 5-nines (99.999%).
- **Common tools & their layers:**
  - TDR / Cable Tester / OTDR → **Layer 1**
  - Wireshark + Dissector → **Layers 2–7**
  - Managed Switch Diagnostics → **Layers 1–2**
  - Protocol Analyzer (PROFINET/CIP) → **Layer 7**
- **Five core cable parameters:** Attenuation, NEXT, Return Loss, Propagation Delay, Delay Skew.
- **Documentation discipline:** Always update as-built drawings after every fix — undocumented fixes become the *next* fault.
- **5-Why analysis** is the KTU-favoured root-cause technique; expect it in case-study questions.
- **EMI countermeasures:** Shielded cable (S/FTP), proper 360° shield bonding at panel entry, segregation from VFD cables (≥ 200 mm parallel, ≥ 100 mm crossover).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Physical Layer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition The **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** is the lowest stratum of the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Reference Model**, governed by **ISO/IEC 7498-1** and **IEEE 802.3** standards. It is responsible for the transparent transmission and reception of unstructured bit-streams across a physical communication medium. Its primary obligations encompass: - Mechanical specifications of connectors and pinouts - Electrical specifications of voltage/current levels, impedance, and line encoding - Functional specifications for transmission/reception procedures - Procedural specifications for activation, deactivation, and link maintenance > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Industrial Networking (PERUT741), the Physical Layer extends beyond classical data communications to include **industrial-grade physical interfaces** such as **RS-232, RS-422, RS-485, Industrial Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, fieldbus physical signaling (PROFIBUS DP/PA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1), and **EMI/EMC-hardened transmission media**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Highway System" Imagine the **Physical Layer** as the **road, pavement, traffic signals, and vehicle mechanics** of a national transportation system: | Networking Element | Highway Analogy | |---|---| | Physical Layer (Layer 1) | Roads, asphalt quality, road signs | | Data Link Layer (Layer 2) | Traffic police & lane discipline | | Network Layer (Layer 3) | GPS routing & navigation | | The signal (bits) | The vehicles transporting goods | The **Physical Layer** does not care *what* message is being sent (that is Layer 2's job). It merely guarantees that **voltages transition properly on a copper wire**, that **photons travel through a glass strand**, or that **electromagnetic waves propagate through air** — converting logical 1s and 0s into physical phenomena and vice-versa. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Distinction:** The Physical Layer transmits **bits**, not frames or packets. Framing and error detection occur at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2). ### 1.3 Industrial Context — Why is the Physical Layer Critical in Industry? In a **harsh factory floor** with motors, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), welding equipment, and high-voltage switchgear, the Physical Layer must: 1. **Reject electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — governed by **IEC 61000** family 2. **Survive temperature extremes** — typically **-40 °C to +85 °C** 3. **Resist mechanical stress** — vibration, oil, chemicals, abrasion 4. **Maintain deterministic latency** — for real-time control loops in **PROFINET IRT**, **EtherCAT**, **SERCOS III** 5. **Support long cable runs** — RS-485 up to **1200 metres**, PROFIBUS DP up to **1000 m** per segment without repeaters ![Importance of Physical Layer in Industry] > [!TIP] > **Memory Anchor for Exams:** Remember the acronym **M.E.F.P** — *Mechanical, Electrical, Functional, Procedural* — the four ISO-defined responsibilities of the Physical Layer. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Four Functional Domains The Physical Layer performs four intertwined operational duties: #### 2.1.1 Mechanical Domain (Connectors & Cabling) - **Pin assignments** for connectors (e.g., DB-9, DB-25, M12, RJ45) - **Gender of connectors** (Male/Female/DTE/DCE) - **Cable construction**: stranded vs solid core, shield type (foil/braid), jacket material (PVC, PUR, TPE for drag chains) #### 2.1.2 Electrical Domain (Signal Levels & Encoding) - **Voltage levels**: e.g., RS-232 uses **±3 V to ±15 V** (logic '0' = +3 to +15 V; logic '1' = -3 to -15 V) - **Current loop signalling**: legacy 0–20 mA / 4–20 mA industrial standard - **Line encoding schemes**: NRZ, NRZI, Manchester, Differential Manchester, MLT-3, PAM #### 2.1.3 Functional Domain (Channel Access) - **Simplex**, **Half-Duplex (HDX)**, **Full-Duplex (FDX)** operation - **Channel multiplexing**: TDM, FDM, WDM, CDM #### 2.1.4 Procedural Domain (Link Initialization) - Handshaking sequences - Bit clock recovery via PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) - Auto-negotiation in Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u/u/ab) ### 2.2 Transmission Modes | Mode | Direction | Industrial Example | |---|---|---| | **Simplex** | One-way only | Broadcast alarms on factory PA | | **Half-Duplex** | Both ways, not simultaneously | RS-485 multi-drop PROFIBUS | | **Full-Duplex** | Both ways simultaneously | 100BASE-TX Ethernet (separate Tx/Rx pairs) | ### 2.3 Physical Network Topologies | Topology | Description | Industrial Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Bus** | Single shared backbone cable | Legacy PROFIBUS DP, RS-485 multidrop | | **Star** | All nodes connect to central hub/switch | Modern Industrial Ethernet panels | | **Ring** | Closed loop; token passed | Token Ring (legacy), Fibre-optic redundancy | | **Mesh** | Every node connects to multiple nodes | Wireless plant networks (WirelessHART) | | **Tree / Hierarchical** | Combination of star and bus | Multi-level SCADA networks | | **Line / Daisy-chain** | Linear chain of devices | PROFINET, EtherCAT daisy-chains | ### 2.4 Transmission Media — The KTU "Three Pillars" #### A. Twisted-Pair Copper Cable - **UTP** (Unshielded Twisted Pair) — Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a, Cat7 - **STP / ScTP** (Shielded) — for industrial EMC compliance - Characteristic impedance: **100 Ω** (Ethernet), **120 Ω** (PROFIBUS), **150 Ω** (some fieldbuses) - Attenuation: **dB/100 m**, increases with √f #### B. Coaxial Cable - Impedance: **50 Ω** (data, e.g., 10BASE-2), **75 Ω** (video, CCTV) - Still used in some legacy industrial CCTV and FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 variants #### C. Optical Fibre - **Multimode (MMF)**: 50/125 µm or 62.5/125 µm; up to **2 km** at 1 Gbps - **Singlemode (SMF)**: 9/125 µm; up to **40–120 km** - Immune to EMI — **preferred for harsh industrial environments** - Wavelengths: **850 nm** (MMF), **1310 nm** & **1550 nm** (SMF) ### 2.5 Industrial Physical-Layer Standards — The Examiner's Favourite | Standard | Use | Max Distance | Max Speed | Topology | |---|---|---|---|---| | **RS-232** | Point-to-point, legacy | 15 m | 20 kbps | Point-to-point | | **RS-422** | Multi-drop (1 driver, up to 10 receivers) | 1200 m | 10 Mbps | Bus | | **RS-485** | True multi-point (32 drivers/receivers) | 1200 m | 10 Mbps (up to 35 Mbps at short range) | Bus | | **PROFIBUS DP** | Fieldbus (RS-485 physical) | 1200 m | 12 Mbps | Bus/Line | | **PROFINET** | Industrial Ethernet | 100 m (copper) | 1 Gbps / 100 Mbps | Star/Line | | **EtherCAT** | Real-time Ethernet | 100 m (copper) | 100 Mbps | Daisy-chain/Line | | **FOUNDATION H1** | Process automation | 1900 m | 31.25 kbps | Bus | | **HART** | 4-20 mA + digital overlay | varies | 1.2 kbps | Point-to-point | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **exam-critical** for the KTU ESE. Memorise units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_{N} = 2 B \log_{2}(M)$ | Nyquist noiseless bit rate | $C_N$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, $M$ = signal levels | | 2 | $C_{S} = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | $C_S$ in bps, $\dfrac{S}{N}$ dimensionless ratio | | 3 | $S/N\_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | SNR in decibels | dB | | 4 | $t_{p} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation time | $d$ in metres, $v$ in m/s | | 5 | $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | Signal velocity in medium | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $n$ = refractive index | | 6 | $t_{t} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay (store-and-forward) | $L$ = bits, $R$ = bps | | 7 | $T_{latency} = t_{p} + t_{t} + t_{q}$ | Total link latency | seconds | | 8 | $E = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy (fibre optics) | Joules, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s | | 9 | $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Attenuation / Loss | dB | | 10 | $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \times 10^{-A_{\text{dB}}/10}$ | Output power from input & loss | Watts / dBm | | 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{useful bps}}{\text{channel bps}} \times 100\%$ | Transmission efficiency | percentage | | 12 | $\text{NRZ efficiency} = 100\%$, $\text{Manchester} = 50\%$ | Encoding efficiency rule | percentage | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Industry 4.0** and **IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things)**, the Physical Layer underpins: 1. **Deterministic motion control** — EtherCAT uses special "on-the-fly" processing at the physical layer, achieving cycle times of **≤ 100 µs** with jitter of **≤ 1 µs**. 2. **Long-reach process automation** — fibre optic SMF links connect offshore oil platforms over 100+ km. 3. **Safety-critical signalling** — PROFIsafe / CIP Safety over Industrial Ethernet relies on deterministic physical-layer latency. 4. **EMC compliance** — failure to shield the Physical Layer correctly (improper grounding of shield at *one end only*) leads to common-mode noise and spurious trips in DCS systems. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel) **Statement:** In a noise-free channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz using $M$ discrete signal levels, the maximum data rate is: $$C_{N} = 2 B \log_{2}(M) \;\; \text{bits per second}$$ **Step-by-Step Reasoning:** - **Step 1:** Consider a band-limited noiseless channel. The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states the channel can resolve **2B independent signal changes per second** (each cycle supports two transitions). - **Step 2:** If each signal change carries $\log_{2}(M)$ bits of information (because $M$ levels encode $\log_{2}(M)$ bits), the data rate multiplies. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bit rate} &= (\text{transitions per second}) \times (\text{bits per transition}) \\[4pt] &= (2B) \times \log_{2}(M) \;\; \text{bps} \\[4pt] \therefore \quad C_{N} &= 2 B \log_{2}(M) \end{aligned} $$ > **Boundary Cases:** > - Binary signalling ($M = 2$): $C_N = 2B$ bps (e.g., 3 kHz telephone channel $\Rightarrow 6$ kbps) > - Quaternary signalling ($M = 4$): $C_N = 4B$ bps ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Channel Capacity (Noisy Channel) **Statement:** The theoretical maximum error-free bit rate over a channel of bandwidth $B$ with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is: $$C_{S} = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits per second}$$ **Step-by-Step Reasoning:** - **Step 1:** Start from the entropy-per-symbol relation for a Gaussian channel. The mutual information $I(X;Y)$ per channel use is maximised when the input distribution is Gaussian. - **Step 2:** The capacity per second equals the capacity per Hz times the bandwidth: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second} \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Example — Exam Style:** > > A twisted-pair channel has $B = 3 \text{ kHz}$ and $S/N = 30 \text{ dB}$. > > - Convert: $S/N = 10^{30/10} = 1000$ > - Capacity: $C_S = 3000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = 3000 \times 9.97 \approx 29.91 \text{ kbps}$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Propagation & Transmission Delay **Total end-to-end latency** combines **propagation delay** (signal travelling through medium) and **transmission delay** (time to push all bits onto wire): $$ \begin{aligned} t_p &= \frac{d}{v} = \frac{d \cdot n}{c} \\[4pt] t_t &= \frac{L}{R} \\[4pt] T_{\text{latency}} &= t_p + t_t + t_q \end{aligned} $$ where: - $d$ = physical cable length (m) - $v$ = signal velocity in medium (m/s) - $n$ = refractive index (≈ 1.5 for fibre, ≈ 2.0 for coax) - $L$ = frame length (bits) - $R$ = data rate (bps) - $t_q$ = queueing + processing delay **Worked Example:** A PROFINET packet of 1500 bytes traverses 100 m of Cat6 cable. - Signal velocity in copper (≈ 2/3 c) $\Rightarrow v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s - $t_p = \dfrac{100}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.5 \,\mu\text{s}$ - $R = 100 \text{ Mbps}$, $L = 1500 \times 8 = 12\,000$ bits - $t_t = \dfrac{12000}{10^{8}} = 120 \,\mu\text{s}$ - $\therefore T_{\text{latency}} \approx 120.5 \,\mu\text{s}$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optical Power Budget (Fibre Link Design) A fibre link is feasible if: $$P_{\text{tx}} - P_{\text{losses}} \geq P_{\text{rx,min}}$$ where total losses include fibre attenuation, connector insertion loss, splice loss, and system margin. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{losses}} &= (\alpha \cdot d) + N_c L_c + N_s L_s + M_{\text{system}} \\[4pt] \alpha &= \text{attenuation coefficient in dB/km} \\[4pt] L_c, L_s &= \text{per-connector / per-splice loss in dB} \\[4pt] N_c, N_s &= \text{number of connectors / splices} \\[4pt] M_{\text{system}} &= 3\text{–}6 \text{ dB safety margin} \end{aligned} $$ > **Worked Example:** > $P_{\text{tx}} = -3 \text{ dBm}$, fibre $\alpha = 0.4 \text{ dB/km}$ at 1310 nm, link length $d = 25 \text{ km}$, 4 connectors each losing $0.5 \text{ dB}$, $M = 3 \text{ dB}$. > > $P_{\text{losses}} = (0.4 \times 25) + (4 \times 0.5) + 3 = 10 + 2 + 3 = 15 \text{ dB}$ > > $P_{\text{rx}} = -3 - 15 = -18 \text{ dBm}$ > > If receiver sensitivity is **-25 dBm**, the link has **7 dB margin** ✓ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Industrial Physical Layers | Parameter | RS-232 | RS-422 | RS-485 | PROFIBUS DP | PROFINET (100BASE-TX) | EtherCAT | FOUNDATION H1 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mode | FDX | FDX | HDX/FDX | HDX | FDX | FDX | HDX | | Topology | Pt-to-Pt | Multi-drop | Multi-point | Bus | Star/Line | Daisy-chain | Bus | | Max Nodes | 2 | 10 RX, 1 TX | 32 (more with repeaters) | 32 / segment | Many (switched) | 65535 | 32 / segment | | Max Distance | 15 m | 1200 m | 1200 m | 1200 m | 100 m | 100 m | 1900 m | | Max Speed | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | 10 Mbps | 12 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 31.25 kbps | | Media | Copper | Twisted pair | Twisted pair | Twisted pair | UTP Cat5e+ | UTP Cat5e+ | Twisted pair / fibre | | Industrial EMC | Poor | Fair | Good | Good | Good (with shielding) | Good | Excellent | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The OSI Model — Physical Layer Position ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7: APPLICATION
HTTP, FTP, SNMP, OPC UA"] L6["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION
SSL, TLS, MIME"] L5["LAYER 5: SESSION
RPC, NetBIOS"] L4["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT
TCP, UDP"] L3["LAYER 3: NETWORK
IP, ICMP, Routing"] L2["LAYER 2: DATA LINK
MAC, LLC, Switches"] L1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL
Cables, Fibres, Voltages, Bits"] MEDIA["TRANSMISSION MEDIA
Copper / Fibre / Wireless RF"] L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 L1 --> MEDIA MEDIA -.feedback.-> L1 classDef highlight fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class L1,MEDIA highlight ``` > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** The **Physical Layer** is the *only* layer that converts **logical data into physical phenomena**. Everything above it stays in the digital domain. ### 4.2 Physical Layer Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DTE["DTE - Data Terminal Equipment"] APP["Application / Higher Layers"] APP -->|Bits| TX end subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER TRANSCEIVER"] TX["TX Encoder
Line Coding"] CLK["Clock Recovery PLL"] CHAN["Channel Driver
Driver Amplifier"] RX["RX Decoder
Equalizer"] TX --> CHAN CHAN -.feedback.-> RX CLK -.synchronizes.-> TX CLK -.synchronizes.-> RX end subgraph MEDIA["TRANSMISSION MEDIA"] COPPER["Twisted Pair Copper"] FIBRE["Optical Fibre"] RF["Wireless RF Channel"] end subgraph DCE["DCE - Data Circuit-terminating Equipment"] RX2["Receiver / Optical Detector"] RX2 -->|Bits| APP2["Higher Layers"] end CHAN -->|Signals| MEDIA MEDIA --> RX2 classDef phy fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef media fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class TX,CLK,CHAN,RX phy class COPPER,FIBRE,RF media ``` ### 4.3 Industrial Network Topology Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BUS["BUS TOPOLOGY - PROFIBUS RS-485"] B1["Master PLC"] --- B2["Slave Drive"] B2 --- B3["Slave Sensor"] B3 --- B4["Slave HMI"] B4 --- B5["Terminator 220 ohm"] end subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY - Industrial Ethernet"] S_SW["Industrial Switch"] S_PLC["PLC"] S_VFD["VFD Drive"] S_HMI["HMI Panel"] S_IO["Remote I/O"] S_SW --- S_PLC S_SW --- S_VFD S_SW --- S_HMI S_SW --- S_IO end subgraph RING["RING TOPOLOGY - Redundant Fibre"] R1["Switch 1"] --- R2["Switch 2"] R2 --- R3["Switch 3"] R3 --- R4["Switch 4"] R4 -.redundant.-> R1 end subgraph LINE["LINE / DAISY-CHAIN - EtherCAT"] L1["Master"] --- L2["Slave 1"] L2 --- L3["Slave 2"] L3 --- L4["Slave 3"] L4 --- L5["Last Slave"] end classDef busStyle fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#e69138,color:#000 classDef starStyle fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#3c78d8,color:#000 classDef ringStyle fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#674ea7,color:#000 classDef lineStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#6aa84f,color:#000 class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 busStyle class S_SW,S_PLC,S_VFD,S_HMI,S_IO starStyle class R1,R2,R3,R4 ringStyle class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5 lineStyle ``` ### 4.4 Line Encoding Schemes — Signal Waveforms ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ENC["LINE ENCODING COMPARISON"] DATA["Data Bit Stream
1 0 1 1 0 0 1"] end subgraph NRZ["NRZ - Non Return to Zero"] NRZ_BIT["Logic 1 = High V
Logic 0 = Low V
Efficiency = 100 percent"] end subgraph MAN["MANCHESTER"] MAN_BIT["Mid-bit transition
1 = High to Low
0 = Low to High
Efficiency = 50 percent"] end subgraph DMAN["DIFFERENTIAL MANCHESTER"] DMAN_BIT["Always mid-bit transition
Plus level change for 0
Used in Token Ring"] end subgraph MLT["MLT-3"] MLT_BIT["Multi-Level Transmit 3
Cycles through 0, plus V, 0, minus V
Used in 100BASE-TX"] end DATA --> NRZ DATA --> MAN DATA --> DMAN DATA --> MLT classDef enc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 class NRZ,MAN,DMAN,MLT enc ``` ### 4.5 Signal Propagation Path — End-to-End Industrial Link ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PLC Master
Industrial Controller"] -->|Electrical Signal| B["Physical Connector
M12 or RJ45"] B --> C["Industrial Cable
Shielded Twisted Pair"] C --> D["Junction Box
EMC Rated"] D --> E["Cable Tray Run
Separated from Power"] E --> F["Field Device
Sensor or Actuator"] F --> G["Transducer
Physical Phenomenon"] classDef src fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 classDef link fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef dest fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#b45f06,color:#000 class A src class B,C,D,E link class F,G dest ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** on the following mistakes: > 1. Confusing **bit rate (bps)** with **baud rate (symbols/sec)** — they differ by the factor $\log_2(M)$. > 2. Stating RS-485 is *"point-to-point"* — it is fundamentally **multi-drop**. > 3. Using Shannon's formula when the question asks for *noiseless* — that requires **Nyquist**. > 4. Forgetting the **factor of 2** in Nyquist ($C = 2B \log_2 M$, not $C = B \log_2 M$). > 5. Mixing up the impedance of RS-485 (**120 Ω termination**) with Ethernet (**100 Ω**). > 6. Not stating units in the final answer. Always write **dB, m, bps, µs** explicitly. --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the Physical Layer of the OSI model. List any four of its responsibilities.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Physical Layer (Layer 1) is the lowest layer of the OSI reference model and is responsible for the **transmission and reception of unstructured bit-streams over a physical medium**. Four responsibilities (1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 = 2 marks): 1. **Mechanical** — connector types and pin assignments (e.g., RJ45, DB-9, M12). 2. **Electrical** — voltage levels, current levels, impedance matching. 3. **Functional** — channel access (simplex/half-duplex/full-duplex) and multiplexing. 4. **Procedural** — handshaking, bit synchronisation, link activation/deactivation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between RS-232 and RS-485 physical-layer standards. (Any 3 points)** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 (1 mark for table) | |---|---|---| | Topology | Point-to-point only | Multi-point (up to 32 drivers) | | Max Distance | 15 m | 1200 m | | Max Speed | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | | Voltage Levels | ±3 to ±15 V (single-ended) | Differential ±1.5 V (balanced) | | EMC Immunity | Poor (single-ended) | Excellent (differential) | | Mode | Full-duplex | Half/Full-duplex | (3 clear points × 1 mark each = 3 marks) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU ESE 2024** regulations, students answer **ONE full question from Module 2** (Module Internal Choice). Each 14-mark question is split into **7 + 7** sub-parts. Below are two alternative choices. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **five physical network topologies** used in industrial communication. For each, mention **one industrial protocol** that typically uses it. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A **physical topology** describes how devices are physically interconnected by the transmission media. **(i) Bus Topology (1 Mark + 0.5 for protocol)** - All devices share a single backbone cable terminated at both ends with characteristic-impedance terminators (typically 120 Ω for RS-485). - Signal travels in both directions from any transmitting node. - *Diagram: Master—Slave1—Slave2—Slave3—Terminator* - **Industrial Protocol:** PROFIBUS DP, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1. **(ii) Star Topology (1 Mark)** - All nodes connect to a central hub/switch; failure of central device breaks the network but individual links remain isolated. - *Diagram: Hub at centre, four nodes radiating.* - **Industrial Protocol:** Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP). **(iii) Ring Topology (1 Mark)** - Nodes form a closed loop. Token or frame circulates unidirectionally. - *Diagram: Square/circular arrangement of four switches.* - **Industrial Protocol:** Token Ring (legacy), Media Redundancy Protocol (MRP) ring in PROFINET. **(iv) Mesh Topology (1 Mark)** - Every node connects to multiple other nodes, providing redundant paths. - **Industrial Protocol:** WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a. **(v) Line / Daisy-Chain Topology (1 Mark)** - Linear sequence where each device has exactly two neighbours (except ends). - **Industrial Protocol:** EtherCAT, PROFINET with copper daisy-chain. **(vi) Tree / Hierarchical (0.5 mark bonus)** — multi-level SCADA hierarchy. --- **(b)** A noisy industrial channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and a signal-to-noise ratio of **1023**. Calculate: (i) The theoretical maximum channel capacity using **Shannon's theorem**. **(2 Marks)** (ii) The Nyquist bit rate if **8-level signalling** is used. **(2 Marks)** (iii) State the meaning of the term **"line encoding"** and explain **Manchester encoding** with a sketch. **(3 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Shannon Capacity (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_S &= B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\[4pt] &= 4000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 1023) \\[4pt] &= 4000 \times \log_{2}(1024) \\[4pt] &= 4000 \times 10 \\[4pt] &= 40\,000 \text{ bps} \;=\; 40 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Nyquist Bit Rate for M = 8 (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_N &= 2B \log_{2}(M) \\[4pt] &= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(8) \\[4pt] &= 2 \times 4000 \times 3 \\[4pt] &= 24\,000 \text{ bps} \;=\; 24 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ > *Note:* Shannon limit (40 kbps) exceeds Nyquist limit (24 kbps) only if a smarter coding (e.g., trellis, OFDM) is used. With pure M-ary NRZ, the channel supports only 24 kbps. **(iii) Line Encoding and Manchester (3 Marks):** **Line encoding (1 Mark):** The technique of converting digital binary data (bits) into a pattern of voltage/current/light pulses suitable for transmission over the physical medium. Objectives: synchronisation, DC balance, error detection, and bandwidth efficiency. **Manchester encoding (2 Marks):** A synchronous line code in which each bit period has a **mid-bit transition**. - Logic **1** = transition from **High → Low**. - Logic **0** = transition from **Low → High**. Sketch (description, 1 mark): ``` Bit: 1 0 1 1 0 Signal: ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ ──┘ └─ ─┘ └─ ─── ─┘ └─ ``` This guarantees at least **one transition per bit**, providing a strong clock-recovery component but only **50% efficiency** (bandwidth is doubled). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **RS-485 physical layer standard** in detail. Why is it preferred over RS-232 in industrial environments? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **RS-485 Standard (5 Marks):** - Defined by **TIA/EIA-485** and **ISO/IEC 8284**, RS-485 is a **balanced (differential) signalling** standard using a twisted pair. - **Electrical:** Differential voltage between two wires (A and B / + and −) of ±1.5 V to ±6 V; receiver threshold ±200 mV. - **Multi-point:** Up to **32 unit loads** (drivers + receivers) on a single bus. With modern transceivers (e.g., 1/8 UL), up to **256 nodes** are feasible. - **Distance vs Speed trade-off:** Up to **1200 m at 100 kbps**, or **10 Mbps at 15 m**. - **Topology:** Bus / daisy-chain with termination resistors of **120 Ω** at both cable ends to suppress reflections. - **Cabling:** Twisted pair, characteristic impedance 120 Ω, with shield grounded at **one end only** (to prevent ground loops). **Why RS-485 is preferred over RS-232 in industry (2 Marks):** | Reason | RS-232 | RS-485 | |---|---|---| | EMI immunity | Poor (single-ended) | Excellent (differential, CMRR > 60 dB) | | Multi-drop | Not supported | Up to 32 nodes | | Cable length | 15 m max | 1200 m max | | Industrial grounding | Sensitive to ground potential differences | Tolerates up to ±7 V common-mode | Therefore, RS-485 is the **physical-layer backbone of PROFIBUS DP, Modbus RTU, BACnet MS/TP**, and most legacy fieldbuses. --- **(b)** A fibre optic link of length **30 km** uses single-mode fibre with attenuation coefficient **0.35 dB/km at 1310 nm**. The transmitter power is **+3 dBm**, the receiver sensitivity is **-25 dBm**, there are **6 connectors** each with insertion loss **0.4 dB**, and **2 fusion splices** each with loss **0.1 dB**. A system margin of **3 dB** is required. **(7 Marks)** Compute: (i) Total link loss. **(2 Marks)** (ii) Received power. **(2 Marks)** (iii) Power budget margin and verify link feasibility. **(3 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total link loss (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{total}} &= (\alpha \cdot d) + (N_c \cdot L_c) + (N_s \cdot L_s) + M \\[4pt] &= (0.35 \times 30) + (6 \times 0.4) + (2 \times 0.1) + 3 \\[4pt] &= 10.5 + 2.4 + 0.2 + 3 \\[4pt] &= 16.1 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Received power (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{rx}} &= P_{\text{tx}} - L_{\text{total}} \\[4pt] &= +3 - 16.1 \\[4pt] &= -13.1 \text{ dBm} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Margin and feasibility (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Margin} &= P_{\text{rx}} - P_{\text{rx,min}} \\[4pt] &= -13.1 - (-25) \\[4pt] &= +11.9 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ Since **Margin = +11.9 dB > required 3 dB**, the link is **feasible with 8.9 dB of additional headroom** for ageing, repairs, and temperature variations. The link design is **acceptable**. ✓ > *Practical tip:* Industry often retains ≥ 6 dB margin after subtracting ageing effects (~ 1 dB/year for uncooled lasers). --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** transmits raw **bits**, not frames; framing belongs to Layer 2. - [ ] Four ISO responsibilities: **M.E.F.P** = Mechanical, Electrical, Functional, Procedural. - [ ] **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C_N = 2B \log_2 M$. **Shannon (noisy):** $C_S = B \log_2(1+S/N)$. - [ ] Shannon capacity is the **upper bound** — practically achievable only with advanced coding (LDPC, Turbo). - [ ] **RS-232:** single-ended, ±12 V, point-to-point, 15 m, 20 kbps. ❌ Not for industrial noise. - [ ] **RS-485:** differential, ±1.5 V, multi-drop (32 nodes), 1200 m, 10 Mbps. ✅ Industry favourite. - [ ] Termination resistor = **120 Ω** for RS-485 / PROFIBUS, **100 Ω** for Ethernet. - [ ] Shielded cables grounded at **one end only** to avoid ground loops. - [ ] Industrial Ethernet: **PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP** — all use **IEEE 802.3** physical layer. - [ ] **Manchester encoding** = self-clocking, 50% efficiency. **NRZ** = 100% efficiency but no clock. - [ ] **Optical fibre:** MMF for ≤ 2 km (LAN); SMF for ≥ 40 km (WAN/backbone). - [ ] Attenuation in fibre at **1310 nm ≈ 0.35 dB/km**; at **1550 nm ≈ 0.2 dB/km**. - [ ] Propagation velocity in copper ≈ **(2/3) c** ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s; in fibre ≈ **(2/3) c** as well, but with much lower attenuation. - [ ] Total latency $T = t_p + t_t + t_q$ — critical for **real-time** protocols (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT). - [ ] Always include **dB, m, bps, µs** units in KTU answers. - [ ] **EMC compliance**: industrial cables should be routed **at least 200 mm away** from VFD power cables, crossed at **90°** if unavoidable. - [ ] **MTBF** of well-shielded industrial copper links ≈ 20+ years; fibre ≈ 25+ years. ---

topics.title: Topology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Topology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Topology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> In the context of Industrial Networking, **Network Topology** is defined as the *arrangement or geometric layout* of the various network elements (nodes, controllers, sensors, actuators, field devices, switches, and communication links) that establishes the physical and logical interconnection pattern used for the exchange of process data, control signals, and diagnostic information across an industrial automation hierarchy.

The term *topology* originates from mathematics (Greek: *topos* = "place" + *logos* = "study"), but in industrial communications, it specifically refers to the **structural blueprint** that dictates how data propagates, how nodes arbitrate for the medium, how faults are detected, and how deterministic response times are achieved.

There are **two distinct dimensions** of topology that KTU examiners frequently test:

| Dimension | Definition | Industrial Relevance |
|-----------|------------|----------------------|
| **Physical Topology** | The actual physical placement of cables, connectors, and devices | Determines installation cost, cable routing, EMI susceptibility, and physical redundancy |
| **Logical Topology** | The way data *actually flows* through the network, regardless of physical layout | Determines protocol behavior, token passing, polling sequence, and determinism |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Distinction:** A network can have a *physical star* but a *logical bus* (e.g., 10BASE-T Ethernet via a hub). A network can also have a *physical ring* but a *logical bus* (e.g., Token Ring over a physical dual-ring). Always specify both dimensions in your answer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city's road system**:

- The **physical topology** is the actual map — where the roads are *built*.
- The **logical topology** is the *traffic flow pattern* — which roads cars actually *use* to reach their destinations.

In an industrial plant:
- The **topology** is the highway system connecting a PLC (master controller) to its 200 temperature sensors and motor drives (slaves).
- A **bus topology** is like a *single-lane one-way street* where everyone queues up.
- A **star topology** is like a *roundabout hub* — every device reports to the center.
- A **ring topology** is like a *conveyor belt* — tokens (authorization slips) travel in a circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Scenario:** In a petrochemical refinery, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 segments are physically wired as a **bus (linear) topology** because it minimizes cable usage in hazardous areas. However, a Modbus TCP network in the same plant is physically wired as a **star topology** because of Ethernet's point-to-point nature — but logically, a master polls slaves one by one.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Highlighted

The following metrics are **bolded** because they are the standard KTU evaluation criteria for any topology-based question:

- **Determinism** — *Guaranteed worst-case message delivery time* (critical for hard real-time control loops).
- **Fault Tolerance / Redundancy** — *Ability to maintain operation after a single point of failure*.
- **Scalability** — *Maximum number of nodes supported without performance degradation*.
- **Latency** — *End-to-end delay between a sensor event and the controller's response*.
- **Throughput** — *Maximum data rate (bits/second) sustainable across the topology*.
- **Cable Cost per Node** — *Total cable length ÷ number of nodes* (capital expenditure metric).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Industrial Network Topologies

KTU Module 2 specifically emphasizes the following **six canonical topologies**. Each is dissected below with operational logic.

#### 2.1.1 Bus (Linear) Topology

**Operational Logic (How it works):**

- All devices are connected to a single shared communication medium (a *trunk* or *backbone* cable).
- Signals travel in **both directions** from the source device; every other device on the bus *hears* the transmission.
- The ends of the bus cable must be terminated with **termination resistors (typically 120 Ω or 150 Ω)** to prevent *signal reflection*, which would otherwise corrupt data.
- **Logical access control:** Typically uses *CSMA/CD* (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) — e.g., Ethernet — or a deterministic *token passing* protocol like PROFIBUS.

**Industrial Use Case:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1, PROFIBUS DP/FMS segments, CAN bus in automotive and machine-level automation, RS-485 based Modbus RTU multi-drop.

**Why it is used in industry:** Minimal cable length, low cost per node, ideal for long, narrow plant areas (e.g., pipeline SCADA systems).

#### 2.1.2 Star Topology

**Operational Logic:**

- All devices connect to a **central hub, switch, or controller**.
- Each device has a **dedicated point-to-point link** to the center.
- Communication between two devices *must* pass through the central node.
- In a *star with a hub* (Layer 1 device) — logically still a bus. In a *star with a switch* (Layer 2 device) — logical point-to-point.

**Industrial Use Case:** Industrial Ethernet (Profinet, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP) deployed using managed Ethernet switches; star is the de-facto topology in control rooms.

**Why it is used in industry:** Centralized management, easy troubleshooting, isolation of faulty segments.

#### 2.1.3 Ring Topology

**Operational Logic:**

- Devices are connected in a **closed loop**. Each device has exactly **two neighbors**.
- Data travels in **one direction** (unidirectional single ring) or **both directions** (bidirectional dual ring).
- A special **Token** circulates; only the device holding the token may transmit — this guarantees **determinism**.
- **Dual-ring redundancy:** If one ring breaks, the other ring re-routes traffic in the opposite direction (e.g., *Media Redundancy Protocol* — MRP, or *Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol* — RSTP).

**Industrial Use Case:** Token Ring (legacy), Fiber Distributed Data Interface (FDDI), Media Redundancy Protocol (MRP) in Profinet, Device Level Ring (DLR) in EtherNet/IP, ControlNet.

**Why it is used in industry:** Highly deterministic, excellent for safety-critical applications, predictable worst-case latency.

#### 2.1.4 Mesh Topology

**Operational Logic:**

- Every device is connected to **every other device** (full mesh) or to several neighbors (partial mesh).
- Data is **routed** dynamically using algorithms like *Shortest Path Bridging (SPB)* or *Routing Information Protocol (RIP)* variants.
- The **n × (n − 1) / 2** formula governs link count in a full mesh.

**Industrial Use Case:** Wireless sensor networks (WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a), large-scale power grid SCADA, redundant plant-wide Ethernet backbones.

**Why it is used in industry:** Ultimate fault tolerance, no single point of failure.

#### 2.1.5 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology

**Operational Logic:**

- A **root node** at the top, branching down to *intermediate* nodes, terminating in *leaf* devices.
- It is essentially *multiple star networks integrated* under a bus backbone.
- Failures in a *sub-branch* do not affect other branches.

**Industrial Use Case:** Purdue Reference Model's hierarchical plant network (Level 0 → Level 4), supervisory SCADA hierarchies, multi-floor building automation.

**Why it is used in industry:** Natural fit for the *Enterprise–Control–Field* layer model of ISA-95.

#### 2.1.6 Hybrid Topology

**Operational Logic:**

- A **combination** of two or more topologies to leverage the strengths of each.
- Most modern industrial plants are hybrid: a *ring backbone* with *star sub-networks* at the field level.

**Industrial Use Case:** Any large-scale Industry 4.0 / Smart Factory deployment.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for topology-related numerical questions. Memorize these formulas and their applicability.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Applicability |
|---|--------------------|-----------|------|----------------|
| 1 | **Number of links in a Full Mesh** | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | dimensionless | Mesh topology link count |
| 2 | **Number of links in a Ring** | $L = n$ | dimensionless | Ring topology link count |
| 3 | **Number of links in a Star** | $L = n$ | dimensionless | Star topology link count |
| 4 | **Number of links in a Tree** | $L = n - 1$ | dimensionless | Tree topology link count |
| 5 | **Number of links in a Bus** | $L = 1$ | dimensionless | Bus topology link count |
| 6 | **Maximum Token Rotation Time (TRT)** | $TRT_{max} = n \times t_{hold}$ | seconds (s) | Token-passing ring/bus |
| 7 | **Worst-case Latency (Token Ring)** | $T_{latency} = (n-1) \times t_{prop} + t_{proc}$ | seconds (s) | Deterministic ring analysis |
| 8 | **Signal Propagation Time** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_p}$ | seconds (s) | Cable length dependent |
| 9 | **Propagation Velocity in Copper** | $v_p \approx 0.66 \times c \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s | Coaxial / Twisted Pair |
| 10 | **Cable Cost per Node (Bus)** | $C_{node} = \dfrac{L_{cable}}{n}$ | meters/node | Bus topology economics |
| 11 | **Network Availability (Series)** | $A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | ratio (0–1) | Single-path topologies |
| 12 | **Network Availability (Parallel Redundancy)** | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$ | ratio (0–1) | Redundant dual-ring/mesh |
| 13 | **Mean Time Between Failures (Series)** | $\dfrac{1}{MTBF_{series}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{MTBF_i}$ | hours | Reliability calculation |
| 14 | **Throughput Efficiency (CSMA/CD)** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ where $a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}$ | ratio | Bus/Ethernet analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formulas above are not academic exercises — they are the exact calculations performed during *network design review* in process plants. For example, **PROFIBUS DP** segment design requires the *maximum segment cable length* (often 1200 m at 9.6 kbps to 100 m at 12 Mbps) to be calculated from the propagation delay budget to ensure that the *slot time* is never violated.

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **L = n(n−1)/2 for full mesh:** A 20-node full mesh requires **190 links** — practically impossible for copper cabling, hence the popularity of *partial mesh* in industrial wireless sensor networks.
- **MTBF series formula:** Demonstrates that *any* series component reduces the system's MTBF. This is why redundant dual-ring topologies are mandatory in safety-instrumented systems per **IEC 61508 SIL 3** requirements.
- **Availability formulas:** Used to justify the cost of redundancy in plant networks — a single-ring topology typically achieves 99.9% availability, while dual-redundant ring can reach 99.999% ("five nines").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Token Rotation Time in a Token-Passing Bus

**Problem Statement (Standard KTU Pattern):** A PROFIBUS DP segment uses token passing among $n = 12$ stations. Each station holds the token for a maximum of $t_{hold} = 5$ ms before passing it. The propagation delay between adjacent stations is $t_{prop} = 2$ μs. The token transmission time is $t_{token} = 0.5$ ms. Calculate the **worst-case token rotation time** $TRT_{max}$.

**Step 1: Identify the components of token rotation time.**

The token must travel through every station once. During one complete rotation, the total time is the sum of:
1. Token holding time at each station.
2. Token transmission time (counted once per rotation since the token is a single frame).
3. Total propagation delay across all segments of the network.

$$TRT_{max} = n \times t_{hold} + t_{token} + n \times t_{prop}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$TRT_{max} = 12 \times 5\text{ ms} + 0.5\text{ ms} + 12 \times 2\text{ μs}$$

**Step 3: Convert all units to milliseconds for consistency.**

The propagation delay per segment: $2 \text{ μs} = 0.002 \text{ ms}$.

$$TRT_{max} = 60 \text{ ms} + 0.5 \text{ ms} + 12 \times 0.002 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Perform the multiplication for the propagation term.**

$$12 \times 0.002 \text{ ms} = 0.024 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Sum all components.**

$$TRT_{max} = 60 + 0.5 + 0.024 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{TRT_{max} = 60.524 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** In a hard real-time control loop, the **worst-case token rotation time** is the *upper bound* on how long any station must wait to gain bus access. For a 12-station PROFIBUS DP network, no station waits more than **60.524 ms** before getting the token — a perfectly acceptable cycle time for most process control loops (typical target: <100 ms for regulatory control, <10 ms for high-speed motion control).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Links and Cable Cost Comparison Across Topologies

**Problem Statement (Standard KTU Pattern):** An industrial plant has $n = 10$ field devices to be networked. Compare the **total cable length required** and **number of links** for: (i) Bus, (ii) Star, (iii) Ring, (iv) Full Mesh. Assume each link is exactly $L_{link} = 50$ m long.

**Step 1: Calculate the number of links for each topology.**

- **Bus:** $L = 1$ (single backbone)
- **Star:** $L = n = 10$
- **Ring:** $L = n = 10$
- **Full Mesh:** $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$

**Step 2: Calculate the total cable length for each.**

Using the formula $L_{total} = L \times L_{link}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{bus} &= 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ m} \\
L_{star} &= 10 \times 50 = 500 \text{ m} \\
L_{ring} &= 10 \times 50 = 500 \text{ m} \\
L_{mesh} &= 45 \times 50 = 2250 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate cable cost per node (assuming uniform cost).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{bus} &= \dfrac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ m/node} \\
C_{star} &= \dfrac{500}{10} = 50 \text{ m/node} \\
C_{ring} &= \dfrac{500}{10} = 50 \text{ m/node} \\
C_{mesh} &= \dfrac{2250}{10} = 225 \text{ m/node}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparative Table.**

| Topology | Links | Total Cable | Cable per Node | Relative Cost |
|----------|-------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Bus** | 1 | 50 m | 5 m/node | **1× (cheapest)** |
| **Star** | 10 | 500 m | 50 m/node | 10× |
| **Ring** | 10 | 500 m | 50 m/node | 10× |
| **Full Mesh** | 45 | 2250 m | 225 m/node | **45× (most expensive)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The full mesh topology is **45 times more expensive in cabling** than the bus topology for the same 10 devices. This is why, in practice, full mesh is reserved only for *wireless* sensor networks (where "links" are radio channels with no cable cost) and for *critical backbone* infrastructure where redundancy justifies the expense.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Topology Selection Decision Tree

| Selection Criterion | Bus | Star | Ring | Mesh | Tree | Hybrid |
|--------------------|-----|------|------|------|------|--------|
| **Lowest cost** | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Easiest to expand** | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Fault tolerance** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Determinism** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Best for hazardous areas** | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Best for wireless** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Best for video/safety** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Number of terminators** | 2 (both ends) | 0 (in switch) | 0 (closed loop) | 0 | 1+ | Varies |
| **Max nodes (typical)** | 32 (RS-485) / 126 (Modbus) | 24 (managed switch port count) | 250 (Token Ring) | Limited by routing table | Unlimited | Unlimited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend:** ✓ = Limited support, ✓✓ = Good fit, ✓✓✓ = Excellent fit, ✗ = Not recommended.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
