[Alternative — May 2024, 14 Marks]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five error detection mechanisms** of CAN 2.0B. How do the error counters TEC and REC govern a node's state? **(b) [7 marks]** A CAN system operates at 250 kbps over a 150 m bus. The propagation delay of the cable is 5 ns/m, and the transceiver loop delay is 100 ns. The MCU clock is 8 MHz. Design the bit timing: find Tq, total Tq per bit, PROP_SEG, and a valid PS1/PS2/SJW combination. Verify the sample point. **Model Answer (a):** The five error detection mechanisms are: 1. **Bit Monitoring** — The transmitter samples the bus during transmission (except arbitration & ACK slot). Any mismatch → Bit Error. 2. **Bit Stuffing Rule** — Violation of the 5-identical-bit rule → Stuffing Error. 3. **CRC Check** — Receiver recomputes 15-bit CRC; mismatch → CRC Error. 4. **Form Check** — Fixed-form fields (CRC delim, ACK delim, EOF) must be recessive → Form Error. 5. **ACK Check** — Transmitter expects at least one receiver to overwrite the ACK slot with dominant → ACK Error. **Error State Machine:** * **Error Active** (default): node transmits 6 dominant bits on detecting error → can interfere with traffic. * **Error Passive** (TEC or REC ≥ 128): node transmits 6 *recessive* bits on error → cannot interfere, just flags. * **Bus Off** (TEC > 255): node disconnects from bus. Returns to Error Active only after detecting 128 sequences of 11 recessive bits. **Model Answer (b):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_q &= \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 125 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} N_{Tq} &= \frac{1}{R_b \cdot t_q} = \frac{1}{250{,}000 \times 125 \times 10^{-9}} = 32 \text{ Tq} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} t_{prop,one\_way} &= L \cdot \tau_m + t_{loop} = 150 \times 5\text{ns} + 100\text{ns} = 850 \text{ ns} \\ t_{prop,total} &= 2 \times 850 = 1700 \text{ ns} \\ \text{PROP} &= \frac{1700}{125} = 13.6 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 14 \text{ Tq} \end{aligned} $$ Remaining Tq = $32 - 1 - 14 = 17$. Choose **PS1 = 10, PS2 = 5, SJW = 4** (SJW $\leq \min(\text{PS1},\text{PS2})$ and $\leq 4$ Tq ✓). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sample Point} &= \frac{1 + 14 + 10}{32} = \frac{25}{32} = 78.125\% \;\; \checkmark \;(\text{within } 75\text{–}90\%) \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. Confusing **CSMA/CR** with **CSMA/CD** (Ethernet) — *fatal conceptual error*. > 2. Forgetting the **SRR bit is recessive** in extended frames — this is what makes 2.0A win over 2.0B during contention. > 3. Setting **PS2 < SJW** — violates ISO 11898-1; verifier will deduct 1–2 marks. > 4. Quoting bus length as 40 m for *any* speed — it scales with bit rate (see table). > 5. Saying CAN "uses CSMA" without specifying **CR** (collision *resolution*, not detection). > 6. Mixing up **TEC/REC** thresholds (128 = passive, 255 = bus off). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CAN 2.0B = extended (29-bit) ID**, backward compatible with CAN 2.0A (11-bit). - **Encoding:** NRZ + bit stuffing (5 identical bits → stuff). - **Access method:** **CSMA/CR** (not CD) with non-destructive bitwise arbitration. - **Dominant bit = 0**, recessive = 1; dominant always wins (wired-AND). - **Frame types:** Data, Remote, Error, Overload (+ Interframe Space). - **Extended Data Frame fields:** SOF → Arbitration (32b) → Control (6b) → Data (0–64b) → CRC (16b) → ACK (2b) → EOF (7b) → IFS. - **Bit rate vs bus length trade-off** — at 1 Mbps: 40 m; at 50 kbps: ~1 km. - **Bit time** = SYNC_SEG (1 Tq) + PROP_SEG + PS1 + PS2; all in Tq. - **Sample point** typically 75%–90% of bit time. - **Five error mechanisms:** Bit monitoring, stuffing, CRC, form, ACK. - **Three error states:** Error Active (TEC, REC < 128), Error Passive (≥ 128), Bus Off (TEC > 255). - **Maximum 8 bytes** payload per Data Frame (a key KTU point). - **Termination:** 120 Ω at both ends; bus topology strictly **linear**. - **Common higher-layer protocols on CAN 2.0B:** CANopen, DeviceNet, SAE J1939.

topics.title: Basic Principles of Radio Frequency Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Radio Frequency Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** is a non-contact, automatic identification technology that uses **electromagnetic (EM) fields** in the radio frequency portion of the spectrum to uniquely identify, capture, and transmit data stored on an *electronic tag* (transponder) attached to or embedded in an object, without requiring line-of-sight or physical contact.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme industrial networking framework, RFID is positioned as a foundational **Auto-ID** technology alongside barcodes and biometrics, distinguished by its ability to:

- Operate without human intervention
- Read multiple tags simultaneously (anti-collision)
- Function in harsh industrial environments (dust, oil, vibration, moisture)
- Support read/write memory updates

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *“RFID is a wireless non-contact system that uses radio-frequency electromagnetic fields to transfer data from a tag attached to an object for the purposes of automatic identification and tracking.”*

## 1.2 Core System Architecture

A complete RFID system consists of **three fundamental building blocks**:

| Component | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Tag / Transponder** | Stores the unique ID, attached to the asset | The "name plate" on a product |
| **Reader / Interrogator** | Generates the RF field, powers tag, decodes response | The "roll-call teacher" calling out names |
| **Antenna** | Mediates the EM coupling between reader and tag | The "loudspeaker + microphone" of the system |
| **Host / Middleware** | Filters, aggregates, forwards data to enterprise IT | The "school register / database" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Classroom Roll-Call Model

Imagine a classroom of **100 sleeping students** (tags). The teacher (reader) stands at the door and shouts loudly using a megaphone (RF signal):

1. The megaphone **wakes up** every student who can hear it — this is *tag energizing*.
2. Each sleepy student has a **unique roll number written on their forehead** (EPC, Electronic Product Code) and can *whisper it back* (backscatter modulation).
3. The teacher has a **microphone** to catch these whispers — but because many students are whispering simultaneously, the room can get noisy. The teacher applies a **discipline rule** (anti-collision algorithm like ALOHA or Tree-Walking) so each student answers in turn.
4. The teacher records attendance in a **register book** (host database).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a barcode, the student (tag) does **not** need to be "looked at" directly. The teacher can hear them through walls, clothes, or packaging — this is *non-line-of-sight (NLOS)* operation.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and frequency bands are central to all RFID calculations:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Characteristic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 377 \, \Omega$
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard RFID Frequency Bands (ISO/IEC Regulated):**
> - **LF (Low Frequency):** **125–134 kHz** — animal tagging, access control
> - **HF (High Frequency):** **13.56 MHz** — smart cards, NFC, library books
> - **UHF (Ultra High Frequency):** **860–960 MHz** — supply chain, toll collection
> - **Microwave:** **2.45 GHz & 5.8 GHz** — vehicle ID, aerospace

## 1.5 Classification of RFID Tags

RFID tags are categorized based on **power source** and **coupling mechanism**:

### By Power Source
- **Passive Tags:** No internal battery. Powered entirely by the reader’s incident EM field. Lifetime: ~10 years. Cheap (~₹5–₹40).
- **Active Tags:** Internal battery powers both the chip and a transmitter. Range up to **100 m**. Expensive (~₹2000+).
- **Semi-Passive (Battery-Assisted Passive, BAP):** Battery powers only the chip; the tag uses backscatter to reply. Range ~10–30 m.

### By Coupling Mechanism
- **Inductive (Near-Field) Coupling:** Operates within $\frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ — used in LF and HF.
- **Backscatter (Far-Field) Coupling:** Operates beyond $\frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ — used in UHF and Microwave.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete RFID read cycle follows a rigorously defined sequence:

### Step 1 — Continuous Wave (CW) Transmission
The reader energizes its antenna with a **continuous-wave RF carrier signal** of frequency $f_c$. This establishes a stable EM field in the interrogation zone.

$$P_{TX} \xrightarrow{\text{Antenna Gain } G_t} E_{\text{field}} \text{ at distance } r$$

### Step 2 — Tag Harvesting Energy (Passive Tags)
The tag’s coil/antenna intercepts a portion of the EM energy. For inductive tags, this induces a **voltage $V_{oc}$** across the coil via Faraday’s Law.

$$V_{oc} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

This induced voltage charges the tag’s internal capacitor. Once $V_{oc} \geq V_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically **1.0–1.5 V**), the chip powers up.

### Step 3 — Command Reception & Modulation
The reader superimposes data onto the carrier using **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**. The tag demodulates the envelope to receive the **SELECT** or **READ** command.

### Step 4 — Response via Backscatter (Far-Field)
The tag varies its antenna’s **radar cross-section (RCS)** by switching its load impedance between two states:
- **Matched state ($\Gamma = 0$):** absorbs power → antenna looks small
- **Short/Open state ($\Gamma = \pm 1$):** reflects power → antenna looks large

This produces an *amplitude-modulated echo* sent back to the reader.

### Step 5 — Reader Demodulation
The reader detects this weak backscatter signal (typically **–60 to –80 dBm**) and recovers the tag’s stored **EPC code**.

## 2.2 Coupling Theory — The "Why" Behind Near vs Far Field

| Property | Near-Field (Inductive) | Far-Field (Backscatter) |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $r < \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ | $r > \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ |
| Mechanism | Magnetic field $H$ coupling | EM wave propagation + reflection |
| Operating band | LF, HF | UHF, Microwave |
| Typical range | < 1 m | 1–15 m (passive), up to 100 m (active) |
| Antenna type | Coil loop | Dipole / patch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary distance** between near and far field at **915 MHz (UHF)**:
>
> $$r_{\text{boundary}} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} = \frac{c}{2\pi f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2\pi \times 915 \times 10^6} \approx 5.2 \text{ cm}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every equation you must memorize for Module 4 RFID questions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi r}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Power loss reader→tag |
| 2 | Friis in dB | $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(r) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (r in km, f in MHz) | Quick dB calculations |
| 3 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | Convert frequency ↔ wavelength |
| 4 | Boundary near/far | $r_b = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ | Choose coupling type |
| 5 | Received power (forward link) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2$ | Power at tag input |
| 6 | Induced voltage (coil) | $V_{oc} = \omega N A B$ | LF/HF tag energizing |
| 7 | Resonant frequency (LC tank) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Tag antenna tuning |
| 8 | Quality factor | $Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Bandwidth selection |
| 9 | Radar range (passive) | $r_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \sigma}{P_{\text{th}}}}$ | Maximum read distance |
| 10 | Mutual inductance | $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Inductive coupling strength |
| 11 | Reflected radar cross-section | $\sigma_{\text{tag}} = \frac{G_t^2 \lambda^2 R_a^2}{4\pi} \vert 1 - \Gamma\vert^2$ | Backscatter signal strength |
| 12 | Power delivered to tag chip | $P_{\text{chip}} = P_r \cdot \tau$ where $\tau$ = power transmission coefficient | Tag activation threshold |
| 13 | Coupling coefficient (NFC) | $k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safe Practice:** In KTU answer scripts, always write equations in **LaTeX inside a `$$` block**, never as ASCII art. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` in the RCS equation — KTU examiners penalize ambiguous notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

RFID is the silent backbone of **Industry 4.0** and **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**:

- **Automotive assembly:** Each body shell carries an RFID tag tracked from stamping → painting → final assembly.
- **Cold-chain logistics:** Temperature-sensing RFID tags transmit data every 30 seconds during pharma transport.
- **Aircraft MRO:** Tags embedded in turbine blades store maintenance history readable at 5 m using UHF.
- **Smart retail:** Amazon’s *Just-Walk-Out* stores use RFID + computer vision.
- **Tool tracking on shop floors:** Active tags on tool holders prevent FOD (Foreign Object Debris) in aerospace hangars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Resonant Frequency of an RFID Tag Antenna

The RFID tag’s antenna is modelled as a **series RLC circuit**. The chip is connected across the capacitor terminals.

### Starting Point: Impedance of a Series RLC

The complex impedance seen by the chip is:

$$Z(\omega) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

Simplifying the imaginary part:

$$Z(\omega) = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

### Resonance Condition

At resonance, the imaginary part equals zero (purely resistive — maximum power transfer):

$$\omega_0 L - \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 0$$

Solving for $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

### Numerical Example

> A passive UHF tag has $L = 28 \, \mu H$ and $C = 1.1 \, pF$. Compute $f_0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(28 \times 10^{-6})(1.1 \times 10^{-12})}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{30.8 \times 10^{-18}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.755 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.103 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&\approx 9.07 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls in the LF/HF region. To shift to **UHF (868 MHz)**, the inductance must drop to roughly:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{1}{(2\pi f_0)^2 C} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 868 \times 10^6)^2 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-12}} \\
L &= \frac{1}{2.97 \times 10^{19} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 30.6 \text{ nH}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Friis Free-Space Power Transmission Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in industrial wireless networking.

### Assumption
A transmit antenna radiates isotropically (uniformly in all directions) with total power $P_t$.

### Step 1 — Power Flux Density at Distance r

The energy spreads over the surface area of a sphere of radius $r$:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi r^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce Antenna Gain

A directional antenna of gain $G_t$ concentrates energy:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi r^2}$$

### Step 3 — Power Captured by Receive Antenna

The effective aperture of the receive antenna is:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

Therefore the power delivered to the load is:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 r^2}$$

### Final Form — Friis Transmission Equation

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2}$$

The factor $\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2$ is called the **free-space path loss** (FSPL).

### Expressing FSPL in Decibels

Taking $20\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi r}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}(r) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

(where $r$ is in km, $f$ in MHz). This is the **Kerala university favourite form** — memorize the constant **32.44**.

### Numerical Verification

> A reader transmits **$P_t$ = 1 W (0 dBW)** at **$f$ = 915 MHz** with $G_t = G_r = 6 \text{ dBi}$ (linear ≈ 3.98). Distance $r$ = 5 m. Find $P_r$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{915 \times 10^6} = 0.3279 \text{ m} \\
\text{FSPL} &= \left(\frac{4\pi \times 5}{0.3279}\right)^2 = (191.6)^2 \approx 36712 \\
P_r &= 1 \times 3.98 \times 3.98 / 36712 \\
&\approx 4.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \\
&\approx -33.6 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}$$

This value is far above the tag chip sensitivity (typically **–15 to –20 dBm**), confirming the tag can activate at 5 m.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Read Range of a Passive RFID Tag

This combines Friis with the tag chip sensitivity threshold.

### Power at the tag chip:

$$P_{\text{tag}} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the **power transmission coefficient** between tag antenna and chip (typically 0.3–0.5).

### Activation Condition

For the tag to power up:

$$P_{\text{tag}} \geq P_{\text{th}}$$

$$\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \geq \frac{P_{\text{th}}}{P_t G_t G_r \tau}$$

$$\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r} \geq \sqrt{\frac{P_{\text{th}}}{P_t G_t G_r \tau}}$$

### Solving for Maximum Range $r_{\max}$

$$\boxed{r_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{\text{th}}}}}$$

### Sample Problem (KTU-Style)

> A passive UHF tag has chip sensitivity **$P_{\text{th}}$ = –18 dBm (15.85 µW)**. Reader EIRP = **4 W (36 dBm)**. Tag antenna gain = 1.64 (linear). $\tau$ = 0.4. Compute $r_{\max}$ at 915 MHz.

Step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t G_t G_r \tau &= 4 \times 1.64 \times 1 \times 0.4 = 2.624 \text{ W} \\
\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{\text{th}}} &= \frac{2.624}{15.85 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.656 \times 10^{5} \\
\sqrt{1.656 \times 10^5} &\approx 407 \\
r_{\max} &= \frac{0.3279}{4\pi} \times 407 \\
&= \frac{0.3279 \times 407}{12.566} \\
&\approx 10.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Theoretical maximum read range ≈ **10.6 m** — typical for a 4 W UHF passive system.

## 3.4 Anti-Collision Algorithm Matrix (Analytical Comparison)

| Algorithm | Principle | Throughput | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure ALOHA** | Tag transmits randomly; collisions tolerated | 18.4% | `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` |
| **Slotted ALOHA** | Transmission only at slot boundaries | 36.8% | `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` |
| **Frame Slotted ALOHA** | Tag picks one slot from N-slot frame | Variable, peaks at N ≈ tags | Frequently tested |
| **Binary Tree Walking** | Reader splits colliding set recursively | ~100% | Advanced topic |
| **Query Tree (QT)** | Reader broadcasts prefix; matching tags reply | High | ISO 18000-63 standard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RFID System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Host[Host IT System]
        DB[(Enterprise Database)]
        MW[Middleware / ALE Layer]
    end

    subgraph Reader[RFID Reader Interrogator]
        DSP[DSP Signal Processor]
        TX[Transmitter Stage]
        RX[Receiver Stage]
        ANT1[Reader Antenna]
    end

    subgraph Tag[RFID Tag Transponder]
        ANT2[Tag Antenna Coil or Dipole]
        IC[Chip IC with EPC Memory]
        MATCH[Impedance Matching Network]
    end

    OBJ[Tagged Object Asset]

    DB <--> MW
    MW <--> DSP
    DSP <--> TX
    DSP <--> RX
    TX <--> ANT1
    RX <--> ANT1
    ANT1 -. EM Wave at 868 to 915 MHz .-> ANT2
    ANT2 <--> MATCH
    MATCH <--> IC
    IC --- OBJ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The bidirectional arrow between Reader and Tag represents **downlink** (reader → tag, CW + command) and **uplink** (tag → reader, backscatter).
- The Middleware layer implements the **Application Level Events (ALE)** specification of EPCglobal.

## 4.2 RFID Read Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CWOn: Reader energizes field
    CWOn --> SelectCmd: Issue SELECT EPC
    SelectCmd --> InventoryCmd: Issue QUERY
    InventoryCmd --> SlotWait: Tag picks Q-slot
    SlotWait --> Reply: Tag transmits RN16
    Reply --> ACKCheck: Reader sends ACK
    ACKCheck --> ReadData: EPC + CRC read
    ACKCheck --> Collision: Multiple RN16 detected
    Collision --> InventoryCmd: Adjust Q parameter
    ReadData --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state machine is the EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63) protocol, which is the **de-facto standard for global UHF RFID** and a frequent KTU short-answer target.

## 4.3 Coupling Mechanism Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RFID Tag Approaches Reader Zone] --> B{Distance vs lambda over 2 pi}
    B -- r less than lambda over 2 pi --> C[Near Field Region]
    B -- r greater than lambda over 2 pi --> D[Far Field Region]
    C --> E[Inductive Coupling]
    D --> F[Backscatter Coupling]
    E --> E1[Magnetic H Field Dominant]
    E --> E2[Coil to Coil Transformer Action]
    E --> E3[LF and HF Bands 125 kHz to 13.56 MHz]
    F --> F1[EM Wave Propagation]
    F --> F2[Modulated Radar Cross Section]
    F --> F3[UHF and Microwave 860 MHz to 5.8 GHz]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**Define RFID. List and briefly explain any four applications of RFID in industrial networking.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) is a wireless, non-contact automatic identification technology that uses radio-frequency electromagnetic fields to transfer identification data between a *tag* attached to an object and a *reader*, enabling tracking and data capture without line-of-sight.

**Four Applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Supply-chain management:** Real-time tracking of pallets and cartons from manufacturer to retailer (e.g., Walmart mandates RFID on all suppliers).
2. **Access control:** HF (13.56 MHz) smart cards replace magnetic-stripe badges in offices and metros.
3. **Asset tracking in manufacturing:** Tools, jigs, and WIP (work-in-progress) items carry active RFID tags for shop-floor visibility.
4. **Animal husbandry & agriculture:** LF glass-injectable tags uniquely identify cattle, dairy, and laboratory animals.
5. *(Bonus)* Toll collection: FASTag uses UHF RFID at 5.8 GHz on Indian highways.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Passive, Semi-Passive, and Active RFID tags. (Any 6 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Feature | Passive | Semi-Passive (BAP) | Active |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power source | None — harvests from reader | Battery for chip only | Battery for everything |
| 2 | Range | < 10 m | 10–30 m | up to 100 m |
| 3 | Cost | Low (₹5–₹40) | Medium | High (₹1000+) |
| 4 | Lifetime | ~10 yrs (no battery) | 3–5 yrs (battery) | 3–7 yrs (battery) |
| 5 | On-tag sensors | Rare | Common (temp, shock) | Common |
| 6 | Communication | Backscatter | Backscatter | Active transmit |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working principle of an RFID system. Discuss the role of each subsystem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The RFID system comprises three primary subsystems:

1. **Reader/Interrogator (3 marks):**
The reader is the master device containing a transmitter, receiver, microcontroller, and DSP. It generates the carrier signal, encodes commands (using ASK modulation), and decodes the weak tag response. Modern readers comply with EPC Class-1 Gen-2 and operate in dense-reader mode to avoid mutual interference.

2. **Tag/Transponder (2 marks):**
The tag is a miniaturized microchip bonded to an antenna, packaged as a label, card, or ruggedized enclosure. Passive tags contain **no battery**; they harvest energy from the reader’s incident field. The chip stores the **EPC (Electronic Product Code)** — a unique 64- or 96-bit identifier — plus optional user memory and TID (Tag Identifier).

3. **Middleware & Host Software (2 marks):**
Middleware implements the **Application Level Events (ALE)** filter to deduplicate reads, aggregate events, and forward them to ERP/WMS. The host stores tag reads and ties them to business logic (inventory deduction, shipment closure, etc.).

The **working principle** proceeds as: (i) reader powers up and emits CW, (ii) tag harvests energy, (iii) reader issues QUERY command, (iv) tag replies via backscatter, (v) reader decodes EPC, (vi) middleware filters and forwards to enterprise.

> *(Note: A block diagram from SECTION 4 should accompany this answer; reproduce the architecture flowchart.)*

**(b)** A passive UHF RFID tag operating at **915 MHz** has a chip sensitivity of **–15 dBm**. The reader’s EIRP is **4 W (36 dBm)**, the reader antenna gain is **8 dBi**, the tag antenna gain is **1.64 linear**, and the power transmission coefficient $\tau$ = 0.5. Calculate the theoretical maximum read range. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given data:
- $f = 915 \text{ MHz}$, so $\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{915 \times 10^6} = 0.3279 \text{ m}$
- $P_{\text{th}} = -15 \text{ dBm} = 10^{-15/10} \text{ mW} = 31.62 \text{ µW} = 3.162 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$
- $P_t G_t = \text{EIRP} = 4 \text{ W}$ (i.e., $P_t G_t$ combined)
- $G_r = 1.64$ (linear, tag antenna)
- $\tau = 0.5$

**Step 1:** Apply the read-range formula:

$$r_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{\text{th}}}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the power-ratio inside the square root:

$$\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{\text{th}}} = \frac{4 \times 1.64 \times 0.5}{3.162 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator evaluation:

$$4 \times 1.64 \times 0.5 = 3.28 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by threshold:

$$\frac{3.28}{3.162 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.037 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5:** Square root:

$$\sqrt{1.037 \times 10^5} \approx 322.0$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}$:

$$\frac{\lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{0.3279}{12.566} = 0.02609 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Final multiplication:

$$r_{\max} = 0.02609 \times 322.0 \approx 8.40 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{r_{\max} \approx 8.4 \text{ m}}$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Module-4 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63)** RFID protocol stack, clearly distinguishing the reader-to-tag and tag-to-reader physical layers. Discuss the role of anti-collision. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The EPC Class-1 Gen-2 protocol is the dominant UHF RFID standard ratified by ISO as **ISO/IEC 18000-63**. It defines a query-response protocol between a single reader and many tags.

**Reader-to-Tag (Forward) Link (3 marks):**
- Modulation: **Double-sideband Amplitude Shift Keying (DSB-ASK)** with a 10–90% modulation depth, or **SSB-ASK** (Single Sideband) for spectral efficiency.
- Data encoding: **Pulse-Interval Encoding (PIE)** — a "0" is a short pulse-interval, a "1" is a longer one. This allows easy envelope detection by the tag’s power-on circuit.
- Commands: `SELECT`, `QUERY`, `QUERY_ADJUST`, `ACK`, `READ`, `WRITE`, `KILL`, `LOCK`.

**Tag-to-Reader (Reverse) Link (2 marks):**
- Modulation: The tag does **not** actively transmit. It modulates the backscatter by toggling its input impedance between two states, changing the **Radar Cross Section (RCS)**.
- Encoding: **FM0** or **Miller subcarrier encoding** (M=2, 4, 8). The choice of Miller modulation index balances data rate vs. robustness.
- Data rate: From **40 kbps** (FM0) up to **640 kbps** (Miller-8).

**Anti-Collision — Slotted ALOHA with Q-Algorithm (2 marks):**
The reader broadcasts a `Q` parameter (4 bits, 0–15). Each tag picks a random slot in the range $0$ to $2^Q - 1$. If exactly one tag picks a slot, it transmits its **RN16** (16-bit random number). If collision occurs, the reader issues `QUERY_ADJUST` to update Q upward (more slots) or downward. This dynamic Q-algorithm maintains throughput near the theoretical **36.8% maximum** of slotted ALOHA under varying tag populations.

**(b)** Discuss the **security vulnerabilities** of RFID systems in industrial settings and propose mitigations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Vulnerability 1 — Eavesdropping (2 marks):**
Because the tag-to-reader backscatter signal travels through free space, an attacker with a sensitive SDR (Software Defined Radio, e.g., USRP) can intercept it from **3–10 m** away.

**Mitigation:** Encrypt the air-interface payload using AES-128 (supported in Gen-2 since 2018). Use short-range HF tags in security-critical zones.

**Vulnerability 2 — Cloning & Spoofing (2 marks):**
A cloned tag with a duplicated EPC can be substituted for a legitimate one, fooling inventory systems.

**Mitigation:** Implement **mutual authentication** using cryptographic challenges (e.g., the Gen-2 `AUTHENTICATE` command). Combine RFID with **PUF (Physically Unclonable Function)** silicon for hardware-rooted trust.

**Vulnerability 3 — Denial of Service (1 mark):**
An attacker can jam the UHF band (860–960 MHz) or flood the reader with spurious replies.

**Mitigation:** Regulatory enforcement (jamming is illegal under Indian Telegraph Act), frequency hopping, and shielded enclosures.

**Vulnerability 4 — Data Tampering & Replay (2 marks):**
An adversary captures a legitimate tag reply and replays it later.

**Mitigation:** Use rolling codes, timestamping, and challenge-response protocols. Maintain **back-end audit logs** with cryptographic signatures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Friis equation.** Always write $r$ in metres (or km if using the dB form with constant 32.44).
> 2. **Confusing EIRP and $P_t G_t$.** EIRP *already* includes $P_t \times G_t$. Don't multiply by $G_t$ again.
> 3. **Mixing up near/far field boundary.** It is $\frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$, **not** $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ (that's the Rayleigh distance for antennas, different context).
> 4. **Writing chip sensitivity as a positive number.** It is **always** in dBm (e.g., –15 dBm), which is a *negative* decibel value. Convert carefully: $P_{\text{W}} = 10^{P_{\text{dBm}}/10} \times 10^{-3}$.
> 5. **In anti-collision, confusing Pure ALOHA (18.4%) with Slotted ALOHA (36.8%).** Both are favourite 3-mark MCQ traps.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows or labels.** KTU examiners award **0.5 marks** for a well-labelled diagram even if the surrounding text is incomplete.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **RFID = Radio Frequency Identification** — wireless, non-contact, automatic ID technology.
- ☐ **Three components:** Tag (transponder) + Reader (interrogator) + Middleware.
- ☐ **Four standard bands:** LF (125 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz), Microwave (2.45/5.8 GHz).
- ☐ **Passive vs Active vs Semi-Passive:** battery presence and read range are the key differentiators.
- ☐ **Near-field boundary:** $r_b = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}$ — inductive coupling operates inside this radius.
- ☐ **Far-field mechanism:** backscatter — tag toggles its radar cross-section to encode data.
- ☐ **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — memorize and re-derive in any numerical.
- ☐ **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2$ — the single most-tested formula.
- ☐ **FSPL in dB form:** $20\log_{10}(r) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (r in km, f in MHz).
- ☐ **Read range equation:** $r_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \tau}{P_{\text{th}}}}$.
- ☐ **Anti-collision:** Slotted ALOHA → 36.8%; Pure ALOHA → 18.4%; Gen-2 uses **dynamic Q-algorithm**.
- ☐ **EPC Class-1 Gen-2** = ISO 18000-63 — global UHF standard.
- ☐ **Security risks:** eavesdropping, cloning, replay, DoS — mitigated by AES-128, PUF, challenge-response.
- ☐ **Industries deploying RFID:** automotive, pharma cold-chain, retail (Amazon Go), toll (FASTag), aerospace MRO.

> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** In the 14-mark Part-B questions, always (1) start with a labelled block diagram, (2) state every assumption before substituting into an equation, (3) carry units to the final answer, and (4) quote the **relevant ISO/EPC standard number** wherever possible — examiners reward it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transponders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transponders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transponder (Transmitter-Responder):** A transceiver device that automatically receives, amplifies, frequency-translates, and re-transmits a signal on a different frequency band upon receiving a specific interrogation or input signal. In industrial networking and telecommunications, transponders are the foundational relay elements in **satellite communication systems**, **fiber-optic transport networks**, **RFID identification systems**, and **aviation surveillance networks (ADS-B)**.

The term is a portmanteau derived from **TRANS**mitter + res**PONDER**. A transponder is a "smart relay" that does not merely amplify blindly — it selectively responds to incoming signals, often shifting them to a different carrier frequency to prevent input/output interference and to enable bidirectional full-duplex communication over a single physical medium (e.g., a satellite in orbit, a single optical fiber pair, or a wireless RF channel).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In *Industrial Networking*, transponders are studied in the context of:
> 1. **Satellite Transponders** (Telecommunication/Industrial broadcast)
> 2. **Optical Transponders** (DWDM/CWDM fiber backbones used in SCADA & Industrial Ethernet)
> 3. **RFID Transponders (Tags)** (Industrial asset tracking, IoT, Industry 4.0)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Echoing Mountain Relay Post**
> Imagine you are standing on one side of a deep valley and want to shout a message to a friend on the other side, but the wind blows your voice back into your own face. You cannot use the same frequency in both directions. So, you station a **sentinel on the mountain top** with two hearing cups tuned to different pitches.
> - The sentinel **listens on Frequency A** (the "uplink").
> - He **shifts** the message to **Frequency B** (the "downlink") in his head.
> - He then **re-shouts** the same message down the mountain on Frequency B.
> - Your friend hears Frequency B clearly, and replies using the same protocol.
>
> That sentinel is a **transponder** — a frequency-shifting, power-boosting relay that lives in the "middle" of a communication path (in space, in a fiber node, or on a tagged object).

**Real-world industrial example:** The **Inmarsat satellite** floating 36,000 km above the Indian Ocean receives an engineer's distress call from a ship on **6 GHz uplink**, amplifies it inside its on-board transponder, and rebroadcasts it to a coastal Earth station on **4 GHz downlink**. Without the transponder, a 36,000 km path loss of nearly **200 dB** would render the signal undetectable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Free-space path loss at GEO | ≈ **196–200 dB** (at 4/6 GHz) |
| Typical satellite TWTA power | **20 W – 250 W** |
| Standard satellite bands | **L, S, C, X, Ku, Ka** |
| Optical transponder reach | **80 km – 2000+ km** (with amplifiers) |
| RFID transponder reply frequencies | **125 kHz, 13.56 MHz, 860–960 MHz, 2.45 GHz** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Transponders (Operational Architecture)

Transponders are classified by **how they process the signal** between reception and re-transmission:

#### A. Bent-Pipe (Transparent / Non-Regenerative) Transponder
- The simplest, most widely used satellite transponder.
- Performs **only three operations**: Low-Noise Amplification → Frequency Down-conversion → High-Power Amplification.
- The signal is **never demodulated** to baseband; modulation format is preserved ("transparent").
- Used in **broadcast satellites (DTH TV)** and traditional **VSAT industrial networks**.

#### B. Regenerative (On-Board Processing — OBP) Transponder
- **Demodulates** the uplink signal to baseband, **re-constructs** the bit stream (error detection/correction), and then **re-modulates** it onto the downlink carrier.
- Reduces accumulated uplink noise; allows **on-board switching/routing** (e.g., between spot beams).
- Used in modern **high-throughput satellites (HTS)** and **inter-satellite links (ISL)**.

#### C. Store-and-Forward Transponder
- Stores the entire message (e.g., a packet or file) in on-board memory before re-transmission.
- Used in **LEO constellations** (Iridium, Orbcomm) and **RFID tags**.

#### D. Optical (Wavelength-Shifting) Transponder
- Receives an optical signal on wavelength $\lambda_1$, converts it to the electrical domain via a photodiode, electrically retimes/reshapes (3R regeneration), and re-transmits on wavelength $\lambda_2$ using a laser.
- Indispensable in **DWDM long-haul fiber backbones** for industrial plants and telecom transport.

### 2.2 Satellite Transponder — Functional Block Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A typical C-band/Ku-band bent-pipe transponder chain consists of the following cascaded stages:**

1. **Receive Antenna (Spot/Global Beam)** — Collects uplink RF energy with gain $G_r$ (dBi).
2. **Input Multiplexer (IMUX)** — A bank of bandpass cavity filters that splits the wide receive band (e.g., 5.925–6.425 GHz = **500 MHz**) into **12–24 narrower transponder channels**, each typically **36 MHz or 54 MHz** wide.
3. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** — The first active stage, often a **High-Electron-Mobility Transistor (HEMT)** cooled to 15–20 K. Noise temperature $T_{LNA} \approx 30$–$75$ K. This stage **dominates the system noise figure**.
4. **Down-Converter (Mixer + Local Oscillator)** — Translates the RF uplink to a lower Intermediate Frequency (IF), then up-converts to the downlink RF. Example: 6 GHz → 4 GHz (C-band). The shift isolates the high-gain downlink from the sensitive uplink to prevent **self-oscillation (feedback)**.
5. **Channel Amplifier & Gain Control (A GC)** — Sets the operating point of the transponder.
6. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA)** — Either a **Travelling-Wave Tube Amplifier (TWTA)** for high-power/broadband or a **Solid-State Power Amplifier (SSPA)** for reliability/linearity. Output power typically **20–250 W** per channel.
7. **Output Multiplexer (OMUX)** — Combines the amplified channels into the downlink feed and rejects intermodulation products.
8. **Transmit Antenna (Spot/Global Beam)** — Radiates downlink with gain $G_t$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield — memorize the symbol, the meaning, and the unit.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ = frequency (Hz) | Converts RF band → wavelength for antenna sizing |
| 2 | $L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ dB | $d$ = slant range (m) | Free-space path loss (Friis) — uplink/downlink |
| 3 | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + \dfrac{T_{mixer}}{G_{LNA}} + \dots$ | $T$ in Kelvin | System noise temperature — sets uplink CNR |
| 4 | $\dfrac{G}{T} = G_{rx} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$ dB/K | $G_{rx}$ in dBi, $T$ in K | Figure of merit of an Earth station |
| 5 | $EIRP = P_{tx} + G_{tx} - L_{feed}$ dBW | $P$ in dBW, $G$ in dBi | Effective isotropic radiated power of satellite |
| 6 | $\left[\dfrac{C}{N_0}\right]_{up} = EIRP_{es} - L_{fs,up} + \left(\dfrac{G}{T}\right)_{sat} - k$ | $k = -228.6$ dBW/K·Hz | Uplink carrier-to-noise spectral density |
| 7 | $\left[\dfrac{C}{N}\right]_{total}^{-1} = \left[\dfrac{C}{N}\right]_{up}^{-1} + \left[\dfrac{C}{N}\right]_{down}^{-1} + \left[\dfrac{C}{N}\right]_{IM}^{-1} + \left[\dfrac{C}{N}\right]_{interf}^{-1}$ | All ratios in linear | Total link CNR (adds in **inverse**) |
| 8 | $C = N + \text{SNR}$ (dB) | Carrier power, Noise power | Bit-error-rate relation via $E_b/N_0$ |
| 9 | $\text{SFD} = \text{Saturation Flux Density}$ | dBW/m² | Input flux that drives HPA to saturation |
| 10 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Hz | Frequency conversion relation (mixer) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When combining $C/N$ ratios from uplink, downlink, and intermodulation, students must convert to **linear (power) ratios first**, sum the reciprocals, and then convert back to dB. Direct dB addition is a **fatal** error that costs 2–3 marks per question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial SCADA Backhaul:** Remote oil rigs/offshore platforms backhaul telemetry via **VSAT transponders** on C-band or Ku-band satellites.
- **Optical DWDM Backbone:** Industrial Ethernet/PROFINET traffic between two plants 800 km apart is carried on **100+ wavelengths**, each regenerated by an **optical transponder** in a ROADM node.
- **Aviation & Maritime Safety:** **ADS-B and AIS transponders** automatically broadcast GPS position of aircraft/ships for collision avoidance.
- **Industry 4.0 Asset Tracking:** **Passive RFID transponders** (tags) glued on machinery, pallets, and tools enable real-time inventory and **predictive-maintenance** analytics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation in Path-Loss Form)

The Friis transmission equation in its basic form states that the received power $P_r$ from an isotropic transmit antenna is:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

The factor $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ is the **free-space path loss** as a power ratio. Converting to decibels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs\,(dB)} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(4\pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}(\lambda) \\
&= 32.44 \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) \quad \text{(dB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- The first term $32.44$ is a unit-conversion constant ($20 \log_{10} 4\pi c$ with $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km).
- The second term increases loss with **frequency** (higher $f$ → smaller $\lambda$ → more loss).
- The third term increases loss with **range** at 20 dB per decade.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — GEO Satellite Uplink $C/N_0$ Derivation

**Problem:** A 6 GHz uplink from an Earth station to a geostationary satellite has the following parameters. Compute the uplink $C/N_0$ in dB·Hz.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Earth-station EIRP | $EIRP_{es} = 60$ dBW |
| Slant range | $d = 36{,}000$ km |
| Satellite $G/T$ | $(G/T)_{sat} = -3$ dB/K |
| Boltzmann constant | $k = -228.6$ dBW/K·Hz |
| Frequency | $f = 6$ GHz (uplink) |

**Step 1 — Compute the free-space path loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(6000) + 20\log_{10}(36000) \\
&= 32.44 + 20(3.778) + 20(4.556) \\
&= 32.44 + 75.56 + 91.13 \\
&= 199.13 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the uplink $C/N_0$ link-budget equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right]_{up} &= EIRP_{es} - L_{fs} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{sat} - k \\
&= 60 - 199.13 + (-3) - (-228.6) \\
&= 60 - 199.13 - 3 + 228.6 \\
&= 86.47 \text{ dB·Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $C/N_0 = 86.47$ dB·Hz means the carrier power is **86.47 dB above the noise in a 1 Hz** bandwidth. For a 36 MHz transponder channel ($10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) \approx 75.56$ dB·Hz), the available $C/N$ is roughly $86.47 - 75.56 \approx 10.9$ dB, which is ample for digital DVB-S2 links.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Transponder Types (High-Yield Exam Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is the single most likely 7-mark answer in KTU Module 4 exams.**

| Feature | Bent-Pipe (Transparent) | Regenerative (OBP) | Optical (Wavelength) | RFID (Passive) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Signal processing** | RF → RF (analog) | RF → Baseband → RF | O → E → O (3R) | EM → Bit → Backscatter |
| **Demodulation?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (O/E) | ✅ Yes (backscatter) |
| **Frequency shift?** | ✅ Yes (essential) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Wavelength change | N/A (replies on same band) |
| **On-board routing?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (spot beam hopping) | ✅ Yes (ROADM) | N/A |
| **Latency (GEO)** | **0.25 s** (one-way) | 0.25 s (lighter FEC) | ≈ 0 (terrestrial) | ≈ ms |
| **Uplink noise** | Passed through | Removed by re-modulation | Removed by 3R | N/A (passive) |
| **Complexity / Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Very low |
| **Typical use** | DTH TV, VSAT | HTS, ISL | DWDM backbone | Asset tracking, IoT |
| **KTU mark weight** | **High** | **High** | **Medium** | **Low–Medium** |

### 3.4 Derivation — Total $C/N$ of a Cascaded Link (Uplink + Downlink + IM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot topic in KTU 2024 — appears in nearly every previous-year paper.**

Let the three contributions (uplink $u$, downlink $d$, intermodulation $i$) be available as linear power ratios. Because **noise powers add**, the inverse ratios add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total}^{-1} &= \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{u}^{-1} + \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{d}^{-1} + \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{i}^{-1} \\
\Rightarrow \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total} &= \Bigg[\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{u}^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{d}^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{i}^{-1}\Bigg]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked mini-example:** $(C/N)_u = 100$ (20 dB), $(C/N)_d = 100$ (20 dB), $(C/N)_i = 400$ (26 dB).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total} &= \left[\frac{1}{100} + \frac{1}{100} + \frac{1}{400}\right]^{-1} \\
&= \left[0.01 + 0.01 + 0.0025\right]^{-1} \\
&= \left[0.0225\right]^{-1} \\
&\approx 44.4 \;\;(\text{linear}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 16.47 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The downlink alone would have given 20 dB. The **uplink + intermodulation together** degrade the total link by **3.53 dB** — a non-trivial loss for a system designer to budget.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Satellite Bent-Pipe Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UPLINK[("Uplink RF Signal\n6 GHz / 14 GHz")]
    ANT1["Receive Antenna\nwith Gain Gr"]
    IMUX[/"Input MUX\nBandpass Filters"/]
    LNA["Low-Noise Amplifier\nHEMT, T ~ 50 K"]
    DC["Down-Converter\nMixer + Local Oscillator"]
    GC["Gain Control\nAGC Amplifier"]
    HPA{{"High-Power Amplifier\nTWTA or SSPA"}}
    OMUX[/"Output MUX\nCombines Channels"/]
    ANT2["Transmit Antenna\nwith Gain Gt"]
    DOWNLINK[("Downlink RF Signal\n4 GHz / 12 GHz")]

    UPLINK --> ANT1 --> IMUX --> LNA --> DC --> GC --> HPA --> OMUX --> ANT2 --> DOWNLINK
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Optical Transponder Functional Block (3R Regeneration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[("Optical In\nlambda_1 = 1550.92 nm")]
    PD["Photodiode\nO to E Conversion"]
    R1["Reshaper\nEqualizer"]
    R2["Retimer\nClock Recovery"]
    R3["Regenerator\nDecision Circuit"]
    LD["Laser Driver\nDFB or EML"]
    OUT[("Optical Out\nlambda_2 = 1550.12 nm")]

    IN --> PD --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> LD --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Classification Tree of Transponders (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T["Transponders"]
    T --> S["Satellite"]
    T --> O["Optical Fiber"]
    T --> R["RFID"]
    T --> A["Aviation / Maritime\nADS-B, AIS"]

    S --> S1["Bent-Pipe\nTransparent"]
    S --> S2["Regenerative\nOn-Board Processing"]
    S --> S3["Store and Forward\nLEO Constellations"]

    O --> O1["OEO Transponder\n3R Regeneration"]
    O --> O2["Wavelength Converter\nDWDM ROADM"]

    R --> R1["Passive Tag\nBackscatter"]
    R --> R2["Active Tag\nBattery-Assisted"]

    A --> A1["Mode S Transponder\nSSR for ATC"]
    A --> A2["AIS Transponder\nShip Identification"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — End-to-End Industrial SCADA via Satellite Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SCADA RTU at\nRemote Oil Rig"]
    SAT[("GEO Satellite\nTransponder")]
    ES["Earth Station\nHub at Plant"]
    S2["Central Control\nDCS / SCADA Server"]

    S1 -- "Uplink\n6 GHz, EIRP 50 dBW" --> SAT
    SAT -- "Downlink\n4 GHz, EIRP 52 dBW" --> ES
    ES -- "Fiber / LAN" --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** Always label **frequencies**, **gains**, and **directional arrows**. A diagram without frequency labels typically loses 1–2 marks even if the topology is correct.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a transponder. With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a satellite transponder.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **transponder** is a combination of a transmitter and a responder, used primarily in satellite, optical, and RFID communication systems. In a satellite, a transponder receives the uplink RF signal, amplifies it using a low-noise amplifier (LNA), down-converts it to a lower frequency, further amplifies it using a high-power amplifier (HPA), and re-transmits it on the downlink. The frequency translation (e.g., 6 GHz → 4 GHz) prevents feedback oscillation between the high-gain uplink and downlink paths. Typical satellite transponders have a bandwidth of **36 MHz** and use either TWTAs or SSPAs as the final stage amplifier.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a bent-pipe transponder and a regenerative transponder.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bent-Pipe | Regenerative |
|---|---|---|
| Processing | RF in → RF out | RF → Baseband → RF |
| Demodulation | Not performed | Performed on board |
| Noise | Uplink noise added to downlink | Uplink noise removed |
| Complexity | Low | High (needs on-board DSP) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Application | DTH, conventional VSAT | HTS, inter-satellite links |

> **Examiner's Tip:** Mentioning **on-board demodulation/remodulation** is the single keyword that secures full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **satellite transponder**. Identify each block and state its function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An Earth station operating at **6 GHz** transmits to a GEO satellite at a slant range of **36,000 km**. The Earth station EIRP is **60 dBW**, satellite $G/T$ is **−3 dB/K**. Compute the uplink $C/N_0$. Take $k = -228.6$ dBW/K·Hz. **(7 Marks)**

**OR**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of transponders** used in satellite communication. Compare their features in a table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A satellite link has $(C/N)_{up} = 20$ dB, $(C/N)_{down} = 22$ dB, and $(C/N)_{IM} = 26$ dB. Compute the **total $C/N$** of the link in dB. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### Solution to Q3-A (a) — Block Diagram of Satellite Transponder

> [!NOTE]
> The transponder consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Receiving Antenna** — Collects the uplink signal at e.g., 6 GHz with gain $G_r$.
2. **Input Multiplexer (IMUX)** — Splits the 500 MHz receive band into 12–24 channels of 36 MHz each.
3. **LNA** — Provides low-noise amplification; noise temperature ≈ 50 K.
4. **Frequency Down-Converter** — Mixes the RF with a local oscillator to produce a 4 GHz downlink IF/RF.
5. **Channel Amplifier / AGC** — Sets the input back-off of the HPA.
6. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA)** — TWTA or SSPA delivering 20–250 W per channel.
7. **Output Multiplexer (OMUX)** — Combines all channels for downlink transmission.
8. **Transmitting Antenna** — Radiates the downlink at e.g., 4 GHz with gain $G_t$.

> **Key phrase required:** "**Frequency translation prevents feedback oscillation**" — write this verbatim for full marks.

---

#### Solution to Q3-A (b) — Uplink $C/N_0$

**Step 1 — Free-space path loss at 6 GHz over 36,000 km:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(6000) + 20\log_{10}(36000) \\
&= 32.44 + 75.56 + 91.13 \\
&= 199.13 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Uplink $C/N_0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N_0}\right]_{up} &= EIRP_{es} - L_{fs} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{sat} - k \\
&= 60 - 199.13 - 3 - (-228.6) \\
&= 60 - 199.13 - 3 + 228.6 \\
&= 86.47 \text{ dB·Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\left(C/N_0\right)_{up} = 86.47 \text{ dB·Hz}}$

---

#### Solution to Q3-B (a) — Types of Transponders

Refer to the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.3 of these notes. Cover:

1. **Bent-pipe (transparent)** — RF → RF, simplest, used in DTH/VSAT.
2. **Regenerative (OBP)** — Demodulates and re-modulates, removes uplink noise, supports on-board routing.
3. **Store-and-forward** — Stores packets in memory, used in LEO constellations (Iridium).
4. **Optical transponder** — OEO conversion, 3R regeneration (Reshape, Retime, Regenerate), used in DWDM.
5. **RFID transponder** — Passive/active tag, used in industrial IoT.

State the comparison table for full 7 marks.

---

#### Solution to Q3-B (b) — Total $C/N$ Calculation

**Step 1 — Convert each $C/N$ to linear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{up} &= 10^{20/10} = 100 \\
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{down} &= 10^{22/10} \approx 158.49 \\
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{IM} &= 10^{26/10} \approx 398.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine reciprocals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total}^{-1} &= \frac{1}{100} + \frac{1}{158.49} + \frac{1}{398.11} \\
&= 0.01 + 0.00631 + 0.00251 \\
&= 0.01882
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert and convert to dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total} &= \frac{1}{0.01882} \approx 53.14 \text{ (linear)} \\
\Rightarrow \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{total\,(dB)} &= 10\log_{10}(53.14) \approx 17.25 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{(C/N)_{total} \approx 17.25 \text{ dB}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Transponder Questions**
> 1. **Direct dB addition of $C/N$ values** — WRONG. Always convert to linear, sum the reciprocals, then convert back. This single mistake loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boltzmann constant** ($-228.6$ dBW/K·Hz) when computing $C/N_0$. A unit mismatch costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the 20 vs. $10 \log_{10}$ factor** in Friis equation. Path loss is a *power* ratio, so use **$20 \log_{10}$**.
> 4. **Confusing $G/T$ with $G$ alone.** $G/T$ is in dB/K; you cannot add $G$ (dBi) and $T$ (K) directly.
> 5. **In block diagrams, forgetting to label frequencies** (uplink/downlink bands) — examiner deducts 1 mark per unlabeled arrow.
> 6. **Writing "transponder = repeater"** — a transponder *frequency-translates*; a repeater does not. This subtle distinction is a 2-mark differentiator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Definition:** Transponder = Transmitter + Responder. Performs receive → amplify → frequency-translate → re-transmit.
- **Two big families:** **Satellite transponders** (RF domain) and **Optical transponders** (OEO, 3R).
- **Bent-pipe** = transparent; **Regenerative** = demod/remod; **Store-and-forward** = LEO packet.
- **Frequency translation** is *essential* to prevent self-oscillation in a single-antenna satellite.
- **TWTA** = high power, nonlinear; **SSPA** = lower power, more linear.
- **LNA** determines the **system noise temperature** $T_{sys}$.
- **Friis path loss** in dB: $L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$.
- **EIRP** (dBW) = $P_{tx} + G_{tx} - L_{feed}$.
- **$G/T$** (dB/K) = $G_{rx} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$.
- **$C/N_0$ link equation:** $C/N_0 = EIRP - L_{fs} + G/T - k$ (all in dB).
- **Cascaded $C/N$:** sum the *inverses* in linear scale, then convert back to dB.
- **RFID transponders** in Industry 4.0: passive (backscatter), semi-passive, active.
- **Optical transponder** in DWDM: receives on $\lambda_1$, performs 3R, transmits on $\lambda_2$.
- **ADS-B / Mode-S / AIS** are aviation/maritime transponders — read the question carefully to identify the domain.
- **Memory keyword map for 3-mark questions:** *Transponder, Frequency translation, LNA, HPA, Bent-pipe, Regenerative*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrogators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT741 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrogators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition — Interrogator**
> An **Interrogator** (also called an **RFID Reader** or **Reader/Writer**) is the central active device in a Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) system that emits electromagnetic waves through one or more antennas to **power**, **identify**, **read**, and optionally **write** data to nearby passive/semi-passive transponders (tags). It decodes the backscattered signal from the tag and forwards the decoded information to a host controller (PLC, SCADA, MES, or ERP server) using industrial fieldbus or Ethernet protocols.

In industrial networking terminology (PERUT741), an interrogator is the **master node** that initiates and controls all over-the-air communication with field-level wireless identifiers, functioning as the bridge between the deterministic wired industrial network and the unpredictable wireless RF domain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **classroom teacher calling roll** at the start of a lecture:

- The **teacher = Interrogator**, who sends out a question: *"Who is here?"*
- The **students = RFID tags**, each carrying a unique ID card.
- The teacher broadcasts a radio signal (his voice). Passive tags harvest energy from this signal (just like students staying quiet and listening).
- Only when the teacher "queries," the tag responds by **backscattering** its ID (the student raises his hand and states his name).
- The teacher **decodes** the response, checks against his register (database), and may take further action (mark attendance → write event to a controller).

> [!NOTE] **Why this analogy works**
> - The teacher never directly "sees" the tag — he must *interrogate* via wireless medium.
> - Multiple students may respond simultaneously → the teacher must use an **anti-collision protocol** (asks them to speak one at a time, like "all those whose roll number starts with 1…").
> - The teacher can also give an **instruction** (write operation), like "Open your textbook to page 42."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are fundamental to interrogator engineering and **must be memorized**:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 = 377 \, \Omega$
- **Standard RFID frequency bands:** **LF = 125–134 kHz**, **HF = 13.56 MHz**, **UHF = 860–960 MHz**, **Microwave = 2.45 GHz & 5.8 GHz**
- **Typical UHF read range:** **1 m to 12 m** depending on tag and environment
- **Maximum Slotted ALOHA theoretical throughput:** **1/e ≈ 36.8%**

> [!TIP] **KTU Exam Tip:** Any time the question says *"define interrogator"* or *"state the role of an interrogator in an RFID system"*, the keywords examiners look for are: **"active device," "powers the tag," "decodes backscatter," "communicates with host," "anti-collision manager."** Miss any one and you lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of an Interrogator

An industrial-grade RFID interrogator consists of **five functional sub-blocks**:

1. **RF Front-End (Transmitter + Receiver)**
   - Synthesizes the carrier frequency (PLL-based).
   - Modulates the carrier for forward link (reader → tag): typically **ASK** or **PSK** (EPC Gen 2 uses DSB-ASK or PR-ASK).
   - Demodulates the backscattered signal (reverse link).

2. **Antenna Subsystem**
   - One or more antennas (mono-static, bi-static, or multi-static configuration).
   - Polarization: linear (horizontal/vertical) or circular (RHCP/LHCP).
   - Gain typically **6 dBi to 12 dBi** for UHF.

3. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP) / Control Unit**
   - Implements **anti-collision algorithms** (Q-algorithm in EPC Gen 2).
   - Performs error detection (CRC-16 or CRC-32).
   - Handles command-response state machine.

4. **Communication / Network Interface**
   - Wired: **RS-232, RS-485, USB, Ethernet, Profibus, Profinet, Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA**.
   - Wireless backhaul: Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, 5G (in IIoT 4.0 contexts).

5. **Power Supply**
   - PoE (Power over Ethernet) for fixed readers.
   - Battery for handheld readers (typically **7.4 V Li-ion**).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Forward and Reverse Link

The interrogator–tag interaction is **half-duplex** and consists of two distinct links:

- **Forward Link (Reader → Tag):** Continuous Wave (CW) signal powers the tag's chip and carries reader commands via amplitude modulation.
- **Reverse Link (Tag → Reader):** Tag modulates its antenna's **radar cross-section (RCS)** by switching its input impedance between two states (matched = absorbs, mismatched = reflects). This is called **backscatter modulation**.

> [!NOTE] **The "Why" of Backscatter**
> Passive tags have **no transmitter**. They cannot generate their own RF signal. They survive by *re-radiating* a portion of the reader's transmitted CW. By toggling between absorbing and reflecting states, they embed data onto the reflected wave — the reader decodes this as 1s and 0s.

## 2.3 Anti-Collision Protocols — How Multiple Tags Are Handled

When multiple tags enter the interrogation zone simultaneously, their responses collide. The interrogator uses one of these protocols:

### (a) ALOHA-Based (Probabilistic)
- **Pure ALOHA:** Tags transmit anytime → throughput $\approx 18.4\%$.
- **Slotted ALOHA:** Time is slotted → throughput $\approx 36.8\%$.
- **Framed Slotted ALOHA (FSA):** $N$ slots per frame → used in EPC Gen 2 with variable frame size $2^Q$.

### (b) Tree-Based (Deterministic)
- **Binary Tree:** Reader splits tag population into two recursively until only one tag replies per slot.
- **Query Tree:** Reader broadcasts a prefix; only matching tags respond.

> [!IMPORTANT] **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 uses a slotted random anti-collision algorithm called the Q-algorithm**, where the reader dynamically adjusts the parameter $Q$ (0 to 15) based on observed collision count. This is the de-facto industrial standard interrogator protocol.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] The table below is your **cheat sheet** for every numerical question KTU has asked (or could ask) on interrogators. Memorize the column headers and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Mapping | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis free-space path loss | $PL_{FS} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 2 | Received power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | W | Far-field: $d > \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ |
| 3 | Maximum read range | $r_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \sigma}{P_{th}}}$ | m | $\sigma$ = tag RCS in m² |
| 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | m | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 5 | Slotted ALOHA throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | dimensionless | Max at $G=1 \Rightarrow S_{max}=36.8\%$ |
| 6 | Pure ALOHA throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | dimensionless | Max at $G=0.5 \Rightarrow S_{max}=18.4\%$ |
| 7 | FSA frame size | $L = 2^Q$ | slots | $Q \in [0,15]$ for EPC Gen 2 |
| 8 | Effective isotropic radiated power | $EIRP = P_t \cdot G_t$ | W or dBW | Regulatory cap (e.g., **4 W EIRP** in EU UHF) |
| 9 | Link budget | $LB = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{th} - PL_{FS}$ | dB | Must be $\geq 0$ for reliable read |
| 10 | Backscatter power ratio | $\Delta \sigma = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | m² | Determines modulation depth |

> **Critical Exam Note:** For any read-range derivation, the examiner expects you to **define every variable explicitly** before plugging numbers. Skipping this loses at least 1–2 marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Interrogator Engineering

| Industry Domain | Interrogator Application | Frequency Used |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive Manufacturing | Body shop tracking, tire tracking | UHF |
| Pharmaceutical & Healthcare | Anti-counterfeit drug serialization | UHF / HF |
| Logistics & Supply Chain | Pallet/case tracking in warehouses | UHF |
| Livestock & Agriculture | Animal identification | LF (134.2 kHz) |
| Toll Collection (FASTag, EZ-Pass) | Vehicle identification at highway speed | UHF / Microwave |
| Access Control | Employee ID cards, contactless payments | HF (13.56 MHz) |
| Aviation (IATA 1740) | Baggage handling (RFID mandatory since 2026) | UHF |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Transmission Equation (Received Power at Tag)

The Friis equation gives the power delivered to a passive tag from an interrogator over free space.

**Starting Point — Power density at distance $d$:**

For an isotropic radiator, power spreads uniformly over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. With antenna gain $G_t$, the power density (Poynting vector magnitude) is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

This equation states that the transmitted power $P_t$ multiplied by the transmit antenna gain $G_t$ is distributed uniformly over the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ centered on the antenna.

**Step 1 — Effective aperture of the receiving tag antenna:**

Every antenna intercepts power proportional to its effective area:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

This is a fundamental antenna theorem relating the gain of a receiving antenna to its physical effective aperture; the larger the wavelength squared, the more energy the tag can harvest.

**Step 2 — Power delivered to the tag load:**

Multiply the incident power density by the effective aperture:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

This combines the previous two results: the power density arriving at the tag multiplied by the tag's effective aperture gives the power actually absorbed by the tag's internal circuitry.

**Step 3 — Final Friis Equation:**

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

This is the canonical Friis free-space transmission formula. The term in parentheses represents the path loss factor; notice that received power falls off as $1/d^2$ (or equivalently $-20\log_{10}(d)$ in dB).

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** To *increase* read range, an engineer has only four knobs: **increase $P_t$** (limited by regulation), **increase $G_t$ or $G_r$** (bigger antennas), or **increase $\lambda$** (lower frequency, but this implies slower data rates).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Read Range of an Interrogator

The read range $r_{max}$ is defined as the maximum distance at which the power received by the tag equals its minimum threshold $P_{th}$.

**Starting Point — Power received by tag at distance $r$:**

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r}\right)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Set $P_r = P_{th}$ at the limiting case:**

$$
P_{th} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r_{max}}\right)^2
$$

At the boundary of the read zone, the power delivered to the tag exactly equals the minimum power required to power up its chip; any greater distance and the tag cannot backscatter a detectable signal.

**Step 2 — Solve for $r_{max}$:**

Rearrange to isolate $r_{max}$:

$$
r_{max}^2 = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{P_{th} (4\pi)^2}
$$

This step inverts the squared term so we can take the square root in the next step.

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$
r_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{th}}}
$$

This is the **free-space maximum read range** for an *isotropic* tag (no directional RCS enhancement).

**Step 4 — Inclusion of tag radar cross-section (bistatic radar form):**

For real tags, the backscattered signal depends on the tag's RCS $\sigma$. For a monostatic reader (same antenna for TX/RX):

$$
r_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt[4]{\frac{P_t G_t^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 P_{reader,th}}}
$$

Here we use the fourth root because the signal makes a **two-way trip** (forward path + backscatter path), and the $G_t$ appears twice since the same antenna both transmits and receives.

> [!TIP] **Why the fourth root?** Because the backscattered signal experiences path loss *twice* — once from reader to tag, and once from tag back to reader. Doubling the exponent of $d$ in the denominator forces the radical index to become 4.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slotted ALOHA Throughput

Slotted ALOHA is the heart of EPC Gen-2 anti-collision. We must derive its maximum throughput.

**Step 1 — Model the system:**

Time axis is divided into slots of duration $T$. A tag chooses a slot uniformly at random and transmits its ID.

This probabilistic model assumes a Poisson distribution of tag arrivals in each slot, which is the standard assumption for ALOHA-family protocols.

**Step 2 — Define the offered load $G$:**

$$
G = \text{average number of transmission attempts per slot}
$$

$G$ captures how "busy" the channel is on average — a $G$ of 2 means each slot sees on average 2 transmission attempts.

**Step 3 — Probability of successful transmission (no collision):**

A slot is successful if **exactly one** tag transmits in it. From Poisson statistics:

$$
P(\text{success}) = \frac{G^1 e^{-G}}{1!} = G e^{-G}
$$

This is the probability mass function of a Poisson random variable with parameter $G$, evaluated at $k=1$; the $e^{-G}$ term arises from the series expansion of $e^x$.

**Step 4 — Throughput $S$ = offered load × success probability:**

$$
S = G \cdot P(\text{success}) = G^2 e^{-G}
$$

Throughput is defined as the rate of successful transmissions, which is the product of how often tags try to transmit ($G$) and the probability each attempt succeeds.

**Step 5 — Maximize $S$ with respect to $G$:**

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = 2G e^{-G} - G^2 e^{-G} = 0
$$

Factoring out the common exponential term allows us to solve the polynomial equation directly.

$$
G e^{-G} (2 - G) = 0
$$

The non-trivial root is:

$$
G = 2 \quad \text{(offered load)}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute maximum throughput:**

$$
S_{max} = 2^2 e^{-2} = \frac{4}{e^2} \approx 0.541
$$

Wait — this is the **Pure ALOHA** result. For **Slotted ALOHA**, the vulnerability window is halved (collision only possible within one slot, not two consecutive slots), so:

$$
S_{slotted} = G e^{-G}, \quad G = 1
$$

$$
S_{max,slotted} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \approx 36.8\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Doubling the throughput:** Slotted ALOHA achieves exactly **twice** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA by aligning transmissions to slot boundaries. This is why EPC Gen-2 uses slotted, not pure, ALOHA.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Question:** A UHF RFID interrogator transmits $P_t = 1 \text{ W}$ with a 6 dBi linearly polarized antenna at 866 MHz (ETSI band). The tag has $G_r = 1.76 \text{ dBi}$ and minimum threshold power $P_{th} = -14 \text{ dBm}$. Calculate the maximum free-space read range. (Assume monostatic configuration, $\sigma = 0.03 \text{ m}^2$.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert $P_t$ and $P_{th}$ to linear (Watts):**

$$
P_t = 1 \text{ W}, \quad P_{th} = -14 \text{ dBm} = 10^{(-14-30)/10} = 10^{-4.4} \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{866 \times 10^6} \approx 0.3464 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert antenna gains to linear:**

$$
G_t = 10^{6/10} = 3.98, \quad G_r = 10^{1.76/10} \approx 1.5
$$

**Step 4 — Apply bistatic read range formula:**

$$
r_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt[4]{\frac{P_t G_t^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 P_{th}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numerical values (organized in sub-terms):**

$$
\frac{P_t G_t^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 P_{th}} = \frac{(1)(3.98)^2 (0.3464)^2 (0.03)}{(4\pi)^2 (3.98 \times 10^{-5})}
$$

Compute numerator: $(15.84)(0.12)(0.03) = 0.0570$

Compute denominator: $(12.566)^2 \times 3.98 \times 10^{-5} = 158.0 \times 3.98 \times 10^{-5} = 6.29 \times 10^{-3}$

$$
\frac{0.0570}{6.29 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 9.06
$$

**Step 6 — Apply fourth root and leading factor:**

$$
r_{max} = \frac{0.3464}{12.566}\sqrt[4]{9.06} = (0.02756)(1.736) \approx 0.0478 \text{ m} \approx 4.78 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

This is a very short read range, indicating the tag has poor RCS or the regulatory EIRP cap is the bottleneck. Real industrial UHF systems achieve **3–8 m** by using high-gain antennas (8–12 dBi) and high-RCS tags.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of an Industrial RFID Interrogator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Power Supply Unit]
        POE[PoE Injector or 24V DC]
    end

    subgraph CPU[Digital Control and DSP]
        MCU[Microcontroller or FPGA]
        DSP[Baseband DSP]
        MEM[EPC Memory and Lookup Table]
    end

    subgraph RF[RF Front End]
        PLL[PLL Synthesizer]
        TX[Power Amplifier]
        RX[Low Noise Amplifier]
        MIX[IQ Demodulator]
    end

    subgraph ANT[Antenna Subsystem]
        CIR[Circulator or Directional Coupler]
        ANT1[Antenna Port 1]
        ANT2[Antenna Port 2]
        ANT3[Antenna Port N]
    end

    subgraph NET[Industrial Network Interface]
        ETH[Ethernet 100M or 1G]
        PB[Profibus or Profinet]
        MB[Modbus TCP]
    end

    subgraph HOST[Host Controller]
        PLC[PLC or SCADA]
        DB[ERP Database]
    end

    subgraph TAGS[Interrogation Zone]
        TAG1[Passive Tag A]
        TAG2[Passive Tag B]
        TAG3[Passive Tag C]
    end

    POE --> MCU
    MCU --> PLL
    PLL --> TX
    TX --> CIR
    CIR --> ANT1
    CIR --> ANT2
    CIR --> ANT3
    ANT1 -. CW forward link .-> TAG1
    ANT1 -. backscatter .-> CIR
    CIR --> RX
    RX --> MIX
    MIX --> DSP
    DSP --> MCU
    MCU <--> MEM
    MCU --> ETH
    ETH --> PLC
    ETH --> DB
    PLC --> PB
    PLC --> MB
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** Power flows upward from PoE to the CPU. The RF chain (PLL → TX → Circulator → Antenna) transmits the forward link. Backscatter from tags re-enters via the same antenna, passes through the circulator to the LNA, gets demodulated by the IQ mixer, and is decoded by the DSP. The MCU then ships the tag data over Ethernet to PLC/SCADA.

## 4.2 Interrogator–Tag Communication Sequence (EPC Gen 2 Q-Algorithm)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Interrogator
    participant T1 as Tag 1
    participant T2 as Tag 2
    participant T3 as Tag 3

    R->>R: Select appropriate Q value from inventory
    R->>T1: Query (Q=4, slot=5)
    R->>T2: Query (Q=4, slot=5)
    R->>T3: Query (Q=4, slot=5)
    Note over T1,T3: Each tag picks random slot in [0,15]
    T1->>R: RN16 in slot 3
    T2->>R: RN16 in slot 7
    T3->>R: COLLISION in slot 11
    R->>R: Observe collision, decrement Q to 3
    R->>T1: ACK with handle
    T1->>R: EPC + CRC
    R->>T1: Req_RN (next command)
    R->>T2: New query with smaller frame
    T2->>R: Succeeds in isolated slot
```

> [!TIP] **How to draw this in your exam:** Even if you can't reproduce the Mermaid block on paper, sketch 4 vertical lanes (Reader, Tag 1, Tag 2, Tag 3), draw downward arrows for forward commands and upward arrows for backscatter responses, and explicitly label one collision event. Examiners award 2–3 marks for clear sequence diagrams.

## 4.3 Q-Algorithm Decision Tree (Anti-Collision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Inventory Round Begins]) --> QSET[Reader sets Q from 0 to 15]
    QSET --> FRSIZE[Frame size L equals 2 power Q]
    FRSIZE --> QRY[Reader sends Query command]
    QRY --> COLLCHK{Collision observed?}
    COLLCHK -->|Yes, many| QINC[Q equals Q plus 0.8]
    COLLCHK -->|Few collisions| QDEC[Q equals Q minus 0.4]
    COLLCHK -->|No collision| QKEEP[Q unchanged]
    QINC --> QSET
    QDEC --> QSET
    QKEEP --> QSET
```

> [!NOTE] **Why Q is a float in practice:** Although Q is an integer, EPC Gen-2 lets the reader update it by fractional increments (typically **+0.8** on heavy collision, **−0.4** on idle slot) so the frame size adapts smoothly to the tag population.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define an RFID interrogator. List its four primary functions.**

**Model Answer (expected length: 3–4 lines):**

An RFID interrogator is an active radio-frequency device that uses one or more antennas to emit electromagnetic waves for the purpose of powering, identifying, and communicating with nearby passive or semi-passive transponders (tags).

Its four primary functions are:
1. **Energy transfer** — providing operating power to passive tags via RF energy harvesting.
2. **Command dispatch** — sending read, write, lock, or kill instructions to tags.
3. **Signal decoding** — demodulating the backscattered signal to extract tag data (EPC, TID, user memory).
4. **Host interfacing** — forwarding decoded data over industrial protocols (Profinet, Modbus TCP, OPC UA) to a controller or database.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]
**Distinguish between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA anti-collision protocols. Which one is used in EPC Gen-2 and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Time synchronization | Not required | Required (slot boundaries) |
| Vulnerability window | $2T$ | $T$ |
| Maximum throughput | $18.4\%$ | $36.8\%$ |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Moderate |

EPC Gen-2 uses **Slotted ALOHA** (specifically the Q-algorithm variant) because it doubles the channel utilization, allowing the reader to inventory up to **1000 tags/second** with low collision probability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers → loses 1 mark per question.
> 2. **Mixing up Pure and Slotted ALOHA throughput** ($18.4\%$ vs $36.8\%$) → loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the variable definition step** in derivations → loses 1–2 marks even if the final answer is correct.
